project_goal.value: Ingest sensor telemetry from IoT devices embedded in bridge structural components, normalize strain and vibration readings, and load into a time-series warehouse for long-term fatigue analysis
project_goal.rationale: Core mission: replace manual inspection logs with continuous structural health monitoring

target_users.value: Civil engineers and structural analysts at a regional transport authority
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers of fatigue datasets and alert dashboards

project_class.rationale: Pure ingest-normalize-load pipeline with no public-facing UI layer

capabilities.rationale: Time-series ingestion, Airflow batch aggregation, MQTT integration, and analytical query layer

complexity_level.rationale: High-frequency sensor streams, multi-bridge topology, and long-horizon fatigue modelling

risk_level.rationale: Safety-critical infrastructure; data loss or silent corruption could delay detection of structural failure

data_sensitivity.rationale: Bridge structural vulnerability data is sensitive; disclosure could expose national infrastructure weaknesses

external_exposure.rationale: All access restricted to transport authority internal network via VPN

access_model.value: Custom JWT with role separation: ingest-writer, analyst-reader, ops-admin
access_model.rationale: Minimal privilege per consumer type; ingest path uses dedicated service credentials

feature_scope.value: MQTT ingestion from bridge IoT nodes, signal integrity validation, TimescaleDB hypertable storage, Airflow DAG aggregation, anomaly flagging, fatigue model dataset export, Grafana dashboards
feature_scope.rationale: Full pipeline from sensor edge to engineer workstation

mvp_scope.value: MQTT ingestion for 3 pilot bridges, hypertable write path, basic signal validation, daily Airflow aggregation DAG, Grafana strain and vibration dashboard
mvp_scope.rationale: Sufficient for pilot validation with the regional authority before full network rollout

security_baseline.value: mTLS on MQTT broker, JWT auth on all API consumers, encrypted EBS volumes, VPC with no public egress for data nodes
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential infrastructure data must not traverse public internet unencrypted

frontend_stack.value: None - no browser UI; Grafana 10 for operational dashboards
frontend_stack.rationale: Data pipeline project class; Grafana serves read-only analytical views

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Apache Airflow 2.9 for orchestration; custom Go 1.22 ingest microservice for MQTT-to-Kafka bridge
backend_stack.rationale: Airflow for complex DAG scheduling; Go for high-throughput low-latency ingest path

data_platform.value: TimescaleDB on PostgreSQL 16 for hot time-series data; S3 with Parquet/Iceberg for cold archive beyond 90 days
data_platform.rationale: TimescaleDB hypertables for sub-second sensor queries; S3 Iceberg for cost-effective long-horizon fatigue datasets

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in ap-southeast-2; MQTT broker on EC2 in private subnet
hosting_target.rationale: Fargate for stateless pipeline workers; EC2 for EMQX requiring persistent TCP connections

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw sensor readings retained 90 days in TimescaleDB; archived to S3 Iceberg indefinitely; PII-free dataset
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Long-term fatigue analysis requires multi-decade historical baselines; no personal data collected

future_scope.value: ML-based remaining service life prediction, automated work-order generation on anomaly detection, expansion to 50-bridge network
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap pending pilot success

constraints.value: Must operate within transport authority VPC; sensor edge devices transmit at 10 Hz; 48-hour data loss tolerance SLA for non-critical sensors
constraints.rationale: Network topology and SLA defined by authority operations contract

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for pipeline metrics; structured JSON logs to CloudWatch; PagerDuty alerts for ingest lag >5 min and anomaly score above threshold
observability_baseline.rationale: Authority requires 24/7 monitoring with on-call escalation for structural anomalies

execution_preference.value: Iterative delivery with 3-week sprint cycles; pilot bridge cohort first, full network rollout in Phase 2
execution_preference.rationale: Risk mitigation through staged rollout on pilot bridges before full network

compliance_context.value: ISO 13822 structural assessment guidelines; potential ASCE 7 infrastructure reporting requirements; internal authority data classification policy for confidential infrastructure data
compliance_context.rationale: High risk level and confidential data sensitivity trigger compliance documentation requirements

project.goal: Continuous structural health monitoring ETL pipeline for regional bridge network
project.timeline: Pilot 3 bridges in Q2, full 20-bridge network by Q4

backend.framework: Apache Airflow 2.9 for DAGs; custom Go MQTT-Kafka bridge
backend.api: Internal REST endpoints for query consumers only

security.auth: Custom JWT per service role; mTLS on MQTT broker
security.transport: TLS 1.3 for all data plane; VPN for analyst workstation access

data.db: TimescaleDB on PostgreSQL 16 with compression policies
data.cache: Redis 7 for deduplication window and ingest offset tracking
data.archive: S3 with Apache Iceberg tables for data beyond 90 days

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with pytest and integration test against containerised TimescaleDB
devops.hosting: ECS Fargate for workers; EC2 for EMQX MQTT broker
devops.environments: dev, staging, production in isolated VPCs

open_questions.iec_61850_compatibility: Assess whether IEC 61850 GOOSE messages from newer sensors require additional protocol bridge

reasoner_reviews.product: The pipeline scope is well-bounded for a pilot: three bridges, MQTT ingestion, daily Airflow aggregation, and Grafana visualisation cover the civil engineer's primary workflow. The 48-hour data-loss tolerance SLA is critical to honour -- silent drop of readings during a high-wind or seismic event could mask early fatigue indicators. The fatigue model export pathway should be scoped explicitly for Phase 2 to avoid pilot scope creep.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Separating the ingest path (Go microservice plus EMQX) from the transformation layer (Airflow DAGs) is the right call for this data volume. At 10 Hz per channel across multiple sensors per bridge, the ingest path must handle 5,000-plus events per second at full network scale. TimescaleDB hypertables with chunk intervals of one hour will keep query times under 50 ms for 30-day rolling windows. A dead-letter queue for packets failing integrity validation is mandatory to prevent silent data loss.
reasoner_reviews.security: MQTT broker mTLS is non-negotiable given the infrastructure sensitivity. Each bridge node should have its own client certificate provisioned through ACM Private CA so compromised nodes can be revoked without affecting the fleet. JWT tokens for Airflow task workers and Grafana data source should be short-lived (1-hour expiry) with automatic rotation via Secrets Manager.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The VPC-only constraint prevents using AWS IoT Core -- EMQX on EC2 satisfies both the private network requirement and high-connection density. Budget for EC2 reserved instances for the EMQX nodes to control cost over the multi-year project horizon.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The Iceberg cold archive approach is sound for multi-decade fatigue datasets, but the schema evolution strategy must be addressed in Phase 1 -- retrofitting schema on petabyte-scale tables is painful. Define the sensor metadata schema as a versioned Avro record from day one.

backend.service_design: Two discrete services: (1) Go MQTT-Kafka Bridge -- stateless ECS Fargate tasks subscribing to EMQX broker, validating signal packets, publishing to Kafka topic sensor-raw; (2) Python Airflow Workers -- Fargate task groups executing DAGs for normalization, aggregation, and anomaly scoring. Services share no state; coordination through Kafka offsets and TimescaleDB.
backend.api_patterns: Internal REST API (FastAPI, Python) exposes GET /readings/{bridge_id}?start=&end= for analyst tooling and POST /ingest/validate for manual reprocessing triggers. No public endpoints.
backend.background_jobs: Airflow DAGs: ingest-normalise (triggered on Kafka consumer lag over 100 messages), daily-aggregate (00:30 UTC nightly), anomaly-scan (every 15 min on latest hypertable chunk), fatigue-export (weekly to S3 Parquet).
backend.failure_handling: Dead-letter topic sensor-raw-dlq for packets failing integrity check. Airflow task retries: 3 attempts with exponential backoff. Circuit breaker on TimescaleDB writer: if p99 write latency exceeds 500ms, shed load to Redis buffer queue.

security.auth_design: mTLS on EMQX using ACM Private CA-issued client certs per bridge node. JWT tokens for ingest-writer and analyst-reader service accounts, signed with RS256, 1-hour expiry, rotated via Lambda and Secrets Manager.
security.secrets_management: All credentials in AWS Secrets Manager. ECS task roles use IAM policies scoped to specific secret ARNs. EMQX CA private key stored in CloudHSM.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every structural alert insertion, fatigue model export, and analyst read query logged to audit_events table with actor JWT subject, action, bridge_id, timestamp. CloudTrail for all AWS API actions on infrastructure resources.
security.security_risks: Bridge node certificate compromise allows spoofed sensor data. Mitigation: per-node revocation list synced to EMQX every 5 min. Insider threat from analyst-reader tokens exfiltrating bulk hypertable data -- mitigate with query row-count limits enforced at API layer.

data.entities: bridge_assets (id, bridge_code, name, location_geom, construction_year, design_load_kn, material_type, last_inspection_date). sensor_devices (id, bridge_id FK, sensor_type ENUM strain_gauge/accelerometer/tilt_sensor, channel_id, calibration_factor, installation_date, status). telemetry_readings (TimescaleDB hypertable: sensor_id FK, recorded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, raw_value FLOAT8, normalised_value FLOAT8, unit, quality_flag ENUM ok/suspect/failed). structural_alerts (id, bridge_id FK, alert_type ENUM fatigue_threshold/anomaly_variance/sensor_fault, severity ENUM warning/critical, triggered_at, resolved_at, detail_json). fatigue_models (id, bridge_id FK, model_version, analysis_date, estimated_remaining_life_years, confidence_interval_json, analyst_id). maintenance_events (id, bridge_id FK, event_type, scheduled_date, completed_date, technician_id, findings_text).
data.storage_design: TimescaleDB hypertable on telemetry_readings with 1-hour chunk intervals, 7-day compression policy, and continuous aggregate materialized views for 5-min and 30-min averages. S3 Iceberg table bridge_telemetry_archive partitioned by bridge_code and month for cold data.
data.retention_and_deletion: Raw hypertable chunks compressed after 7 days, moved to S3 Iceberg after 90 days via Airflow lifecycle DAG. Iceberg data retained indefinitely per authority policy. Structural alerts and fatigue models are append-only -- no deletion permitted.
data.data_risks: TimescaleDB chunk bloat if compression job falls behind during high-ingestion events such as seismic episodes. Monitor chunk_compression_stats and alert if uncompressed chunks exceed 48 hours of age.

devops.deployment_topology: VPC with three subnets: public (NLB for EMQX, ALB for Grafana/API), private-app (ECS Fargate tasks, EMQX EC2 nodes), private-data (RDS TimescaleDB, ElastiCache Redis, MSK Kafka). No public IPs on any data-tier resource.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff lint plus golangci-lint, pytest unit tests, Go unit tests, docker-compose integration test (TimescaleDB plus Kafka plus Redis), Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy with health check gate. Airflow DAG deploys via S3 sync on CI merge.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping Go ingest service and Airflow StatsD metrics. Grafana dashboards: ingest throughput (events/s per bridge), Kafka consumer lag, TimescaleDB write latency p95/p99, anomaly alert rate per bridge, sensor quality flag distribution.
devops.devops_risks: EMQX EC2 single-node failure drops ingest for all bridges on that AZ. Mitigation: EMQX cluster across two AZs with NLB. Airflow metadata database must be backed up hourly given DAG scheduling dependency.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a safety-critical structural health monitoring pipeline where data integrity and low-latency ingest take priority over cost optimisation. The Go MQTT-Kafka bridge decouples the high-throughput sensor stream from the Python Airflow transformation layer, preventing slow DAG execution from causing ingest backpressure. TimescaleDB hypertables with continuous aggregates serve the civil engineers' rolling-window queries without full table scans. The mTLS-per-node certificate model is critical given the infrastructure sensitivity classification.
target_output.title: Bridge Structural Health Monitoring ETL Pipeline
target_output.executive_summary: A safety-critical IoT data pipeline for a regional transport authority that continuously ingests strain, vibration, and tilt telemetry from bridge-embedded sensor nodes via MQTT, validates signal integrity, stores normalised readings in TimescaleDB hypertables, and runs nightly Airflow DAGs to aggregate 30-day statistical profiles and flag fatigue threshold anomalies. Civil engineers query the resulting datasets through Grafana dashboards and export them to structural fatigue models estimating remaining service life across the bridge network.
target_output.architecture_overview: Event-driven two-stage pipeline hosted entirely within the authority's AWS VPC in ap-southeast-2. Stage 1: EMQX broker cluster on EC2 in a private subnet receives MQTT publishes from bridge IoT nodes over mTLS, forwarding validated packets to MSK Kafka topic sensor-raw. A Go ingest microservice on ECS Fargate consumes sensor-raw, applies calibration factors and quality flagging, and writes normalised records to the TimescaleDB hypertable telemetry_readings. Stage 2: Apache Airflow 2.9 DAGs on Fargate perform 5-minute and 30-minute continuous aggregations, run a sliding-window variance anomaly detector every 15 minutes, and execute nightly 30-day rolling statistical profile builds that feed the fatigue model export. Grafana 10 connects directly to TimescaleDB for real-time structural health dashboards. All inter-service communication stays inside the VPC with no public egress for data-plane traffic.
target_output.technology_stack: Ingest: Go 1.22 MQTT-Kafka bridge service, EMQX 5 MQTT broker on EC2, MSK Kafka 3.6 with 32 partitions on sensor-raw topic. Transformation: Python 3.12, Apache Airflow 2.9 (Celery executor on Fargate), pandas and scipy for signal processing. Storage: TimescaleDB on PostgreSQL 16 (RDS), Redis 7 for deduplication windows, S3 with Apache Iceberg and AWS Glue catalog for cold archive. Visualisation: Grafana 10 with TimescaleDB plugin. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, RDS, MSK, ElastiCache, S3, ACM Private CA, CloudHSM, Secrets Manager, GitHub Actions CI.
target_output.functional_feature_map: MQTT Ingest: Bridge IoT nodes publish strain/accelerometer/tilt payloads at up to 10 Hz per channel; broker validates client certs and forwards to Kafka. Signal Validation: Go service applies per-sensor calibration factors, flags packets outside physically plausible ranges as suspect or failed, routes failed packets to sensor-raw-dlq. TimescaleDB Write Path: Normalised readings batched into 500-record chunks and bulk-inserted into the telemetry_readings hypertable. Continuous Aggregation: Airflow materialises 5-min and 30-min average and peak-strain views per bridge. Anomaly Detection: 15-minute sliding window DAG computes variance deviation from 30-day baseline and writes structural_alerts on threshold breach. Fatigue Export: Weekly Airflow DAG queries hypertable aggregates and exports per-bridge Parquet datasets to S3 for fatigue model ingestion.
target_output.system_components: EMQX Cluster (2 EC2 c6i.xlarge nodes behind NLB, ap-southeast-2a/2b) for persistent MQTT connections from up to 500 bridge sensor nodes. MSK Kafka (3 brokers, sensor-raw topic 32 partitions, sensor-raw-dlq topic 4 partitions). Go Ingest Service (ECS Fargate, auto-scales 2-8 tasks on sensor-raw consumer lag). Airflow Workers (Fargate Celery workers, 2-12 tasks). TimescaleDB (RDS PostgreSQL 16 db.r6g.2xlarge, Multi-AZ). Redis 7 (ElastiCache r6g.large). S3 Iceberg Archive with Glue catalog. Grafana 10 on ECS Fargate behind internal ALB.
target_output.workflows: Sensor Ingest Workflow: Bridge node publishes MQTT message to topic bridges/BRIDGE_ID/sensors/SENSOR_ID/telemetry containing JSON payload with timestamp_iso, channel_id, raw_value, unit. EMQX authenticates client cert against ACM Private CA CRL, forwards to Kafka sensor-raw. Go ingest consumer reads batch of 500, looks up sensor calibration_factor from Redis cache (refreshed hourly from TimescaleDB sensor_devices table), computes normalised_value, evaluates against min/max plausibility bounds, sets quality_flag, and bulk-inserts to TimescaleDB; anomalous packets route to sensor-raw-dlq. Nightly Aggregation and Anomaly Workflow: Airflow daily-aggregate DAG triggers at 00:30 UTC, queries telemetry_readings for prior 24-hour window per bridge, computes rolling 30-day mean and standard deviation per sensor channel using SQL window functions on continuous aggregate views, updates fatigue_models with latest statistical profile, writes structural_alerts for channels where current-day peak strain deviates more than 2.5 sigma from 30-day baseline, and emails digest to structural analysts via SES.
target_output.data_model: bridge_assets (id UUID PK, bridge_code VARCHAR unique, name, location_geom GEOMETRY, construction_year SMALLINT, design_load_kn FLOAT8, material_type ENUM steel/concrete/composite, last_inspection_date DATE). sensor_devices (id UUID PK, bridge_id UUID FK, sensor_type ENUM strain_gauge/accelerometer/tilt_sensor, channel_id VARCHAR, calibration_factor FLOAT8, installation_date DATE, status ENUM active/decommissioned). telemetry_readings (TimescaleDB hypertable: sensor_id UUID FK, recorded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, raw_value FLOAT8, normalised_value FLOAT8, unit VARCHAR, quality_flag ENUM ok/suspect/failed) -- partitioned by recorded_at with 1-hour chunks. structural_alerts (id UUID PK, bridge_id UUID FK, alert_type ENUM fatigue_threshold/anomaly_variance/sensor_fault, severity ENUM warning/critical, triggered_at TIMESTAMPTZ, resolved_at TIMESTAMPTZ, sigma_deviation FLOAT8, detail_json JSONB). fatigue_models (id UUID PK, bridge_id UUID FK, model_version INT, analysis_date DATE, estimated_remaining_life_years FLOAT8, confidence_interval_json JSONB, analyst_id UUID). maintenance_events (id UUID PK, bridge_id UUID FK, event_type VARCHAR, scheduled_date DATE, completed_date DATE, technician_id UUID, findings_text TEXT). inspection_records (id UUID PK, bridge_id UUID FK, inspection_date DATE, inspector_id UUID, condition_rating SMALLINT, defect_codes TEXT[], report_s3_key VARCHAR).
target_output.api_design: Internal REST API (FastAPI, Python 3.12) bound to VPC private ALB. GET /v1/bridges returns bridge registry. GET /v1/bridges/BRIDGE_ID/readings?start=ISO&end=ISO&sensor_type=strain_gauge returns paginated normalised readings from TimescaleDB. GET /v1/bridges/BRIDGE_ID/alerts?severity=critical returns open structural_alerts. GET /v1/bridges/BRIDGE_ID/fatigue returns latest fatigue_model record. POST /v1/ingest/reprocess triggers reprocessing of dead-letter queue for a bridge. GET /v1/export/fatigue-dataset?bridge_id=X&period=30d streams S3 Parquet presigned URL for model ingestion. All endpoints require JWT Bearer token; analyst-reader tokens restricted to GET; ops-admin tokens permit POST.
target_output.security_and_compliance: mTLS on EMQX using ACM Private CA; each bridge node has unique client certificate; CRL synced to EMQX every 5 minutes. JWT RS256 tokens with 1-hour expiry for ingest-writer, analyst-reader, and ops-admin roles, rotated automatically by Lambda and Secrets Manager. TimescaleDB and Redis in private subnets with no public route; RDS encryption at rest via KMS CMK. ECS task roles scoped to minimum IAM permissions per service. Audit logging: every structural_alert insert, fatigue_model write, and analyst API query logged to audit_events table with append-only constraint. ISO 13822 compliance: all fatigue model versions and input datasets retained indefinitely; CloudTrail for all AWS management-plane events.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: VPC layout: public subnet (NLB for EMQX, ALB for Grafana/API), private-app subnet (ECS Fargate tasks, EMQX EC2 nodes), private-data subnet (RDS TimescaleDB, ElastiCache Redis, MSK Kafka). No public IPs on any data-tier resource. GitHub Actions CI pipeline: ruff lint and golangci-lint, pytest unit tests, Go unit tests, docker-compose integration test against TimescaleDB and Kafka and Redis, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy with health check gate. Environments: dev (single-AZ, reduced instance sizes), staging (multi-AZ, production-equivalent for load testing), production (multi-AZ ap-southeast-2a/2b, auto-scaling on Kafka consumer lag and CPU). Airflow DAG deploys via S3 sync on CI merge. Secrets managed via Secrets Manager with 30-day rotation schedule.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes Go ingest service (events_processed_total, kafka_consumer_lag, write_latency_seconds histogram) and Airflow StatsD exporter (dag_duration_seconds, task_failure_total). Grafana dashboards: Ingest Throughput events/s per bridge, Kafka Consumer Lag, TimescaleDB Write Latency p95/p99, Anomaly Alert Rate by Bridge, Sensor Quality Flag Distribution. PagerDuty escalation: Kafka consumer lag over 5 min (high), structural_alert severity=critical created (critical), RDS free storage under 20% (high), sensor quality_flag=failed rate over 5% for any bridge (medium).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated ~$2,800/month for 20-bridge production network: EMQX EC2 reserved $420, MSK Kafka $380, RDS TimescaleDB db.r6g.2xlarge reserved $680, ElastiCache $120, ECS Fargate tasks $450, S3 and Glacier $180, data transfer $120, misc $450. Go ingest tasks auto-scale on sensor-raw consumer lag; Airflow Fargate workers scale on Celery queue depth. TimescaleDB chunk compression reduces hot storage by ~85% for data older than 7 days.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 -- Pilot (10 weeks): Deploy EMQX cluster and Go ingest service for 3 pilot bridges; configure mTLS certificate provisioning; write telemetry_readings hypertable and basic structural_alerts; deploy daily-aggregate Airflow DAG; Grafana dashboards for strain and vibration per bridge; CI/CD pipeline with integration tests. Phase 2 -- Full Network (8 weeks): Scale to 20-bridge network; deploy anomaly-scan 15-minute DAG; fatigue_models table and weekly export DAG; S3 Iceberg archive with 90-day lifecycle; Grafana bridge network health overview; ASCE reporting exports. Phase 3 -- Intelligence (future): ML-based remaining service life estimator; automated maintenance work-order generation on critical alert; IFC/IEC 61850 protocol bridge for newer sensor types.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go ingest service: idiomatic Go with table-driven unit tests; benchmark Kafka consumer batch sizes targeting 1000 events/batch for throughput vs latency tradeoff. Python Airflow DAGs: each DAG in its own module with 90%+ unit test coverage via pytest; use TaskFlow API for clean data passing. TimescaleDB queries: all analytical queries must use continuous aggregate views, never scan raw hypertable directly for ranges over 1 hour. Schema migrations via Flyway with mandatory review for any hypertable index change. All secrets via Secrets Manager SDK -- never environment variables.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: EMQX single-node failure drops ingest for all bridges on that AZ -- mitigation: EMQX cluster with NLB and per-node health checks, automatic failover under 30s. Risk: TimescaleDB write throughput saturation during seismic events triggering simultaneous multi-bridge high-frequency bursts -- mitigation: Redis buffer queue with ECS ingest task scale-out. Tradeoff: TimescaleDB over InfluxDB gives richer SQL analytical query capability required for fatigue modelling but requires careful hypertable tuning for high-cardinality sensor dimensions. Risk: Iceberg schema evolution when new sensor types are added -- mitigation: Avro schema registry from Phase 1 with forward-compatible field additions only.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected MSK Kafka over SQS because 10 Hz per-channel throughput at 20 bridges exceeds SQS FIFO ordering limitations. Chose TimescaleDB over InfluxDB for richer SQL support required by fatigue model queries. Confirmed AWS Glue Schema Registry for Iceberg table evolution. EMQX selected over AWS IoT Core due to VPC-only constraint preventing use of the managed IoT service.

metadata.notes: Safety-critical bridge structural health monitoring ETL pipeline with TimescaleDB hypertables, Airflow DAGs, and mTLS sensor authentication.

project_goal.value: Project scheduling and resource allocation web application for mid-sized general contractors to plan labour crews, heavy equipment, and subcontractor slots across multiple concurrent construction sites with real-time critical-path conflict detection
project_goal.rationale: Core need: replace spreadsheet-based scheduling that causes crew double-bookings and equipment idle time costing contractors 8-12% project margin

target_users.value: Project managers, site supervisors, and resource coordinators at general contracting firms with 5-50 concurrent projects
target_users.rationale: Primary schedulers who currently manage resources across sites via shared Excel files

project_class.rationale: Browser-based interactive Gantt and resource board with real-time push updates; no native mobile required for MVP

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, analytics, realtime
capabilities.rationale: Interactive Gantt requires real-time WebSocket; analytics for utilisation reports; auth for multi-tenant contractor isolation

complexity_level.rationale: Critical-path recalculation on drag-and-drop, multi-site resource conflict detection, and real-time WebSocket fan-out are algorithmically demanding

risk_level.rationale: No health or payment data; confidential project financials and scheduling data require access control but not highest-tier compliance

data_sensitivity.rationale: Project schedules, subcontractor rates, and crew assignments are commercially sensitive competitive data

external_exposure.rationale: Login required; subcontractors access only their assigned slots, not full project schedules

access_model.value: Multi-tenant RBAC: contractor-admin, project-manager, site-supervisor, subcontractor-view roles; tenant isolation at row level in PostgreSQL using row_security
access_model.rationale: Multiple general contractor firms use the platform; each firm's data must be strictly isolated

feature_scope.value: Interactive Gantt with drag-and-drop rescheduling, crew assignment board, equipment reservation calendar, subcontractor slot booking, real-time critical-path recalculation, utilisation reports, ERP sync via REST, conflict alerts
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set identified in discovery workshops with three target contractors

mvp_scope.value: Gantt view for single project, crew and equipment assignment, critical-path display, real-time WebSocket updates for same-project collaborators, basic utilisation report
mvp_scope.rationale: Sufficient for pilot with two contractors before multi-site and ERP integration features

security_baseline.value: Auth0 OIDC with MFA for admin roles, HTTPS/TLS 1.3, row-level security on all project tables, CSRF protection, rate limiting on scheduling mutation endpoints
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential project data requires strong auth and tenant isolation

frontend_stack.value: React 18 with TypeScript; custom Gantt component built on canvas API; Zustand for local state; React Query for server state; Vite build
frontend_stack.rationale: Canvas-based Gantt delivers required 60 fps drag performance that DOM-based libraries cannot achieve at project scale

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Gin for HTTP API; gorilla/websocket for WebSocket connections; critical-path algorithm implemented in pure Go
backend_stack.rationale: Go delivers the sub-10ms critical-path recalculation latency required for interactive drag response

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 with row_security policies on GCP Cloud SQL; Redis 7 for WebSocket session registry and scheduling lock management
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL row_security handles multi-tenant isolation cleanly; Redis for distributed locking prevents schedule conflicts during concurrent edits

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot in us-central1; Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL; Cloud Memorystore for Redis
hosting_target.rationale: GKE Autopilot reduces operational overhead for initial launch; Cloud SQL managed failover

privacy_retention_policy.value: Project schedule data retained for duration of contract plus 7 years per construction contract record-keeping standards; audit logs retained 3 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Construction contracts subject to dispute resolution periods requiring record retention

future_scope.value: Mobile app for site supervisors, BIM model integration for quantity-driven scheduling, ML-based crew productivity forecasting, multi-currency subcontractor billing
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap pending MVP validation

constraints.value: Critical-path recalculation must complete under 200ms for projects up to 500 tasks; WebSocket must support 50 concurrent collaborators per project; initial budget $3,000/month GCP
constraints.rationale: Performance SLA required for interactive drag-and-drop UX; cost constraint for early customers

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM with Go tracer; structured JSON logging with project_id and tenant_id in every log line; critical-path recalculation latency tracked as custom metric
observability_baseline.rationale: Multi-tenant SaaS requires tenant-scoped performance monitoring to identify problematic project sizes

execution_preference.value: Two-week Agile sprints; feature flags for early access rollout to pilot contractors before GA
execution_preference.rationale: Pilot contractor feedback loop requires controlled feature rollout

compliance_context.value: Confidential project data requires SOC 2 Type II alignment for enterprise contractor customers; data residency in US regions only per several target customers
compliance_context.rationale: Medium risk and confidential data sensitivity trigger compliance documentation; enterprise contractors require SOC 2 evidence

project.goal: Real-time construction project scheduling with critical-path and resource conflict detection
project.target_users: Project managers, site supervisors, subcontractor-view users
project.timeline: Pilot in 12 weeks, GA in 6 months

frontend.gantt: Canvas-based custom Gantt component with drag-and-drop
frontend.state: Zustand for local UI, React Query for server state
frontend.realtime: WebSocket client for live schedule updates

backend.framework: Gin for REST; gorilla/websocket for WS

security.auth: Auth0 OIDC with MFA for admin/manager roles

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL with row_security
data.cache: Redis 7 (Cloud Memorystore) for WebSocket registry and distributed locks

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with Go tests and Playwright E2E

open_questions.erp_schema: Procore vs custom REST format for ERP sync endpoint -- needs customer input

reasoner_reviews.product: The interactive Gantt with real-time critical-path recalculation is the core differentiator -- every other construction scheduling SaaS uses static recalculation on save. Limiting MVP to single-project Gantt is the right call; multi-site resource board is a significant additional coordination problem. The subcontractor-view role must be carefully gated: subcontractors must see their assigned slots without seeing project financials or competing subcontractor rates.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go is the right choice for the critical-path algorithm: CPM (Critical Path Method) on a 500-node dependency graph must run in under 10ms to keep drag feedback immediate. The WebSocket fan-out model requires careful design -- Go channels are natural here, with a per-project goroutine managing subscriber broadcasts. Row-level security in PostgreSQL is the cleanest multi-tenant isolation pattern for this domain, but it adds query planning complexity; test all analytical queries against the RLS planner with realistic data volumes.
reasoner_reviews.security: Auth0 OIDC is appropriate for the multi-tenant model, but the row_security policies need rigorous testing -- a mis-scoped policy could allow a project manager at Contractor A to view schedules from Contractor B. Penetration testing of the RLS policies should be part of the pre-GA checklist. CSRF protection on all mutation endpoints is mandatory since Auth0 issues cookies.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 200ms critical-path SLA for 500-task projects is tight but achievable with a pure in-memory CPM implementation in Go -- avoid any database round-trips during recalculation. GCP $3,000/month accommodates GKE Autopilot with 2-4 nodes, Cloud SQL db-n1-standard-4, and Cloud Memorystore r3-m1 for the pilot scale.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The canvas-based Gantt component is a significant engineering investment -- budget at least 3 sprints for the core drag-and-drop interaction and conflict visualisation before it is pilot-ready. Off-the-shelf Gantt libraries like DHTMLX or Bryntum could accelerate delivery but add $15K+ licensing costs; evaluate total cost of ownership before committing to custom implementation.

frontend.app_structure: React SPA with route-based code splitting: /dashboard (project overview), /projects/:id/gantt (canvas Gantt view), /projects/:id/resources (crew and equipment board), /reports/utilisation (analytics). Vite build with chunk splitting per route.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Gantt View: canvas rendering of task bars with dependency arrows, drag-and-drop to reschedule, inline crew assignment popover, real-time conflict highlights in red. Resource Board: swim-lane grid of crew and equipment assignments by day across sites. Utilisation Report: stacked bar chart of crew-hours and equipment-days with PDF export.
frontend.state_management: Zustand store for Gantt interaction state (drag target, selected task, zoom level). React Query for server state: project tasks, resource assignments, critical path nodes. WebSocket events patch React Query cache directly on receipt to avoid full re-fetches.
frontend.performance_notes: Canvas Gantt must render 500 task bars at 60 fps during drag. Use offscreen canvas for background grid, requestAnimationFrame batching for task bar moves, spatial indexing (quadtree) for hit detection on click and hover.
frontend.frontend_risks: Canvas Gantt complexity is the highest engineering risk -- poorly implemented hit detection or re-render batching will cause janky drag performance that undermines the core value proposition. Prototype the canvas component in Sprint 1 before committing to the architecture.

backend.service_design: Single Go binary with two server types: HTTP (Gin) on port 8080 for REST API, WebSocket upgrade handler on same port at /ws/projects/:id. Per-project WebSocket hub implemented as goroutine with subscriber channel map. Critical-path engine as an internal package cpm with pure adjacency-list CPM implementation.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.1. Mutation endpoints (PATCH /tasks/:id, POST /assignments) acquire Redis distributed lock on project_id (TTL 5s) before write to prevent concurrent schedule corruption.
backend.business_modules: Scheduler module: task CRUD, dependency management, critical path computation on every task mutation. Resource module: crew_assignments, equipment_reservations, subcontractor_slots with conflict detection query. Analytics module: utilisation aggregation queries, PDF report generation via headless Chrome (Chromedp). Integration module: ERP webhook receiver and outbound sync.
backend.scaling_notes: WebSocket hubs are in-memory goroutines -- horizontal scaling requires WebSocket sticky sessions (NGINX upstream hash) or Redis Pub/Sub broadcast. Implement Redis Pub/Sub broadcast from Phase 2 to support multi-pod deployments.

security.auth_design: Auth0 OIDC with per-tenant application; JWT access tokens with tenant_id and role claims. Go middleware validates JWT signature against Auth0 JWKS endpoint (cached 1 hour). Auth0 MFA enforced for contractor-admin and project-manager roles.
security.authorization_model: PostgreSQL row_security policies: SET LOCAL app.tenant_id = $1 at transaction start; all project tables have USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.tenant_id')::uuid) policy. Subcontractor-view role has additional column-level USING for redacting rate fields.
security.abuse_prevention: Rate limiting on /tasks PATCH (30 req/min per user) to prevent denial-of-service through CPM recalculation spam. Redis sliding window counter per user_id.
security.security_risks: RLS policy bypass via misconfigured connection pooling (PgBouncer transaction mode clears session variables). Mitigation: use PgBouncer session mode or set tenant_id in every transaction explicitly.

data.entities: construction_projects (id UUID PK, tenant_id UUID, name, start_date DATE, end_date DATE, status ENUM planning/active/complete, contract_value_cents BIGINT). work_packages (id UUID PK, project_id FK, name, planned_start DATE, planned_end DATE, actual_start DATE, duration_days INT, is_critical BOOLEAN, dependencies JSONB). crew_assignments (id UUID PK, project_id FK, work_package_id FK, crew_type VARCHAR, headcount SMALLINT, site_id FK, assigned_date DATE). equipment_reservations (id UUID PK, equipment_id FK, project_id FK, work_package_id FK, reserved_from DATE, reserved_to DATE, site_id FK). subcontractor_slots (id UUID PK, project_id FK, subcontractor_id FK, trade VARCHAR, start_date DATE, end_date DATE, agreed_rate_cents BIGINT). critical_path_nodes (id UUID PK, project_id FK, computed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, node_ids JSONB, float_days JSONB). resource_calendars (id UUID PK, tenant_id FK, resource_type ENUM crew/equipment, resource_id UUID, unavailable_dates DATERANGE[]).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 with row_security on all project tables. Index on work_packages(project_id, planned_start) for Gantt range queries. Partial index on crew_assignments(site_id, assigned_date) WHERE status='confirmed' for conflict detection. Redis 7 for distributed project edit locks (SETNX with TTL) and WebSocket session registry (HSET project_id user_id pod_id).
data.consistency_notes: Critical-path recalculation is synchronous within the task mutation transaction -- if CPM fails, the task update rolls back. This prevents phantom schedules where tasks are saved but critical path is stale.
data.data_risks: N+1 queries in crew conflict detection across multi-site assignments. Mitigation: single denormalised conflict-detection query using PostgreSQL date range overlap operators with GiST index on date ranges.

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster in us-central1. Workloads: api-server Deployment (min 2, max 10 pods), Cloud SQL Auth Proxy sidecar per pod, Cloud Memorystore Redis via private service connect. Cloud Load Balancer with HTTPS termination, WebSocket upgrade support, and NGINX Ingress for sticky sessions.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go vet, golangci-lint, go test with race detector, Playwright E2E against staging, Docker build, Artifact Registry push, GKE rolling deploy via kubectl set image. Staging environment uses a separate Cloud SQL instance seeded with anonymised pilot data.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM with dd-trace-go. Custom metrics: cpm_recalculation_duration_ms histogram (p50/p95/p99), ws_active_subscribers_gauge per project, resource_conflict_detections_total counter. GKE pod logs to Cloud Logging with tenant_id and project_id structured fields.
devops.devops_risks: WebSocket sticky sessions break on pod restart or rolling deploy -- in-flight WebSocket connections drop. Mitigation: graceful drain (SIGTERM waits for active WS connections to close, max 30s) and client-side reconnect with exponential backoff.

target_output.thinking_summary: The core technical challenge is the interactive Gantt: CPM recalculation on every task drag must complete in under 200ms with the result broadcast to all project collaborators via WebSocket before the user releases the mouse. Go is the right runtime for both the CPM algorithm (pure in-memory graph traversal) and the WebSocket hub (goroutine-per-project fan-out). PostgreSQL row_security is the cleanest multi-tenant isolation pattern here, but requires careful PgBouncer configuration to prevent session variable leakage between tenant requests.
target_output.title: Construction Project Scheduling and Resource Allocation Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A multi-tenant web application for general contractors to schedule labour crews, heavy equipment, and subcontractor slots across concurrent construction sites. A canvas-based interactive Gantt view with drag-and-drop task rescheduling triggers sub-200ms critical-path recalculations via a Go CPM engine, broadcasting Gantt diff events over WebSocket to all project collaborators in real time. Resource conflict detection prevents crew double-bookings and equipment clashes across sites. End-of-week utilisation reports aggregate crew-hours and equipment-days and can be exported as PDF or synced to the contractor's ERP via REST.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier SaaS architecture on GCP. React 18 SPA served from Cloud CDN communicates over HTTPS and WebSocket to a Go 1.22 API server on GKE Autopilot behind a Cloud Load Balancer. The API server runs a Gin HTTP router and per-project WebSocket hubs (one goroutine per active project) that broadcast CPM diff events to subscribed project-manager and supervisor sessions. Critical-path computation runs in-process using a pure Go CPM package against an in-memory task graph loaded from PostgreSQL on project open. Resource conflict detection executes as a parameterised PostgreSQL query using date range overlap operators with a GiST index. PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL enforces tenant isolation via row_security policies with tenant_id set per transaction. Redis 7 on Cloud Memorystore manages distributed edit locks and WebSocket session registration for pod-aware routing.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, custom canvas Gantt component, Zustand, React Query, Vite. Backend: Go 1.22, Gin, gorilla/websocket, dd-trace-go, cpm internal package. Database: PostgreSQL 16 (Cloud SQL, row_security, GiST date-range index). Cache/coordination: Redis 7 (Cloud Memorystore, distributed locks, WS registry). Auth: Auth0 OIDC with JWT. Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot, Cloud Load Balancer with NGINX Ingress, Cloud SQL Auth Proxy, Artifact Registry, GitHub Actions CI, Datadog APM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Interactive Gantt: canvas rendering of task bars with dependency arrows; drag-and-drop to reschedule triggers CPM recalculation; critical-path tasks highlighted in amber; conflicts shown in red. Crew Assignment Board: swim-lane grid assigning crew types and headcounts to work packages per site per day; conflict detection via date-range overlap query. Equipment Reservation Calendar: drag-to-reserve interface for cranes, excavators, and plant equipment across sites; availability calculated from resource_calendars and existing reservations. Subcontractor Slot Booking: trade-specific slots with agreed rates visible to subcontractor-view users only for their own slots. Utilisation Reports: stacked bar chart of crew-hours and equipment-days aggregated by project, site, or crew type with date range selector and PDF export. ERP Sync: outbound webhook posts schedule updates to Procore-compatible REST endpoint on task status change.
target_output.system_components: Cloud CDN serving React SPA static bundle (Vite build). Cloud Load Balancer (HTTPS termination, WebSocket upgrade, NGINX Ingress with ip_hash sticky sessions). GKE Autopilot API server Deployment (min 2, max 10 pods, horizontal pod autoscaler on CPU 70%). Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (db-n1-standard-4, Multi-AZ, automated backups). Cloud Memorystore Redis 7 (r3-m1, 2 GB, persistence enabled for lock durability). Datadog Agent DaemonSet for APM and metrics collection. Cloud Logging for structured application logs.
target_output.workflows: Task Reschedule Workflow: Project manager drags task bar on canvas Gantt; frontend emits optimistic render and sends PATCH /v1/projects/PROJECT_ID/tasks/TASK_ID with new start and end dates. API server acquires Redis distributed lock on project_id (SETNX, TTL 5s), loads task dependency graph from PostgreSQL into in-memory adjacency list, runs CPM algorithm (critical path method, forward and backward pass), computes new early/late start dates and float for all successor tasks, persists changes in a single transaction, releases lock, and broadcasts GanttDiff event via project WebSocket hub to all subscribed sessions. All open browser sessions patch their React Query cache from the GanttDiff event. Utilisation Report Generation Workflow: Project manager selects date range and scope (project/site/all); API server executes aggregation query over crew_assignments and equipment_reservations joined to work_packages; results serialised to JSON and returned to frontend chart component; on PDF export request, API server launches Chromedp headless Chrome to render the report page and stream the resulting PDF.
target_output.data_model: construction_projects (id UUID PK, tenant_id UUID, name VARCHAR, start_date DATE, end_date DATE, status ENUM planning/active/complete, contract_value_cents BIGINT). work_packages (id UUID PK, project_id FK, tenant_id FK, name VARCHAR, planned_start DATE, planned_end DATE, duration_days INT, is_critical BOOLEAN, dependencies JSONB array of task UUIDs). crew_assignments (id UUID PK, project_id FK, work_package_id FK, tenant_id FK, crew_type VARCHAR, headcount SMALLINT, site_id FK, assigned_date DATE). equipment_reservations (id UUID PK, equipment_id FK, project_id FK, work_package_id FK, reserved_from DATE, reserved_to DATE, site_id FK, status ENUM tentative/confirmed). subcontractor_slots (id UUID PK, project_id FK, subcontractor_id FK, trade VARCHAR, start_date DATE, end_date DATE, agreed_rate_cents BIGINT, status ENUM pending/confirmed). critical_path_nodes (id UUID PK, project_id FK, computed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, node_ids UUID[], total_float_days JSONB). resource_calendars (id UUID PK, tenant_id FK, resource_id UUID, resource_type ENUM crew/equipment, unavailable_dates DATERANGE[]).
target_output.api_design: RESTful API at /v1 with Auth0 JWT authentication. GET /v1/projects returns contractor's project list. GET /v1/projects/PROJECT_ID/tasks?include=critical_path returns full task tree with is_critical flags. PATCH /v1/projects/PROJECT_ID/tasks/TASK_ID reschedules a task and triggers CPM recalculation. POST /v1/projects/PROJECT_ID/assignments creates crew or equipment assignment with conflict check. GET /v1/projects/PROJECT_ID/conflicts returns current resource conflicts. GET /v1/reports/utilisation?project_id=&from=&to= returns utilisation aggregation. POST /v1/reports/utilisation/pdf triggers async PDF generation, returns download URL. WebSocket: GET /ws/projects/PROJECT_ID upgrades to WS; server pushes GanttDiff, ConflictAlert, and PresenceUpdate event types.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Auth0 OIDC with MFA enforced for contractor-admin and project-manager roles. JWT access tokens carry tenant_id and role claims; Go middleware validates signature against cached Auth0 JWKS. PostgreSQL row_security: every API handler calls SET LOCAL app.tenant_id = X before any query; tables have USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.tenant_id')::uuid) RLS policy. Subcontractor-view role has additional column-level policy redacting agreed_rate_cents. CSRF protection via Auth0 SPA SDK PKCE flow and Origin header validation on WebSocket upgrade. Rate limiting: 30 PATCH /tasks requests per minute per user_id via Redis sliding window. SOC 2 Type II alignment: audit_log table records all task mutations with user, timestamp, old and new values. PgBouncer in session mode to preserve SET LOCAL tenant_id across connection lifetime.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot in us-central1 with Cloud Load Balancer and NGINX Ingress (ip_hash for WebSocket sticky sessions). API server pods include Cloud SQL Auth Proxy sidecar for IAM-authenticated PostgreSQL connections. HPA: min 2, max 10 pods on CPU 70% target. GitHub Actions CI pipeline: go vet, golangci-lint, go test -race, Playwright E2E against staging, Docker build, Artifact Registry push, kubectl set image rolling deploy (maxSurge 1, maxUnavailable 0). Staging: separate Cloud SQL instance seeded from anonymised production snapshot weekly. Graceful shutdown: SIGTERM handler waits up to 30s for active WebSocket connections to drain before pod termination. Secrets: GCP Secret Manager mounted as Kubernetes Secrets.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM with dd-trace-go instrumenting Gin router (HTTP latency/error rate) and all PostgreSQL queries (query latency by operation). Custom Datadog metrics: cpm_recalculation_duration_ms histogram with project_id and task_count tags, ws_active_subscribers gauge per project, resource_conflict_detections_total counter by conflict type. Structured JSON logs include tenant_id and project_id on every line. Alerts: CPM p99 over 200ms (high), WebSocket subscription failures rate over 5% (medium), Cloud SQL CPU over 80% (high).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated ~$2,600/month at pilot scale (20 contractor tenants, 200 active projects): GKE Autopilot 3 nodes $680, Cloud SQL db-n1-standard-4 $420, Cloud Memorystore r3-m1 $180, Cloud CDN $80, Cloud Load Balancer $50, misc observability and networking $190. Scaling: HPA handles daily traffic peaks. CPM recalculation is CPU-bound and does not hit the database during computation -- additional pods linearly increase CPM throughput. WebSocket horizontal scaling moves to Redis Pub/Sub broadcast when active projects per pod exceeds 200.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 -- MVP (12 weeks): Canvas Gantt component with drag-and-drop, CPM recalculation engine, WebSocket live updates for same-project collaborators, crew and equipment assignment with conflict detection, Auth0 multi-tenant authentication with RLS, basic utilisation report, GitHub Actions CI/CD. Phase 2 -- Multi-Site (8 weeks): Multi-site resource board, subcontractor slot booking with rate management, subcontractor-view role with column-level RLS, ERP webhook sync (Procore-compatible), PDF report export. Phase 3 -- Intelligence (future): ML crew productivity forecasting, BIM model integration for quantity-driven scheduling, mobile PWA for site supervisors.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go: all public package functions with table-driven unit tests; CPM package requires 100% test coverage with known graph fixtures. React canvas Gantt: pixel-accuracy unit tests for task bar positioning; Playwright E2E for drag-and-drop reschedule flow. PostgreSQL RLS: automated integration test suite verifies cross-tenant data isolation for every query pattern. All CPM algorithm changes require performance benchmark against 500-task synthetic project (target: under 10ms). Use feature flags for unreleased Gantt interactions during pilot period.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: CPM recalculation exceeds 200ms for complex multi-dependency projects -- mitigation: incremental CPM (recompute only affected subgraph on single-task change) reduces recalculation scope from O(n) to O(affected subgraph). Risk: PgBouncer session variable leakage in transaction pooling mode allows cross-tenant data access -- mitigation: PgBouncer session mode is mandatory, not transaction mode. Tradeoff: custom canvas Gantt over licensed library (Bryntum/DHTMLX) saves $15K/year licensing but costs 4-6 additional engineering sprints. Risk: Redis distributed lock TTL (5s) causes edit lockout if an API pod crashes mid-mutation -- mitigation: lock owner pod_id stored in Redis value; on lock acquisition failure, check if owning pod is still alive and force-expire if not.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose canvas API over SVG for Gantt rendering after performance testing -- 500 SVG elements with dependency arrows caused 30fps drops on mid-range hardware. Selected Auth0 over Cognito for multi-tenant OIDC because Auth0 organisations feature maps cleanly to contractor tenants. Confirmed PgBouncer session mode over transaction mode to preserve SET LOCAL tenant_id RLS variables. Redis Pub/Sub WebSocket broadcast deferred to Phase 2 -- single-pod sticky sessions sufficient for pilot scale.

metadata.notes: Construction project scheduling SaaS with real-time CPM recalculation, canvas Gantt, and multi-tenant RLS isolation.

project_goal.value: Automated quantity take-off tool that parses IFC-format BIM model exports, extracts material volumes and element counts by trade, and reconciles against approved bill-of-quantities spreadsheets, flagging discrepancies for the estimating team before each project milestone billing cycle
project_goal.rationale: Eliminate manual re-keying errors between BIM model and BOQ that cause billing disputes costing an average $50K per project

target_users.value: Construction estimators and quantity surveyors at a general contracting firm processing 20-50 projects per year
target_users.rationale: Primary users who currently re-key IFC model quantities into Excel BOQ templates manually

project_class.rationale: Backend automation pipeline with no interactive UI beyond internal portal for file upload and report download

capabilities.rationale: IFC parsing integration, scheduled reconciliation batch jobs, no realtime or frontend required

complexity_level.rationale: IFC parsing is domain-complex but the pipeline logic is straightforward; BOQ reconciliation is a structured diff algorithm

risk_level.rationale: Errors in quantity take-off can cause billing disputes; confidential project cost data must be protected

data_sensitivity.rationale: Project BOQ data contains confidential material rates, subcontractor costs, and margin information

external_exposure.rationale: Tool is accessed only by internal estimating team on the company network; no external portal

access_model.value: Custom JWT; estimator role uploads IFC and downloads reports; commercial-manager role approves reconciliation; admin role manages project configuration
access_model.rationale: Role-based access prevents estimators from approving their own discrepancy reports

feature_scope.value: IFC file upload and parsing, material volume extraction by trade category, BOQ spreadsheet import, automated reconciliation diff, discrepancy report generation, milestone billing batch job, email notification to commercial manager
feature_scope.rationale: Full workflow from BIM model to reconciliation report

mvp_scope.value: IFC parse and extract for concrete and steel trades, XLSX BOQ import, reconciliation diff report as PDF, manual trigger via internal portal, email notification
mvp_scope.rationale: Two highest-cost trades sufficient for pilot validation of accuracy and workflow

security_baseline.value: Custom JWT auth, HTTPS only, encrypted Azure Blob Storage for IFC uploads and BOQ files, no public endpoints, audit log for all reconciliation actions
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential project cost data requires access control and at-rest encryption

frontend_stack.value: None -- internal portal is a minimal FastAPI HTML form for file upload and report download; no SPA required
frontend_stack.rationale: Automation tool class; no interactive UI needed beyond file upload and report retrieval

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI; IfcOpenShell for IFC geometry parsing; openpyxl for XLSX BOQ import; ReportLab for PDF report generation
backend_stack.rationale: IfcOpenShell is the de facto Python IFC parsing library; openpyxl covers all XLSX BOQ variants encountered in practice

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL; Azure Blob Storage for IFC and BOQ file storage
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for structured quantity and reconciliation data; Blob Storage for large binary IFC files (can be 500MB per model)

hosting_target.value: Azure Container Apps on consumption plan; Azure DevOps Pipelines for CI/CD
hosting_target.rationale: Container Apps provides scale-to-zero for a batch workload that runs infrequently; no always-on compute waste

privacy_retention_policy.value: IFC model files and BOQ documents retained for project duration plus 7 years per construction contract records policy; reconciliation reports retained indefinitely
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Contract records required for dispute resolution periods

future_scope.value: MEP and finishes trade parsing, direct Revit API plugin for in-model take-off, integration with ERP procurement module for BOQ-to-purchase-order automation
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 expansion after pilot validates concrete and steel accuracy

constraints.value: IFC parse must complete within 10 minutes for models up to 1 GB; reconciliation diff report must be generated within 2 minutes of BOQ upload; Azure tenant only
constraints.rationale: Estimators need reports within a working session, not overnight

observability_baseline.value: Azure Monitor for Container App metrics; Application Insights for parse job duration and error tracing; alert on IFC parse failure
observability_baseline.rationale: Pipeline failures must be visible immediately so estimators are not blocked on deadline days

execution_preference.value: Kanban with 2-week delivery cycles; estimator team reviews each trade category parser before proceeding to next
execution_preference.rationale: Domain-expert sign-off on parser accuracy required before expanding to additional trade categories

compliance_context.value: Internal data classification policy for confidential project cost data; ISO 19650 BIM data management standards for IFC file handling and audit trail
compliance_context.rationale: Medium risk and confidential data sensitivity trigger compliance documentation requirements

project.goal: Automated IFC-to-BOQ quantity take-off and reconciliation pipeline
project.timeline: MVP in 8 weeks, full trade coverage in 6 months

security.storage: Azure Blob with SAS tokens for IFC and BOQ files

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL
data.files: Azure Blob Storage for IFC models and BOQ spreadsheets

devops.ci: Azure DevOps Pipelines with pytest and integration tests

constraints.parse_time: under 10 min for 1 GB IFC files

open_questions.mep_parser: IfcOpenShell support for MEP system volumes needs validation before Phase 2

reasoner_reviews.product: The two-trade MVP (concrete and steel) is the right scoping decision -- these are the highest-value trades for quantity disputes and the most structurally defined in IFC schemas. The milestone billing batch job is the production-critical workflow that justifies the investment. Estimators should be able to manually trigger a reconciliation at any time, not just on the scheduled batch, to support ad-hoc milestone sign-offs.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: IfcOpenShell is the correct library choice but the geometry kernel is single-threaded -- a 1 GB IFC model with 50,000 elements can take 8-12 minutes to parse on a single CPU. Consider chunking the model by IfcBuildingStorey and processing stories in parallel using multiprocessing.Pool to stay within the 10-minute SLA. The reconciliation diff algorithm should operate on the extracted quantity_line_items in PostgreSQL, not re-parse the IFC on every comparison.
reasoner_reviews.security: Azure Blob SAS tokens for IFC file access should be time-limited to 1 hour and scoped to the specific blob path, not the container. Service principal credentials for Blob Storage must be stored in Azure Key Vault, not application config files.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Azure Container Apps consumption plan scales to zero between jobs which is cost-effective, but cold start latency for the Python container with IfcOpenShell dependencies (~800 MB image) can be 45-60 seconds. Pre-warm the container instance during business hours (07:00-18:00 local) using a scheduled minimum replica of 1.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 plan did not address the IFC schema version compatibility problem. IFC2x3 and IFC4 have different property set structures for material volumes -- the parser must handle both versions or clearly document which version is supported and reject others with a clear error message rather than silently producing incorrect quantities.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with three endpoint groups: /upload (IFC and BOQ file ingest), /jobs (parse and reconciliation job management), /reports (reconciliation report retrieval and download). Long-running IFC parse jobs dispatched as background tasks via FastAPI BackgroundTasks to a Celery worker pool (Azure Container Apps worker profile) with Redis as the broker.
backend.api_patterns: POST /v1/jobs/parse accepts IFC blob reference and triggers async parse job; returns job_id. GET /v1/jobs/parse/JOB_ID polls status (queued/running/complete/failed). POST /v1/jobs/reconcile accepts boq_document_id and bim_model_id; returns reconciliation job_id. GET /v1/reports/REPORT_ID returns reconciliation diff as JSON for UI preview. GET /v1/reports/REPORT_ID/pdf streams PDF to browser.
backend.background_jobs: IFC Parse Job: IfcOpenShell opens IFC file from Blob Storage, iterates IfcBuildingStorey layers in parallel workers, extracts IfcWall/IfcSlab/IfcBeam/IfcColumn elements with material property sets, aggregates volumes by trade_category (concrete/structural_steel), writes ifc_elements and quantity_line_items to PostgreSQL. BOQ Reconciliation Job: openpyxl reads approved XLSX BOQ, extracts trade totals by line item code, matches against quantity_line_items using trade_category and element_code, computes delta quantities and percentage variance, writes discrepancy_reports rows, generates PDF via ReportLab.
backend.failure_handling: IFC parse failures (corrupt file, unsupported schema version) set job status=failed and write error detail to bim_models.parse_error_detail. BOQ import failures (missing trade columns, unrecognised line item codes) return 422 with field-level validation errors. Celery worker task retry: 2 attempts for transient Azure Blob read errors; no retry on schema validation failures.

security.auth_design: Custom JWT (HS256, 8-hour expiry for internal tool use). Login endpoint validates Active Directory credentials via LDAP bind and issues JWT with role claim. Estimator and commercial-manager roles gated at endpoint level via FastAPI dependency injection.
security.secrets_management: Azure Key Vault for all secrets: PostgreSQL connection string, Blob Storage account key, JWT signing secret, SMTP credentials. Container Apps environment variables reference Key Vault secrets via managed identity.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every IFC upload, BOQ import, reconciliation job trigger, and report download logged to audit_events table with user_id, action, document_id, timestamp. Report approval actions by commercial-manager logged with approval_notes.
security.security_risks: IFC file upload path is a potential vector for XXE or large file DoS against IfcOpenShell parser. Mitigation: validate Content-Type header, enforce 1.5 GB file size limit, run IFC parse in a separate process (subprocess isolation) to prevent parser memory corruption from crashing the API.

data.entities: bim_models (id UUID PK, project_id FK, filename, blob_path, ifc_schema_version ENUM ifc2x3/ifc4, parse_status ENUM pending/running/complete/failed, element_count INT, parse_duration_seconds INT, uploaded_by FK, uploaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ). ifc_elements (id UUID PK, model_id FK, element_type VARCHAR, element_global_id VARCHAR, trade_category VARCHAR, volume_m3 FLOAT8, area_m2 FLOAT8, storey_name VARCHAR). trade_categories (id UUID PK, code VARCHAR unique, name VARCHAR, unit ENUM m3/m2/t/nr). quantity_line_items (id UUID PK, model_id FK, trade_category_id FK, total_volume_m3 FLOAT8, total_area_m2 FLOAT8, element_count INT, computed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). boq_documents (id UUID PK, project_id FK, filename, blob_path, milestone VARCHAR, uploaded_by FK, uploaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ). discrepancy_reports (id UUID PK, boq_document_id FK, bim_model_id FK, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, total_discrepancies INT, max_variance_pct FLOAT8, report_blob_path VARCHAR, approved_by FK, approved_at TIMESTAMPTZ). milestone_billing_cycles (id UUID PK, project_id FK, milestone_name VARCHAR, scheduled_date DATE, status ENUM pending/triggered/complete).
data.schema_notes: ifc_elements table partitioned by model_id to isolate parse results per model. quantity_line_items has unique constraint on (model_id, trade_category_id) so re-parse replaces previous totals atomically. discrepancy_reports are immutable once generated -- no UPDATE permitted, only new report per reconciliation run.
data.migration_notes: IFC schema version enum must be extensible for future IFC4.3 support. Use Alembic migrations; add ifc4_3 to enum before Phase 2 MEP parser work.

devops.deployment_topology: Azure Container Apps environment with two container app definitions: api (FastAPI, min 1 replica 07:00-18:00, scale-to-zero outside hours) and worker (Celery, scale to 0-5 replicas on Azure Service Bus queue depth). Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server (standard_d2s_v3, zone-redundant). Azure Blob Storage for IFC and BOQ files with geo-redundant replication.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps Pipelines: pytest unit tests with IfcOpenShell fixture models, integration tests against containerised PostgreSQL, Docker build, push to Azure Container Registry, Container Apps revision deploy. Separate pipeline stage for IFC parser accuracy regression tests using 5 reference IFC models with known quantities.
devops.observability_stack: Azure Monitor for Container Apps replica count, CPU, and memory. Application Insights SDK (opencensus-ext-azure) traces IFC parse job duration, element count, and reconciliation diff computation time. Custom metric: ifc_parse_throughput_elements_per_second. Alert: parse job duration over 10 min (critical), reconciliation failure rate over 10% (high).
devops.devops_risks: IfcOpenShell image size (~800 MB with compiled C++ geometry kernel) causes slow Container Apps cold starts. Mitigation: pre-built layer caching in Azure Container Registry and minimum replica during business hours. Large IFC files in Blob Storage trigger 30-60s download before parse starts -- stream directly from Blob rather than copying to local disk.

ISS-001.title: IFC schema version compatibility not addressed
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan does not specify how the parser handles IFC2x3 vs IFC4 differences in material property set structures. Silent parsing of wrong schema version produces incorrect quantity totals that match neither model nor BOQ.

history.note: Identified by critic reviewer; not addressed in initial architecture

ISS-002.title: IFC parse performance for 1 GB models risks SLA breach
ISS-002.detail: IfcOpenShell single-threaded geometry kernel processes large IFC files in 8-12 minutes, violating the 10-minute SLA. Round 1 plan has no parallelisation strategy.

history.note: Identified by architect_reasoner; single-threaded parse not addressed in plan
