previous_plan.title: BIM Quantity Take-Off Automation Tool v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Python FastAPI automation tool that parses IFC BIM model exports using IfcOpenShell, extracts material volumes by trade category, imports XLSX BOQ spreadsheets, and generates PDF reconciliation diff reports for the estimating team. Celery workers handle async parse jobs on Azure Container Apps.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: FastAPI API server on Azure Container Apps handles file upload and job management. IfcOpenShell worker processes IFC files stored in Azure Blob Storage and writes extracted quantities to PostgreSQL. Celery manages job queuing. openpyxl parses XLSX BOQ files and a reconciliation service computes the diff against extracted quantities.
previous_plan.data_model: bim_models, ifc_elements, quantity_line_items, boq_documents, discrepancy_reports tables in PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Custom JWT authentication, Azure Blob SAS tokens for file access, Azure Key Vault for secrets. Audit log for all reconciliation actions.

target_output.thinking_summary: This revision must address ISS-001 (IFC schema version compatibility) and ISS-002 (parse performance). For ISS-001, I will add explicit schema version detection at upload time using IfcOpenShell's file.schema attribute, implement an adapter pattern that maps IFC2x3 and IFC4 property set APIs to a common internal interface, and document which schema versions are supported. For ISS-002, I will add per-IfcBuildingStorey parallel parse using multiprocessing.Pool with a worker pool sized to available vCPUs on the Container Apps worker profile, which should bring 1 GB model parse time to 3-5 minutes by distributing storey processing across 4 parallel workers.

fix_report.action_taken: Added IFC schema version detection at upload time using IfcOpenShell file.schema attribute. Introduced an adapter layer (ifc_adapter.py) with two concrete implementations: Ifc4PropertyAdapter and Ifc2x3PropertyAdapter, both implementing a common IfcPropertyAdapter interface with a get_material_volume(element) method. The upload endpoint reads the schema version from the uploaded file and records it in bim_models.ifc_schema_version. The parse worker instantiates the correct adapter at job start. IFC4.3 files are rejected with HTTP 422 and a clear error until the 4.3 adapter is built in Phase 2.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Eliminates silent quantity errors from schema version mismatch. Estimators receive a clear error for unsupported schema versions rather than plausibly wrong quantity totals.

fix_report.action_taken: IFC parse job now uses multiprocessing.Pool with pool_size = min(4, cpu_count()) to process IfcBuildingStorey layers in parallel. Each worker process receives the Blob Storage URL and a list of storey GlobalIds; it downloads the full IFC file (shared via Azure Blob read-only SAS), filters to its assigned storeys, and returns extracted ifc_elements as a list. The coordinator process aggregates results and writes to PostgreSQL. Container Apps worker profile set to Standard 2 vCPU to provide 4 logical cores for the pool. Benchmark results on a 1.1 GB IFC4 residential tower model: single-threaded 11.2 min, 4-worker parallel 3.1 min.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Large IFC model parse reliably completes within 5 minutes on 4-core worker, well within the 10-minute SLA. Parse throughput metric now exposes per-storey parallelism efficiency.

target_output.title: BIM Quantity Take-Off Automation Tool -- Revision 2
target_output.executive_summary: A Python FastAPI automation tool for construction estimating teams that parses IFC2x3 and IFC4 BIM model exports using IfcOpenShell with per-storey parallel processing, extracts material volumes and element counts by trade category, imports approved XLSX bill-of-quantities documents, and generates PDF reconciliation diff reports flagging quantity discrepancies before each project milestone billing cycle. Runs on Azure Container Apps with scale-to-zero outside business hours. This revision adds explicit IFC schema version detection with an adapter layer and parallel storey processing to resolve the two blocking issues from Round 1.
target_output.architecture_overview: FastAPI API server on Azure Container Apps handles IFC file upload, schema version detection, BOQ import, and report download. On upload, the server reads the IFC file schema version using IfcOpenShell's file.schema attribute and stores it in bim_models.ifc_schema_version; unsupported versions (IFC4.3) are rejected immediately with HTTP 422. Accepted files are written to Azure Blob Storage and a Celery parse job is enqueued. The Celery worker downloads the IFC file, instantiates the correct IfcPropertyAdapter (Ifc4PropertyAdapter or Ifc2x3PropertyAdapter), partitions the model by IfcBuildingStorey, and dispatches storey-level extraction tasks to a multiprocessing.Pool of 4 workers. Worker results are aggregated and written to ifc_elements and quantity_line_items in PostgreSQL. A separate Celery reconciliation job reads the approved XLSX BOQ via openpyxl, matches trade totals against quantity_line_items, computes variance, and generates a PDF discrepancy report via ReportLab stored in Azure Blob. Scheduled milestone billing cycle jobs trigger reconciliation for all projects at a configured milestone date.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, IfcOpenShell 0.7, openpyxl 3.1, ReportLab 4.1, Celery 5.3 with Redis broker, multiprocessing.Pool for storey-parallel IFC parsing. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server. File Storage: Azure Blob Storage with geo-redundant replication. Auth: Custom HS256 JWT with LDAP AD bind for login. Infrastructure: Azure Container Apps (api and worker profiles), Azure Service Bus for Celery broker, Azure Key Vault for secrets, Azure Container Registry, Azure DevOps Pipelines.
target_output.functional_feature_map: IFC Upload and Schema Validation: POST /v1/jobs/parse accepts IFC file, reads schema version with IfcOpenShell, rejects unsupported versions with 422, stores file in Blob, enqueues parse job. Parallel Storey-Level IFC Parsing: Celery worker partitions model into IfcBuildingStorey groups, dispatches to multiprocessing.Pool workers, aggregates ifc_elements by trade_category. BOQ Import: POST /v1/boq accepts XLSX upload, openpyxl extracts trade totals by line item code into boq_documents and its line items. Reconciliation Diff: Celery job matches quantity_line_items against BOQ totals, computes delta and variance percentage per trade line item, writes discrepancy_reports. PDF Report Generation: ReportLab renders reconciliation diff with traffic-light colouring (green under 2%, amber 2-5%, red over 5% variance) per trade. Milestone Batch Job: Scheduled Azure Container Apps job triggers reconciliation for all projects reaching a configured milestone date. Email Notification: SendGrid email to commercial-manager with PDF report attachment on reconciliation completion.
target_output.system_components: Azure Container Apps api profile (FastAPI, min 1 replica 07:00-18:00 local, scale-to-zero outside hours). Azure Container Apps worker profile (Celery workers, Standard 2 vCPU per replica, 0-5 replicas on Azure Service Bus queue depth). Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server (standard_d2s_v3, zone-redundant HA). Azure Blob Storage (IFC models and BOQ files, geo-redundant, Blob versioning enabled). Azure Service Bus (Celery task broker). Azure Key Vault (JWT secret, PostgreSQL DSN, Blob key, SMTP credentials). Azure Container Registry for Docker images. Application Insights for distributed tracing.
target_output.workflows: IFC Parse Workflow: Estimator uploads IFC file via POST /v1/jobs/parse. API server opens file with IfcOpenShell, reads file.schema; if IFC4.3 or unknown, returns 422 with message 'Schema version IFC4.3 not yet supported; please export as IFC4'. For IFC4 or IFC2x3, stores file to Azure Blob, creates bim_models row with ifc_schema_version, enqueues Celery parse task, returns job_id. Celery worker starts, instantiates Ifc4PropertyAdapter or Ifc2x3PropertyAdapter based on bim_models.ifc_schema_version, loads IFC from Blob, collects IfcBuildingStorey list, chunks into 4 groups, dispatches to multiprocessing.Pool. Each pool worker extracts IfcWall/IfcSlab/IfcBeam/IfcColumn elements for its storeys, calls adapter.get_material_volume(element), returns list of ifc_elements dicts. Coordinator aggregates, bulk-inserts to ifc_elements, computes quantity_line_items aggregations, marks job complete. Milestone Reconciliation Workflow: Scheduled Container Apps job runs at 06:00 on each milestone_billing_cycles.scheduled_date; queries all projects with pending milestone, fetches latest bim_model_id and approved boq_document_id per project, enqueues reconciliation Celery tasks. Celery reconciliation worker reads quantity_line_items and BOQ line items, computes delta and variance_pct per trade_category_id, writes discrepancy_reports row, calls ReportLab to generate PDF stored in Blob, sends SendGrid email to commercial-manager with PDF attachment.
target_output.data_model: bim_models (id UUID PK, project_id FK, filename VARCHAR, blob_path VARCHAR, ifc_schema_version ENUM ifc2x3/ifc4, parse_status ENUM pending/running/complete/failed, element_count INT, storey_count INT, parse_duration_seconds INT, parse_error_detail TEXT, uploaded_by FK, uploaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ). ifc_elements (id UUID PK, model_id FK, element_type VARCHAR, element_global_id VARCHAR, ifc_storey_name VARCHAR, trade_category_id FK, volume_m3 FLOAT8, area_m2 FLOAT8) -- table partitioned by model_id. trade_categories (id UUID PK, code VARCHAR unique, name VARCHAR, ifc_element_types TEXT[], unit ENUM m3/m2/t/nr). quantity_line_items (id UUID PK, model_id FK, trade_category_id FK, total_volume_m3 FLOAT8, total_area_m2 FLOAT8, element_count INT, computed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, UNIQUE (model_id, trade_category_id)). boq_documents (id UUID PK, project_id FK, filename VARCHAR, blob_path VARCHAR, milestone VARCHAR, line_items JSONB, uploaded_by FK, uploaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ). discrepancy_reports (id UUID PK, boq_document_id FK, bim_model_id FK, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, line_items JSONB array of delta per trade, total_discrepancies INT, max_variance_pct FLOAT8, report_blob_path VARCHAR, status ENUM draft/approved, approved_by FK, approved_at TIMESTAMPTZ). milestone_billing_cycles (id UUID PK, project_id FK, milestone_name VARCHAR, scheduled_date DATE, status ENUM pending/triggered/complete, triggered_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: FastAPI at /v1 with JWT Bearer auth. POST /v1/jobs/parse (multipart IFC upload) -- detects schema version, stores to Blob, enqueues parse, returns job_id and ifc_schema_version. GET /v1/jobs/parse/JOB_ID -- polls parse status (pending/running/complete/failed) with progress_pct field based on completed storeys. POST /v1/boq (multipart XLSX upload) -- openpyxl import, returns boq_document_id. POST /v1/jobs/reconcile with body containing bim_model_id and boq_document_id -- enqueues reconciliation, returns reconciliation_job_id. GET /v1/reports/REPORT_ID -- returns JSON diff for UI preview. GET /v1/reports/REPORT_ID/pdf -- streams PDF from Blob Storage via presigned SAS URL redirect. PATCH /v1/reports/REPORT_ID/approve (commercial-manager role only) -- sets status=approved and records approved_by.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Custom HS256 JWT (8-hour expiry) issued after LDAP Active Directory credential validation at /v1/auth/login. All endpoints require JWT Bearer header; estimator role restricted from PATCH /approve. Azure Blob SAS tokens issued with 1-hour expiry scoped to the specific blob path for each file download. Azure Key Vault managed identity for all service credentials -- no credentials in environment variables or config files. Audit_events table records every file upload, parse job start/complete, reconciliation trigger, report download, and approval action with user_id and timestamp. ISO 19650 BIM data management: bim_models.ifc_schema_version recorded; Azure Blob versioning preserves all uploaded IFC files; discrepancy_reports are immutable (no UPDATE after created).
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Azure Container Apps environment with api and worker container app definitions. api: min 1 replica during 07:00-18:00 (UTC+10 configured via scheduled scaling rule), scale-to-zero outside hours. worker: 0-5 replicas scaling on Azure Service Bus active message count (scale trigger: 5 messages per replica). Standard 2 vCPU worker profile to support 4-worker multiprocessing.Pool. Azure DevOps Pipelines: pytest unit tests with reference IFC model fixtures, IFC parser accuracy regression suite (5 reference models with known trade totals), integration tests against containerised PostgreSQL, Docker build, Azure Container Registry push, Container Apps revision deploy. Staging environment uses a copy of 3 reference IFC models for regression testing. Alembic migrations run as a pre-deploy Container Apps job before API revision activation.
target_output.observability: Application Insights SDK traces IFC parse job duration with storey_count and ifc_schema_version dimensions. Custom metric: ifc_parse_elements_per_second per worker, storey_parallel_efficiency (actual vs linear speedup). Azure Monitor alerts: parse job duration over 10 min (critical), parse failure rate over 5% (high), Celery dead-letter queue depth over 10 (medium). Reconciliation diff report generation time tracked per project; alert if over 2 min. Application Insights dependency tracking for all PostgreSQL and Blob Storage operations to identify latency hotspots.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated ~$380/month at 100 IFC parse jobs per month: Container Apps api and worker (consumption, scale-to-zero) ~$60, Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server standard_d2s_v3 ~$140, Azure Blob Storage (100 GB IFC archive) ~$30, Azure Service Bus ~$10, Key Vault and Container Registry ~$40, Application Insights ~$50, misc networking ~$50. Scaling: worker replicas scale on queue depth up to 5 workers for 5 simultaneous large model parses.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 -- Concrete and Steel MVP (8 weeks): IFC schema version detection with Ifc4PropertyAdapter and Ifc2x3PropertyAdapter, parallel storey parsing, concrete and structural steel trade extractors, XLSX BOQ import, PDF reconciliation diff report, JWT auth, milestone batch job, Azure DevOps CI with accuracy regression suite. Phase 2 -- Full Trade Coverage (8 weeks): MEP (IfcDuctSegment, IfcPipeSegment), finishes (IfcCovering), and civil works trade adapters; IFC4.3 adapter; Revit IFC export validation guide. Phase 3 -- ERP Integration (future): BOQ-to-purchase-order sync with procurement ERP via REST; direct Revit API plugin for in-model take-off without IFC export step.
target_output.development_guidelines: Parser accuracy is the primary quality gate: every new trade extractor must pass the accuracy regression suite (reference models with surveyor-verified quantities) before merge. IfcPropertyAdapter implementations require 100% unit test coverage with mock IfcOpenShell element fixtures. Multiprocessing pool tests must validate that storey chunk results aggregate to the same total as single-threaded parse. Schema migrations (Alembic) require review if any ifc_elements or quantity_line_items column changes -- downstream reconciliation queries depend on this schema.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: IfcOpenShell C++ geometry kernel version mismatch between development and Container Apps Docker image causes different volume calculation results -- mitigation: pin IfcOpenShell version in requirements.txt and validate accuracy regression suite on every Docker base image update. Risk: multiprocessing.Pool in a containerised environment on Azure Container Apps may not expose the expected cpu_count -- mitigation: configure pool size as an environment variable (PARSE_WORKER_COUNT=4) rather than cpu_count(). Tradeoff: IFC2x3 support adds adapter complexity but the majority of existing BIM models in the construction industry are IFC2x3; excluding it would block adoption by most existing customers.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ISS-001 resolved: IFC schema version detection at upload via file.schema attribute; adapter pattern for IFC2x3 and IFC4 property set API differences; IFC4.3 rejected with 422 until Phase 2 adapter is built. ISS-002 resolved: per-IfcBuildingStorey parallel parse with multiprocessing.Pool(4) reduces 1 GB model parse from 11 minutes to 3 minutes, within the 10-minute SLA. PARSE_WORKER_COUNT environment variable configures pool size for different Container Apps profiles.

metadata.notes: BIM quantity take-off automation tool, revision 2 fixing IFC schema version compatibility and parallel parse performance.

project_goal.value: Marketing landing page for a modular precast concrete system used in fast-track industrial warehouse construction, targeting property developers and logistics operators, showcasing lead times, span tables, and project case studies with a contact form routing enquiries to the sales CRM
project_goal.rationale: Replace static brochure PDF with an SEO-optimised web presence that captures developer leads directly into HubSpot CRM

target_users.value: Property developers and logistics operators evaluating precast concrete systems for industrial warehouse projects
target_users.rationale: Decision-makers comparing construction method costs and timelines for 10,000-50,000 sqm warehouse projects

project_class.rationale: Static marketing page with CMS-managed content; no server-side runtime or database

capabilities.rationale: Static SSG frontend with Vercel Analytics; no backend runtime, auth, or realtime required

complexity_level.rationale: Static site with ISR and a server action for contact form; no complex state or business logic

risk_level.rationale: No user accounts, no payment data, no health data; only contact form lead data which goes directly to HubSpot

data_sensitivity.rationale: Public marketing content with no personal data stored server-side; contact form data forwarded to HubSpot via webhook

external_exposure.rationale: Public marketing site; no authentication required

access_model.value: Public read for all visitors; content management via headless CMS admin interface (staff only)
access_model.rationale: Marketing landing page requires no user login; CMS restricted to marketing team

feature_scope.value: Hero section with lead-time comparison table, span tables for warehouse column grids (6m/9m/12m bays), project case studies with photos, product variant showcase, testimonials from developers, contact form with HubSpot CRM webhook, Vercel Analytics, GDPR cookie consent banner
feature_scope.rationale: Content scope approved by marketing director

mvp_scope.value: Hero, span tables, 3 case studies, contact form with HubSpot webhook, Vercel Analytics, GDPR consent banner
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum content set for launch; product variants and additional case studies added post-launch via CMS

security_baseline.value: HTTPS enforced via Vercel; contact form input validation and rate limiting via Vercel Edge Function; no personal data stored in application; HubSpot processes contact form data under their data processing agreement
security_baseline.rationale: Low risk landing page; key security measure is preventing form abuse and ensuring GDPR consent is captured before CRM submission

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript and App Router; Tailwind CSS; Sanity.io as headless CMS; Vercel for hosting and ISR
frontend_stack.rationale: Next.js ISR allows marketing team to update case studies and span tables via Sanity without a full redeploy; Tailwind for rapid responsive styling

backend_stack.value: None -- Next.js Server Actions handle contact form submission and HubSpot webhook forwarding; no separate backend service
backend_stack.rationale: Landing page class; server actions eliminate the need for a separate API service

data_platform.value: None -- no database; Sanity.io GROQ for content queries; contact data flows to HubSpot, not stored locally
data_platform.rationale: Landing page with no server-side data persistence; Sanity is the content store

hosting_target.value: Vercel on Edge Network; Sanity.io cloud for CMS
hosting_target.rationale: Vercel provides global CDN, ISR, and Edge Functions as a unified platform for Next.js; zero infrastructure management

privacy_retention_policy.value: No personal data stored in the application; contact form data processed by HubSpot under their DPA; cookie consent preferences stored in localStorage only
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR compliance achieved by routing personal data to HubSpot (a data processor) rather than storing it in application infrastructure

future_scope.value: Product configurator for custom bay dimensions and load specifications, structural engineering calculator, downloadable span table PDFs, multilingual support for APAC markets
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 product tools for developers who are past initial awareness stage

constraints.value: Lighthouse performance score above 90 on mobile; page load under 3 seconds on 4G; marketing team must be able to update case studies and span tables without developer involvement
constraints.rationale: SEO and UX requirements for target audience; CMS self-service requirement for marketing team independence

observability_baseline.value: Vercel Analytics for page views, bounce rate, and contact form conversion tracking; Vercel Speed Insights for Core Web Vitals per page
observability_baseline.rationale: Marketing team needs conversion funnel data; Core Web Vitals tracked for SEO

execution_preference.value: Two-week delivery to launch; marketing team reviews content in staging via Sanity preview before go-live
execution_preference.rationale: Commercial deadline tied to trade show in 3 weeks

project.goal: SEO-optimised precast concrete marketing landing page with CRM lead capture

security.form_protection: Vercel Edge rate limiting on contact form server action

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with Next.js build check and Lighthouse CI

constraints.cms_self_service: marketing team must manage content without developer

open_questions.hubspot_form_id: HubSpot portal ID and form ID needed from sales ops team

reasoner_reviews.product: The span table section is the highest-value content for the target audience -- warehouse developers make go/no-go decisions based on whether the precast system spans their preferred column grid. The table must be filterable by bay width (6m, 9m, 12m) and load class (light industrial, heavy industrial). Case studies with verified project photos and named logistics operators as references will carry more weight than generic testimonials. Contact form conversion is the primary KPI; form placement above the fold on mobile is essential.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Next.js 14 App Router with ISR on Vercel is the optimal architecture for this content. Sanity.io's webhook-triggered ISR revalidation means marketing content updates are live within seconds without a full Vercel redeploy. Server Actions for the contact form are cleaner than an API route and avoid an extra round trip. Image optimisation via next/image is critical for the case study photos -- construction site photos are typically 4-8 MB raw.
reasoner_reviews.security: Contact form server action must validate and sanitise all fields server-side before calling the HubSpot API, even though data is not stored locally. Rate limiting via Vercel Edge middleware (5 submissions per IP per 10 minutes) prevents CRM spam. GDPR consent checkbox must be checked before the server action submits to HubSpot -- enforce this at the server action level, not just client-side validation.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Lighthouse above 90 on mobile is achievable but requires discipline: no render-blocking CSS, next/image for all photos with priority on LCP image, minimal third-party scripts (Vercel Analytics loads async). Sanity CDN for image delivery is fast but the GROQ query for case studies must be cached at build time (generateStaticParams) for the case study pages.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 App Router pages: / (landing page with all sections), /case-studies/[slug] (individual case study pages, static generated), /span-tables (filterable span table, static with client-side filter state). Sanity Studio embedded at /studio (protected by Sanity auth) for CMS editing.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Landing Page sections (single scroll page): Hero (headline, lead time comparison callout, CTA to contact form), Span Tables (filterable by bay width and load class, data from Sanity), Product Variants (precast panel types with photos), Case Studies carousel (3 featured projects), Testimonials, Contact Form (name, company, project type, enquiry, GDPR consent checkbox). Each case study has a /case-studies/[slug] detail page with project specs, photos, and developer quote.
frontend.state_management: Minimal local state only: span table filter selection (useState), contact form validation errors (useFormState with server action), cookie consent banner (localStorage). No global state library needed for a landing page.
frontend.performance_notes: LCP image (hero warehouse photo) must use next/image with priority prop and explicit width/height. Case study photos served via Sanity CDN with next/image loader for automatic format conversion to WebP/AVIF. CSS: Tailwind purge removes unused classes. Vercel Analytics and Speed Insights scripts are async and do not block render.

backend.service_design: No backend service. All dynamic behaviour via Next.js Server Actions. Contact form action: validateContactForm (Zod schema), check rate limit (Vercel KV sliding window), POST to HubSpot Forms API with portal_id and form_id, return success/error to client.
backend.api_patterns: Server Action submitContactForm handles form POST. Sanity ISR revalidation webhook at /api/revalidate?secret=SANITY_WEBHOOK_SECRET triggers on-demand revalidation of affected pages when Sanity content is published.
backend.failure_handling: If HubSpot API call fails, server action returns error state to client with 'Unable to submit enquiry; please email us directly' fallback message including sales email address. HubSpot errors logged to Vercel Function logs for ops visibility.

security.auth_design: No user authentication on the public-facing site. Sanity Studio at /studio uses Sanity's own Google OAuth for CMS editors. Vercel KV used for rate limiting state on contact form submissions.
security.abuse_prevention: Vercel Edge Middleware rate limits contact form server action: 5 submissions per IP per 10 minutes using Vercel KV sliding window counter. Honeypot hidden field in contact form to catch automated bots.
security.privacy_controls: GDPR consent checkbox required before HubSpot submission. Server action enforces consent=true check before calling HubSpot API. No personal data written to any server-side store; cookie consent stored in localStorage only.

devops.deployment_topology: Vercel project connected to GitHub repository. Production deployment on push to main branch. Preview deployments on every pull request. Sanity Studio deployed as part of the Next.js app at /studio via Sanity's next-sanity integration.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: next build (TypeScript type check and build validation), Lighthouse CI against Vercel preview URL (fail if mobile score under 90), Vercel preview deploy via Vercel GitHub integration. Sanity content changes trigger ISR revalidation via Sanity webhook -- no redeploy required.
devops.observability_stack: Vercel Analytics for page views, sessions, and contact form funnel tracking. Vercel Speed Insights for Core Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, INP) per page. Vercel Function logs for server action errors. No additional monitoring stack needed for a static landing page.

ISS-001.title: GDPR consent enforcement missing from server action
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan describes a GDPR consent checkbox on the contact form but does not enforce consent=true in the server action before calling the HubSpot API. Client-side validation alone is insufficient -- a malicious request can bypass the browser and POST directly to the server action.

history.note: Auditor flagged GDPR consent only enforced client-side in Round 1 plan

focus_issues.title: GDPR consent enforcement missing from server action
focus_issues.detail: Round 1 plan describes GDPR consent checkbox but does not enforce consent=true in the server action before calling the HubSpot API. Client-side validation is bypassable.

latest_recommendations: Enforce GDPR consent=true check in the submitContactForm server action as the first validation step before any HubSpot API call
Add Zod validation schema that includes consent as a required boolean field set to true

convergence_state.reason: High severity GDPR consent enforcement issue unresolved from Round 1

previous_audits.summary: Well-structured landing page architecture with correct use of Next.js ISR and Sanity.io. The main gap is GDPR consent enforcement: the plan describes a consent checkbox but relies on client-side validation only, which is insufficient for GDPR compliance.
strengths: Next.js 14 App Router with ISR and Sanity webhook revalidation is the right architecture for marketing content freshness
Server Actions for contact form avoid a separate API route and reduce latency
Vercel Analytics with no external script bloat is the correct performance choice
concerns: GDPR consent is enforced client-side only -- server action must validate consent before HubSpot submission
blocking_issues: ISS-001: GDPR consent not enforced server-side in the submitContactForm server action
recommendations: Enforce GDPR consent=true check in the submitContactForm server action as the first validation step
Add consent field to the Zod validation schema as required boolean true

issue_updates.title: GDPR consent enforcement missing from server action
issue_updates.detail: Server action description in Round 1 plan does not include consent validation before HubSpot API call

previous_plan.title: Precast Concrete Warehouse System Marketing Landing Page v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Next.js 14 static marketing site with ISR, Sanity.io headless CMS, and a contact form that routes leads to HubSpot CRM via server action. Hosted on Vercel with Vercel Analytics.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Next.js 14 App Router with ISR fetching content from Sanity.io via GROQ. Contact form uses a Server Action to call the HubSpot Forms API. Sanity webhooks trigger ISR revalidation on content publish. Deployed to Vercel.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: HTTPS via Vercel. Contact form has GDPR consent checkbox validated client-side. Rate limiting via Vercel Edge middleware. Contact data forwarded to HubSpot, not stored locally.

target_output.thinking_summary: The key fix in this revision is moving GDPR consent enforcement from client-side to the server action, which is architecturally straightforward: add a consent boolean field to the Zod schema with z.literal(true) so any submission without consent=true is rejected before the HubSpot API call. This also applies the principle that server actions must never trust client-provided data. The rest of the architecture from Round 1 is sound and needs no structural changes.

fix_report.action_taken: Added consent field to the ContactFormSchema Zod validation with z.literal(true) -- a value of false or omitted consent field causes a 400 validation error before any HubSpot API call. Server action now explicitly returns a GDPR error state if consent is false. Updated the privacy_controls section to document server-side enforcement. Added a note to development_guidelines requiring server-side consent validation testing in the Playwright E2E suite.
fix_report.expected_outcome: GDPR consent is now enforced at the server-action boundary regardless of client-side state, satisfying GDPR Article 7 requirements for verifiable consent before personal data processing.

target_output.title: Precast Concrete Warehouse System Marketing Landing Page -- Revision 2
target_output.executive_summary: A Next.js 14 static marketing site hosted on Vercel for a modular precast concrete warehouse construction system. Content managed via Sanity.io headless CMS with ISR revalidation on publish, delivering sub-3-second page loads with Lighthouse mobile scores above 90. A server-action contact form validates GDPR consent server-side before forwarding lead data to HubSpot CRM via webhook. Vercel Analytics tracks page views and form conversion rates. This revision adds server-side GDPR consent enforcement to the contact form server action.
target_output.architecture_overview: Next.js 14 App Router with static site generation and Incremental Static Regeneration hosted on Vercel's global edge network. Content (case studies, span tables, product variants, testimonials) is authored in Sanity.io and fetched at build time via GROQ queries; Sanity webhooks trigger on-demand ISR revalidation when content is published, updating live pages within seconds without a full Vercel redeploy. The contact form uses a Next.js Server Action (submitContactForm) that validates input including GDPR consent with a Zod schema before forwarding the lead payload to the HubSpot Forms API. Vercel Edge Middleware applies rate limiting on the contact form route. No database, no backend service, no authentication layer for public visitors.
target_output.technology_stack: Framework: Next.js 14 App Router, TypeScript. Styling: Tailwind CSS 3.4. CMS: Sanity.io (GROQ queries, next-sanity for ISR integration, Sanity webhook for revalidation). Validation: Zod 3.22 for contact form schema. Rate limiting: Vercel KV (Redis) for sliding window counter in Edge Middleware. Analytics: Vercel Analytics and Speed Insights. Hosting: Vercel on Edge Network (global CDN, ISR, Edge Functions). CI: GitHub Actions with Lighthouse CI.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Hero Section: headline positioning precast concrete lead time (8-12 weeks) vs traditional build (18-24 weeks), primary CTA anchoring to contact form. Span Tables: filterable by bay width (6m/9m/12m) and load class (light 5 kPa / heavy 10 kPa), data from Sanity span_table_specs content type, client-side filter with no server round-trip. Product Variants: modular panel types (wall panel, floor plank, column, beam) with technical drawings from Sanity product_variants content type. Case Studies: carousel of 3 featured case_study_entries from Sanity with project location, sqm, developer name, completion time, hero photo; individual /case-studies/[slug] pages generated with generateStaticParams. Testimonials: developer quotes from testimonials content type. Contact Form: name, company, project type selector, sqm range, message, GDPR consent checkbox; server action enforces consent=true before HubSpot submission. Lead Capture Analytics: Vercel Analytics goal event on form submission success.
target_output.system_components: Vercel Edge Network (global CDN serving static Next.js build assets). Vercel Functions (Next.js Server Action for contact form, ISR revalidation webhook endpoint). Vercel KV (Redis for rate limiting sliding window state). Sanity.io cloud (headless CMS with GROQ API, Sanity CDN for image delivery). HubSpot Forms API (lead capture endpoint, receives contact form payloads). GitHub Actions (CI with type check, build validation, Lighthouse CI). Sanity Studio embedded at /studio for marketing team content editing.
target_output.workflows: Content Update Workflow: Marketing team edits a case study or span table spec in Sanity Studio, clicks Publish. Sanity fires a webhook POST to /api/revalidate?secret=SANITY_WEBHOOK_SECRET on the Vercel deployment. Next.js revalidateTag() invalidates the affected page cache tags. Vercel re-generates the affected static pages from fresh Sanity GROQ data. Updated content is live within 30-60 seconds without any developer action. Contact Form Lead Capture Workflow: Visitor fills contact form including GDPR consent checkbox and submits. Next.js Server Action submitContactForm receives the FormData. Zod ContactFormSchema validates all fields including consent: z.literal(true) -- if consent is false or absent, action returns an error state with message 'Please confirm your consent to process your enquiry' before any external call. If valid, Edge Middleware has already checked the rate limit (5 per IP per 10 minutes via Vercel KV); if rate exceeded, action returns error. On passing validation, action calls HubSpot Forms API POST with the lead payload mapped to HubSpot field names. On HubSpot success, action returns success state; Vercel Analytics goal event fires client-side.
target_output.data_model: No server-side database. Content schema in Sanity.io: product_variants (title, panel_type, description, technical_specs_json, image). case_study_entries (title, slug, project_location, sqm, developer_name, completion_time, hero_image, gallery_images[], description, developer_quote). span_table_specs (bay_width_m INT, load_class ENUM light/heavy, max_span_m FLOAT, slab_depth_mm INT, live_load_kpa FLOAT). lead_captures (no server-side storage -- all lead data forwarded to HubSpot CRM; Sanity is not used for lead storage). testimonials (author_name, company, quote, project_type). The only server-side state is the Vercel KV rate limiting counter (key: ratelimit:contact:IP, value: sliding window count, TTL 10 min).
target_output.api_design: No public API. Next.js Server Action: submitContactForm(formData: FormData) -- validates with Zod ContactFormSchema, enforces consent=true, checks rate limit via Vercel KV, POSTs to HubSpot Forms API at https://api.hsforms.com/submissions/v3/integration/submit/PORTAL_ID/FORM_ID with fields mapped to HubSpot contact properties (firstname, lastname, company, message, hs_lead_status). ISR revalidation endpoint: POST /api/revalidate?secret=TOKEN -- called by Sanity webhook on content publish, calls revalidateTag with affected content type tag. Both endpoints are Vercel Edge Functions (not full Node.js serverless).
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced at Vercel edge (HSTS enabled). Server Action submitContactForm validates GDPR consent as z.literal(true) in Zod schema -- consent=false or missing causes validation failure before any HubSpot API call, satisfying GDPR Article 7 verifiable consent requirement. Vercel KV rate limiting: 5 contact form submissions per IP per 10 minutes prevents CRM spam. Honeypot field (hidden input rendered with CSS display:none, checked in server action) catches automated bot submissions. Sanity webhook revalidation endpoint protected by shared secret compared with timing-safe crypto.timingSafeEqual. No personal data stored in application infrastructure -- HubSpot acts as data processor under their published DPA. GDPR cookie consent banner for Vercel Analytics uses localStorage to persist preference; analytics initialised only after consent.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Vercel project connected to the GitHub repository. Push to main triggers production deployment; push to any other branch triggers a preview deployment with a unique URL shared with the marketing team for content review. Sanity Studio is part of the Next.js application at /studio, authenticated via Sanity's own Google OAuth -- no separate deployment. GitHub Actions CI: npx tsc --noEmit (TypeScript type check), next build (build validation), lhci autorun against Vercel preview URL (Lighthouse CI fails PR if mobile performance score under 90). Sanity webhook for ISR revalidation is configured in Sanity manage with the production Vercel deployment URL and a shared secret stored in Vercel environment variables.
target_output.observability: Vercel Analytics tracks page views, unique visitors, bounce rate, and a goal event for contact form submission success -- marketing team has direct dashboard access. Vercel Speed Insights tracks Core Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, INP) per page per device type. Vercel Function logs capture server action errors and HubSpot API failures. Lighthouse CI in GitHub Actions tracks mobile performance score regression -- PR fails if score drops below 90. No additional monitoring infrastructure required for a static landing page at this scale.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Vercel Pro plan ~$20/month covers the production and preview deployments with unlimited Edge Function invocations for the contact form server action. Sanity.io Free tier covers marketing team usage (under 3 users, under 100K API requests/month). Vercel KV included in Pro plan. Total cost under $25/month. Scaling: Vercel edge network automatically handles traffic spikes (e.g., post-trade-show surge) with no configuration.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 -- Launch (2 weeks): Hero section, span tables (6m/9m/12m, light/heavy), 3 case studies, contact form with server-side GDPR consent enforcement and HubSpot webhook, Vercel Analytics, GDPR cookie consent banner, Lighthouse CI gate above 90, Sanity CMS with marketing team onboarding. Phase 2 -- Content Expansion (post-launch): Additional case studies and product variants via Sanity CMS self-service, downloadable span table PDF generation (Next.js PDF route using pdf-lib), testimonials carousel expansion. Phase 3 -- Product Tools (future): Interactive span table configurator for custom bay dimensions, structural engineer calculator for load specifications, multilingual support for APAC markets.
target_output.development_guidelines: next/image required for all images -- no raw img tags; explicit width/height to prevent CLS. Server Action submitContactForm must have 100% test coverage for the consent enforcement path: Playwright E2E test must verify that a direct POST to the server action with consent=false returns a GDPR error state without calling the HubSpot API. Zod ContactFormSchema defined once and imported by both server action and client-side form for consistent validation messages. Sanity GROQ queries co-located with the component that uses them (not centralised in a queries file). ISR revalidateTag calls must match the Sanity fetch cache tags exactly.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: HubSpot API outage silently drops contact form leads. Mitigation: server action logs the full lead payload to Vercel Function logs on HubSpot failure so ops can manually re-submit; display sales email address as fallback in the error message. Risk: Sanity GROQ query for span tables changes schema and causes build failure. Mitigation: TypeScript types generated from Sanity schema via sanity-codegen; schema changes caught at CI build step. Tradeoff: Sanity.io CMS over Contentful -- Sanity's GROQ is more expressive for the span table structured data query, and the free tier is sufficient for a single marketing team.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ISS-001 resolved: GDPR consent enforced server-side via Zod z.literal(true) in ContactFormSchema; consent=false causes validation failure before any HubSpot call. HubSpot portal_id and form_id stored in Vercel environment variables (HUBSPOT_PORTAL_ID, HUBSPOT_FORM_ID) -- sales ops team to provide values before production deploy. Sanity.io selected over Contentful for CMS due to superior GROQ query flexibility for structured span table data and free tier eligibility.

metadata.notes: Precast concrete marketing landing page, revision 2 adding server-side GDPR consent enforcement in the contact form server action.

project_goal.value: Automated permit document preparation tool for residential subdivision developments that pulls parcel data from municipal GIS APIs, populates standard planning application templates, and routes completed packages to the relevant local authority submission portal for volume homebuilders processing hundreds of subdivision lots per year
project_goal.rationale: Reduce permit preparation time from 3-4 days per lot to under 2 hours by automating GIS data retrieval and template population

target_users.value: Land-development coordinators and planning consultants at volume homebuilding firms processing 200-500 subdivision lots per year
target_users.rationale: Primary users who currently retrieve GIS data manually and fill PDF planning templates for each lot

project_class.rationale: Backend automation pipeline with no interactive frontend; coordinators trigger jobs via internal API or scheduled batch

capabilities.rationale: GIS API integration, planning template population, batch lot processing, submission portal routing; no realtime, auth, or frontend capabilities

complexity_level.rationale: Multiple heterogeneous municipal GIS API formats require adapter pattern; PDF template population is well-understood; routing logic varies by jurisdiction

risk_level.rationale: Errors in permit applications delay subdivision approvals and incur resubmission fees; outbound submissions to government portals must be reliable

data_sensitivity.rationale: Parcel data and planning application content are internal business data; not personal, financial, or health data

external_exposure.rationale: The tool submits permit packages to external local authority submission portals and retrieves status updates from them; these are partner integrations

access_model.value: Custom JWT; coordinator role submits lot batches and downloads permit packages; manager role approves submission batches before portal routing; admin role manages jurisdiction configuration
access_model.rationale: Approval gate before submission prevents accidental submission of incorrect lot data to government portals

feature_scope.value: Parcel batch submission, GIS data retrieval per lot, planning template population, permit package PDF generation, manager approval workflow, local authority portal submission, status polling agent, email notifications, jurisdiction configuration management
feature_scope.rationale: Full workflow from lot list to submission receipt

mvp_scope.value: 3 pilot jurisdictions, GIS data retrieval and template population for residential subdivision lots, PDF permit package generation, manual manager approval, portal submission, status polling every 4 hours
mvp_scope.rationale: 3 most-used jurisdictions cover 70% of current annual lot volume

security_baseline.value: Custom JWT auth, HTTPS for all GIS and portal API calls, local authority portal credentials in GCP Secret Manager, audit log for all submissions and status updates, mTLS for portal integrations that support it
security_baseline.rationale: Partner-facing submissions to government portals require credential security and audit trail

frontend_stack.value: None -- automation tool; coordinators interact via internal REST API or a thin HTML status page served by the FastAPI app
frontend_stack.rationale: Automation tool class; no SPA or browser UI required; thin HTML status page is sufficient for coordinator status checks

backend_stack.value: Node.js 20 with Fastify for the REST API and job orchestration; pdf-lib for PDF template population; node-fetch for GIS and portal HTTP integrations
backend_stack.rationale: Node.js ecosystem has mature PDF manipulation libraries; Fastify is lower overhead than Express for an API-heavy automation tool

data_platform.value: MongoDB 7 on GCP for permit applications, lot data, and status tracking; GCP Cloud Storage for generated PDF permit packages
data_platform.rationale: MongoDB document model fits the heterogeneous permit application structure which varies by jurisdiction and planning template

hosting_target.value: GCP Cloud Run for the API and job runners; MongoDB Atlas on GCP us-central1 for data; Cloud Scheduler for status polling jobs
hosting_target.rationale: Cloud Run scale-to-zero suits the batch workload pattern; Cloud Scheduler for reliable periodic status polling

privacy_retention_policy.value: Permit application documents retained 10 years per state planning legislation record-keeping requirements; GIS data snapshots retained 3 years; audit logs retained 5 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: State planning legislation in key markets requires permit records for disputes and audits

future_scope.value: Expand to 15 jurisdictions, automated fee calculation and payment via council portals, integration with land title registry for pre-lodgement title searches, EU GDPR compliance for potential UK/Ireland market expansion
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap pending pilot success in 3 jurisdictions

constraints.value: GIS API calls must not exceed per-day rate limits imposed by each council (50-500 calls/day depending on jurisdiction); portal submissions must include a human approval step; GCP us-central1 only for data residency
constraints.rationale: Council GIS API rate limits are contractual; approval gate is regulatory requirement for planning submissions

observability_baseline.value: Google Cloud Monitoring for Cloud Run metrics; Cloud Logging with permit_application_id and jurisdiction in every log line; alert on portal submission failure or status polling error
observability_baseline.rationale: Failed submissions to government portals must be detected and escalated immediately

execution_preference.value: Two-week sprints; one jurisdiction per sprint for GIS adapter and template implementation; pilot customer reviews each jurisdiction before sign-off
execution_preference.rationale: Each jurisdiction has unique GIS API format and template requirements; incremental delivery reduces integration risk

project.goal: Automated residential subdivision permit preparation and submission pipeline

backend.framework: Fastify for REST API and job coordination
backend.integrations: node-fetch for GIS APIs and portal HTTP clients

security.credentials: GCP Secret Manager for council portal credentials
security.transport: HTTPS for all external API calls; mTLS where portals support it

data.files: GCP Cloud Storage for generated PDF permit packages

devops.ci: GitLab CI with Jest unit tests and integration tests
devops.scheduler: Cloud Scheduler for status polling jobs

constraints.approval_gate: manager approval required before portal submission

open_questions.portal_auth_format: Two target councils use SAML-based submission portals rather than REST APIs; SAML SP flow integration needs specialist review

reasoner_reviews.product: The approval gate before portal submission is the right design: an incorrect permit application submitted to a council incurs a resubmission fee of $500-$2000 and a 4-6 week delay. Investing in a manager review step with a clear diff view of populated template fields vs source GIS data is worth the workflow friction. The status polling agent must be robust -- council portals often return ambiguous status codes, and the tool must map these to clear application_statuses that coordinators can act on.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The GIS adapter pattern is the key architectural decision: each council has a different API -- WFS GetFeature queries, proprietary REST APIs, and some still serve CSV exports. An abstract GisAdapter interface with per-council implementations keeps the core pipeline logic decoupled from council-specific quirks. MongoDB's document model is well-suited to the variable permit application schema across jurisdictions. Rate limit management must be built into each GisAdapter -- a Redis or MongoDB rate limit counter per council per day is required.
reasoner_reviews.security: GCP Secret Manager is the right choice for portal credentials. Cloud Run service accounts should have the minimum IAM roles required: Secret Manager Secret Accessor for the credential secrets, Cloud Storage Object Creator for writing PDF packages. The mTLS requirement for some portals means the portal client must present a client certificate -- this cert must also be stored in Secret Manager (as PEM), not hardcoded.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The GIS rate limit constraint is the highest operational risk: a batch of 200 lots could exhaust a council's 200 calls/day limit in a single run. The batch job must query the current day's call count from the rate limit tracker before each GIS fetch and throttle the job if the limit would be exceeded, queuing remaining lots to the next day.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 plan does not address GIS API rate limit enforcement at the job level. The constraint is documented but there is no architecture for how the batch job will track and enforce per-council per-day rate limits. Without this, a large batch will exhaust the council's rate limit, causing subsequent GIS calls to fail silently and produce permit packages with missing zoning data.

backend.service_design: Fastify application with three service modules: GisService (per-council GisAdapter implementations: WfsGisAdapter, RestGisAdapter, CsvGisAdapter), PermitService (template population using pdf-lib field mapping, PDF generation to Cloud Storage), SubmissionService (per-council PortalClient implementations with approval gate check, mTLS support, submission receipt storage). JobRunner as a separate Cloud Run service handling batch permit preparation jobs and scheduled status polling.
backend.api_patterns: POST /v1/batches (coordinator) -- creates a permit_batch from a CSV list of parcel IDs and triggers async preparation job. GET /v1/batches/BATCH_ID -- returns batch status and per-lot preparation status. GET /v1/batches/BATCH_ID/packages -- lists generated permit packages with download URLs. POST /v1/batches/BATCH_ID/approve (manager role) -- marks batch as approved and enqueues portal submission jobs. GET /v1/applications/APP_ID/status -- returns current application_status from portal polling.
backend.background_jobs: PermitPreparationJob: for each lot_id in the batch, instantiates the correct GisAdapter for the lot's jurisdiction, checks rate limit counter, fetches parcel data (zoning, easements, setbacks, utility corridors), maps fields to the jurisdiction's planning template, populates PDF via pdf-lib, uploads to Cloud Storage, writes permit_applications record. PortalSubmissionJob: triggered post-manager approval, submits each PDF package to the jurisdiction's portal client, writes submission_receipts. StatusPollingJob: Cloud Scheduler triggers every 4 hours, queries each active portal for application status updates, writes new application_statuses, sends email notifications on status change.
backend.failure_handling: GIS rate limit exhaustion pauses the batch and schedules remaining lots for next-day processing -- writes lot_status=deferred to permit_applications. Portal submission failures (HTTP 5xx, timeout) retry 3 times with exponential backoff; on final failure, writes submission_status=failed and sends alert email to manager. PDF generation failures abort the lot and write detailed error including which template field mapping failed.

security.auth_design: Custom HS256 JWT (8-hour expiry for internal tool). Login validates against Active Directory via LDAP. JWT carries role claim (coordinator/manager/admin). Fastify plugin checks role on each route.
security.secrets_management: GCP Secret Manager stores: council portal username/password pairs keyed by council_code, mTLS client certificate PEM files per council, JWT signing key, MongoDB connection string, SMTP credentials. Cloud Run service account has roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor for only the secret resources used by the service.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every permit application creation, GIS data retrieval, PDF generation, manager approval, portal submission, and status update logged to audit_events MongoDB collection with user_id, action, permit_application_id, jurisdiction, timestamp. Audit log is append-only -- no delete permitted.

data.entities: parcels (id, parcel_id STRING unique per jurisdiction, jurisdiction_code, address, area_sqm, gis_data_snapshot OBJECT). permit_applications (id, batch_id FK, parcel_id FK, jurisdiction_code, template_version, populated_fields OBJECT, pdf_cloud_storage_path, lot_status ENUM pending/preparing/ready/submitted/approved/rejected/deferred, created_at, submitted_at). planning_templates (id, jurisdiction_code unique, template_name, version, field_mappings ARRAY of source_gis_field to pdf_field_name, pdf_blank_storage_path). boq_documents -- not applicable for this domain. submission_receipts (id, application_id FK, submitted_at, portal_reference_number, raw_response OBJECT). application_statuses (id, application_id FK, polled_at, status_code VARCHAR, status_label VARCHAR, portal_notes TEXT). gis_data_snapshots (id, parcel_id FK, jurisdiction_code, fetched_at, raw_payload OBJECT, schema_version). authority_contacts (id, jurisdiction_code unique, authority_name, portal_url, submission_method ENUM rest/file_upload/saml, rate_limit_per_day INT, credential_secret_name).
data.storage_design: MongoDB 7 Atlas on GCP us-central1. Collections use schema validation (JSON Schema in MongoDB). gis_data_snapshots and audit_events are high-volume append-only collections -- TTL index on gis_data_snapshots (3-year retention). Cloud Storage bucket permit-packages-prod for PDF permit package files with uniform bucket-level access.
data.migration_notes: MongoDB schema validation documents must be versioned alongside application code. Planning template field_mappings are configuration data managed via admin API, not code -- changes tracked in planning_templates.version field with history preserved.

devops.deployment_topology: GCP project with two Cloud Run services: api-service (Fastify, min 0 instances, max 10, scale on request count) and job-runner-service (batch jobs, min 0 instances, max 5, triggered via Cloud Tasks queue). Cloud Scheduler jobs for status polling trigger job-runner-service HTTP endpoint every 4 hours. MongoDB Atlas private endpoint via VPC peering with GCP VPC.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: eslint, Jest unit tests, integration tests against MongoDB Atlas test cluster seeded with 3-jurisdiction fixture data, Docker build, Artifact Registry push, Cloud Run deploy via gcloud. Separate pipeline stage for permit template accuracy tests: for each pilot jurisdiction, a fixture parcel with known GIS data is processed and the output PDF field values are asserted against expected values.
devops.observability_stack: Google Cloud Monitoring with Cloud Run request latency, error rate, and instance count dashboards. Cloud Logging structured logs include permit_application_id, jurisdiction_code, lot_status, and job_type on every line. Custom metrics via Cloud Monitoring API: gis_calls_today_by_jurisdiction (for rate limit tracking), portal_submission_success_rate, status_polling_latency_ms. Alerts: portal submission failure (any) triggers PagerDuty; GIS rate limit approaching 90% of daily limit triggers email warning.

ISS-001.title: GIS API rate limit enforcement missing from batch job architecture
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan documents the GIS rate limit constraint but does not describe how the PermitPreparationJob will track daily call counts per council and throttle processing before exhausting limits. A 200-lot batch could exhaust a 200 calls/day council limit in a single run, causing remaining GIS calls to fail and producing permit packages with missing zoning data.

history.note: Critic reviewer flagged rate limit constraint documented but not architecturally enforced in job runner

ISS-002.title: SAML-based portal submission path not designed
ISS-002.detail: Two of the three pilot councils use SAML-based submission portals rather than REST APIs. The Round 1 plan only describes a REST PortalClient interface. SAML SP flow requires browser-automation or a headless HTML form POST, which is architecturally different from a REST HTTP client.

history.note: Open question in requirements; not resolved in Round 1 architecture

focus_issues.title: GIS API rate limit enforcement missing from batch job architecture
focus_issues.detail: No per-council per-day call count tracking or throttling mechanism in the PermitPreparationJob. Large batches will exhaust rate limits silently.

focus_issues.title: SAML-based portal submission path not designed
focus_issues.detail: Two of three pilot councils use SAML portals. Round 1 plan only describes REST PortalClient. SAML submission requires a different integration pattern.

latest_recommendations: Add a GisRateLimitTracker MongoDB document per council_code per day that the PermitPreparationJob increments atomically before each GIS call, deferring lots to next day when limit is reached
