best_plan.title: AKS Construction Platform - Initial Architecture
best_plan.executive_summary: AKS private cluster with Istio service mesh and Flux v2 GitOps for hosting construction microservices.
best_plan.architecture_overview: Private AKS cluster with Istio ingress; Flux v2 reconciling Helm releases per namespace; Keycloak OIDC for cluster auth.

target_output.thinking_summary: Resolving ISS-001 by defining concrete Flagger Canary analysis metrics thresholds: error rate <=1% via istio_requests_total 5xx ratio and p99 latency <=800ms via istio_request_duration_milliseconds histogram. Also resolving ISS-002 by enumerating specific NetworkPolicy allow rules for Prometheus cross-namespace scraping on port 9090 and Istio control plane on ports 15012 and 15014. Platform must maintain SOC 2 audit evidence via PostgreSQL audit_events and Kubernetes audit logs shipped to Loki.

fix_report.action_taken: Added explicit Flagger Canary analysis metrics: error rate threshold <=1% via istio_requests_total 5xx ratio and p99 latency <=800ms via istio_request_duration_milliseconds histogram. Canary increments 10% traffic every 5 minutes; 3 consecutive failed metric checks trigger automatic rollback to primary-stable revision.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Flagger will automatically halt canary promotions and revert traffic split when error rate or latency SLOs are breached

fix_report.action_taken: Enumerated explicit NetworkPolicy allow rules: prometheus-operator pods in monitoring namespace permitted ingress to all namespaces on port 9090; istiod in istio-system permitted ingress on port 15012 for xDS config push and port 15014 for control plane metrics.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Prometheus scraping and Istio service discovery will function under default-deny baseline without broad namespace-wide exceptions

target_output.title: AKS Construction Microservices Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A private AKS cluster hosting independently deployable construction domain microservices (scheduling, document management, cost control, site safety) with Istio service mesh, Flux v2 GitOps, and Flagger canary deployments with defined SLO safety gates. The Rust/Axum control plane API orchestrates Helm release operations and streams deployment events to a PostgreSQL audit trail. ISO 27001 and SOC 2 controls are enforced through Workload Identity, namespace-scoped RBAC, and Azure Defender for Containers.
target_output.architecture_overview: AKS private cluster with no public IP, accessed via Azure Private Link, running Istio 1.21 service mesh with strict mTLS PeerAuthentication. Flux v2 reconciles Helm releases from a Git monorepo under namespaces scoped per construction domain: scheduling, docs, cost, and safety. Flagger canary controller manages progressive traffic shifts via Istio VirtualService weight adjustments with error-rate and latency SLO gates now explicitly defined. Rust/Axum control plane API in the platform-system namespace is backed by Azure Database for PostgreSQL 16 for platform state and Redis 7 for distributed deployment locks. All secrets are injected via Azure Key Vault CSI driver with Workload Identity; no Kubernetes Secrets hold sensitive values. Azure Defender for Containers performs runtime threat detection across all node pools.
target_output.technology_stack: Platform runtime: AKS 1.29 with system node pools Standard_D4s_v5 and per-team user node pools. Service mesh: Istio 1.21 with strict mTLS PeerAuthentication. GitOps: Flux v2 with Kustomization per namespace. Deployment controller: Flagger 1.37 with Istio provider and SLO metric analysis. Control plane API: Rust 1.78 with Axum, Tower, and sqlx async PostgreSQL driver. IaC: Terraform AzureRM provider with module separation for cluster, networking, and namespaces. Observability: Prometheus Operator, Grafana 10, Loki, Alertmanager. Secrets: Azure Key Vault CSI driver with Workload Identity. Admission control: OPA Gatekeeper with ConstraintTemplates for Pod Security and resource quota enforcement.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Helm GitOps Deployment: Flux v2 reconciles chart updates from Git; Azure DevOps Pipeline runs helm lint, kubeval, and dry-run before merging. Canary Rollout Controller: Flagger Canary resources per microservice with 10% per 5-minute increment, automatic rollback on error-rate >1% or p99 latency >800ms. Secrets Rotation: Kubernetes CronJob triggers quarterly rotation via Azure Key Vault; updates ExternalSecret objects; performs rolling pod restarts with zero downtime. Namespace Isolation: OPA Gatekeeper enforces resource quotas, Pod Security Standards restricted profile, and NetworkPolicy default-deny per namespace. Platform Audit Trail: all Helm release operations written to audit_events PostgreSQL table with actor, chart version, and values diff hash.
target_output.system_components: AKS Private Cluster: 3-zone system node pool for Kubernetes control plane adjacency plus per-team user node pools with node taints. Istio Ingress Gateway: Azure Internal Load Balancer only, no public endpoint. Flux v2 Source Controller and Kustomize Controller polling Git every 60 seconds. Flagger Canary Controller: analysis interval 30s, threshold 3 consecutive successes, stepWeight 10, maxWeight 100, metrics error-rate <=1% via istio_requests_total 5xx ratio and p99-latency <=800ms via istio_request_duration_milliseconds histogram. Control Plane API in platform-system namespace. NetworkPolicy allow rules: monitoring/prometheus-operator ingress to all namespaces port 9090; istio-system/istiod ingress to all namespaces ports 15012 and 15014. OPA Gatekeeper admission webhook enforcing Pod Security restricted profile on all user namespaces.
target_output.workflows: Helm Chart Canary Deployment: DevOps engineer merges chart PR to main; Flux detects Git commit within 60 seconds and applies Kustomization; Flagger Canary resource initiates traffic split at 10% to new pod revision; Prometheus evaluates error-rate and p99 latency every 30 seconds; on 3 consecutive passing intervals traffic increments 10%; if error-rate exceeds 1% or p99 latency exceeds 800ms Flagger sets Canary status to Failed, rolls VirtualService weights back to 0% on primary-stable, and emits rollback event to Alertmanager which pages the on-call SRE. Secrets Quarterly Rotation: CronJob in platform-system namespace calls Azure Key Vault REST API to generate new secret version; patches ExternalSecret objects in all namespaces referencing the rotated vault path; CSI driver remounts tmpfs volumes on next pod restart; control plane initiates rolling restart of affected Deployments with maxUnavailable=0; rotation completion written to secrets_vaults.last_rotated_at and audit_events.
target_output.data_model: service_deployments (id UUID PK, namespace VARCHAR, helm_release_name VARCHAR, chart_version SEMVER, values_hash CHAR(64), status ENUM pending/running/succeeded/failed/rolled_back, deployed_by VARCHAR, deployed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, rollback_of UUID nullable). namespace_configs (id UUID PK, namespace VARCHAR UNIQUE, team_owner VARCHAR, resource_quota_cpu_milli INT, resource_quota_mem_mi INT, network_policy_version INT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). secrets_vaults (id UUID PK, namespace VARCHAR, key_vault_name VARCHAR, secret_ref VARCHAR, rotation_schedule CRON, last_rotated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). helm_releases (id UUID PK, release_name VARCHAR, namespace VARCHAR, chart VARCHAR, app_version VARCHAR, revision INT, values_sha256 CHAR(64), created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). cluster_nodes (id UUID PK, node_name VARCHAR, node_pool VARCHAR, instance_sku VARCHAR, allocatable_cpu_milli INT, allocatable_mem_mi INT, cordoned BOOL, last_seen_at TIMESTAMPTZ). service_mesh_policies (id UUID PK, namespace VARCHAR, policy_type ENUM traffic/retry/circuit_breaker, config_json JSONB, applied_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audit_events (id UUID PK, actor VARCHAR, action VARCHAR, resource_type VARCHAR, resource_id VARCHAR, payload_hash CHAR(64), occurred_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: Control plane REST API at /api/v1. POST /api/v1/releases with body {namespace, chart, version, values_override} triggers Flux reconciliation via webhook and writes pending service_deployments record. GET /api/v1/releases?namespace=scheduling returns Helm release history from helm_releases table. POST /api/v1/rollbacks with body {namespace, release_name, revision} calls helm rollback and records rollback_of FK. GET /api/v1/namespaces returns cluster topology from namespace_configs. POST /api/v1/secrets/rotate with body {namespace, secret_ref} triggers on-demand rotation. All endpoints require Keycloak JWT with platform-admin or namespace-owner claim; responses include X-Request-ID for audit correlation.
target_output.security_and_compliance: ISO 27001 and SOC 2 Type II controls: Azure Defender for Containers runtime threat detection on all node pools. OPA Gatekeeper admission policies enforce Pod Security Standards restricted profile: no privileged containers, no hostPath mounts, read-only root filesystem. Workload Identity binds each namespace service account to a dedicated Azure Managed Identity with least-privilege Key Vault access. Istio mTLS PeerAuthentication in STRICT mode prevents plaintext service-to-service traffic. Kubernetes audit policy at RequestResponse level for all write verbs; logs shipped to Loki with 12-month retention. NetworkPolicy: default-deny ingress and egress per namespace with documented allow rules: monitoring/prometheus-operator ingress to port 9090 across all namespaces; istio-system/istiod ingress to ports 15012 and 15014 across all namespaces; platform-system/control-plane egress to PostgreSQL port 5432 and Redis port 6379. Annual penetration test scoped to AKS API server and Istio ingress gateway.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform modules: aks-cluster for private cluster and node pools; networking for VNet and Private Link; platform-namespaces for RBAC and quotas. Azure DevOps Pipeline stages: PR triggers terraform plan (no apply); chart PRs run helm lint plus kubeval; helm upgrade dry-run against platform-staging; manual approval gate before prod Flux reconciliation trigger. Flux v2 GitRepository polling main every 60 seconds with Kustomization health checks before proceeding. Flagger Canary configuration: analysis interval 30s, threshold 3 consecutive successes, maxWeight 100, stepWeight 10, metrics error-rate <=1% and p99-latency <=800ms. Environments: platform-dev single-zone with one node pool; platform-staging multi-zone mirroring prod SKUs; platform-prod 3-zone with system and per-team user node pools. Rollback: Flagger automated on SLO breach; manual rollback via POST /api/v1/rollbacks; all rollback events paged via PagerDuty.
target_output.observability: Prometheus Operator with PodMonitor and ServiceMonitor CRDs per namespace. Grafana dashboards: DORA metrics per construction domain team (deployment frequency, lead time, change failure rate, MTTR); Flagger canary progress per service with traffic weight time-series; node pool utilisation (CPU, memory, pod density). Loki for structured JSON log aggregation from all namespaces; Kubernetes audit logs forwarded via Promtail. Alertmanager rules: error-rate >1% for 2 minutes pages on-call; p99 latency >800ms for 5 minutes warns; pod restart count >5 per hour critical; node NotReady critical. Azure Monitor Container Insights for node-level metrics.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: AKS system node pools: 3x Standard_D4s_v5 reserved instances approximately $420 per month. User node pools: burstable B-series with cluster autoscaler scaling 1 to 5 nodes per pool. PostgreSQL Flexible Server General Purpose 2 vCores zone-redundant HA approximately $180 per month. Redis C1 Standard approximately $55 per month. Azure Defender for Containers approximately $15 per node per month. Total estimated platform cost approximately $900 per month for 4 active microservice teams. Cost optimisation: autoscaler right-sizes user node pools overnight; reserved instances for stable system node pools.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Platform Bootstrap (6 weeks): Terraform modules for AKS private cluster, VNet, Private Link; Flux v2 installation; Istio 1.21 with strict mTLS; Prometheus Operator and Grafana; OPA Gatekeeper with baseline ConstraintTemplates; PostgreSQL and Redis provisioning; control plane API releases and namespaces endpoints; onboard scheduling microservice as first tenant. Phase 2 Safety and Secrets (4 weeks): Flagger Canary configuration with defined SLO thresholds for all onboarded services; Azure Key Vault CSI driver and quarterly rotation CronJob; Workload Identity per namespace; Loki log aggregation with Kubernetes audit forwarding; NetworkPolicy allow rules for Prometheus and Istio validated. Phase 3 Full ERP Migration (8 weeks): migrate document management, cost control, and site safety microservices; per-team user node pools; Grafana DORA dashboards; SOC 2 evidence package generation scripts.
target_output.development_guidelines: All IaC in Terraform with module versioning in Azure Artifacts feed; no direct kubectl apply in production. Helm charts must pass helm lint and kubeval in CI before merge. OPA Gatekeeper ConstraintTemplates tested with conftest unit tests in the same PR. Rust control plane: cargo clippy deny warnings in CI; integration tests against Kind cluster. PostgreSQL schema changes via sqlx migrate with rollback scripts mandatory. Git branch protection on main requires one SRE approval and all pipeline stages green.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Flux Kustomization scope misconfiguration could apply namespace-scoped changes cluster-wide. Mitigation: OPA Gatekeeper restricts Flux service account to write only within declared namespace paths; Kustomization path validated in CI. Risk: Flagger Canary SLO evaluation during low-traffic periods may produce statistically unreliable error-rate measurements. Mitigation: minimum request volume threshold of 100 requests per interval before Flagger evaluates metrics. Risk: Azure Key Vault throttling during mass concurrent rotation. Mitigation: CronJob staggers namespace rotation with 60-second delays between batches. Tradeoff: Rust control plane adds build complexity versus Go; justified by sub-10ms p99 for cluster management operations.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Flagger canary thresholds now defined as error-rate <=1% and p99 latency <=800ms with 10% per 5-minute increment. NetworkPolicy exceptions enumerated for Prometheus scraping port 9090 and Istio control plane ports 15012 and 15014. Azure Private Link confirmed for cluster access. Terraform state stored in Azure Blob backend with Azure Table Storage state locking.

metadata.notes: AKS construction platform revision resolving Flagger SLO threshold and NetworkPolicy exception gaps under ISO 27001 and SOC 2 scope.

project_goal.value: Modernised desktop application replacing a 1990s CAD macro toolkit for generating road cross-section drawings, cut-and-fill tables, and drainage layouts from survey CSV files, with AutoCAD COM automation for direct geometry insertion into drawing templates
project_goal.rationale: Core business need to replace unmaintainable VBA macros with a supportable Electron/Python application

target_users.value: Civil drafters and road design engineers at a national infrastructure contractor
target_users.rationale: Primary end users identified in requirements workshops

project_class.rationale: Native Windows desktop installer with local file processing and COM automation; no hosted service required

capabilities.rationale: Electron UI, embedded Python computation engine, SQLite project storage, and AutoCAD COM integration

complexity_level.rationale: Geometric computation pipeline and COM interop add complexity beyond standard CRUD; no distributed systems

risk_level.rationale: Local-only deployment with no network exposure; data is internal project files not regulated PII

data_sensitivity.rationale: Survey data and road designs are commercially sensitive internal IP but not regulated personal data

external_exposure.rationale: Application runs on drafter workstations with no outbound network calls; all data stays on-premise

access_model.value: Windows LDAP/Active Directory single sign-on; no application-level user management required for MVP
access_model.rationale: Corporate AD handles identity; application trusts Windows session identity

feature_scope.value: Survey CSV import, road alignment definition, cross-section profile rendering, cut-and-fill volume calculation per chainage interval, drainage element layout, AutoCAD COM geometry export, drafter project management
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set replacing VBA macro capabilities with improved UX

mvp_scope.value: Survey CSV import, cross-section profile rendering with cut-and-fill table, single road template export to AutoCAD via COM
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for pilot with one road project team

security_baseline.value: Windows Defender on host machine; no application-level authentication beyond Windows session; SQLite database file stored in user profile directory
security_baseline.rationale: Local-only app with low risk profile; enterprise endpoint security handles host-level protection

frontend_stack.value: Electron 31 with React 18 and TypeScript; Tailwind CSS for component styling
frontend_stack.rationale: Team has React expertise; Electron provides native Windows file system and COM access via node-ffi

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 embedded subprocess for geometric computation; NumPy and Shapely for cross-section and drainage calculations
backend_stack.rationale: Python ecosystem has mature geospatial computation libraries; subprocess isolation keeps main process stable

data_platform.value: SQLite via better-sqlite3 for drafter project storage; survey CSV files and DWG templates stored on local file system
data_platform.rationale: Embedded database for zero-configuration local storage; no server required

hosting_target.value: Windows 10/11 desktop installer packaged as MSIX via GitHub Actions
hosting_target.rationale: MSIX provides clean install/uninstall and supports corporate Intune deployment

privacy_retention_policy.value: Project files retained on drafter local workstation per corporate data retention schedule; no application-managed deletion
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Local desktop application; data lifecycle managed by corporate file server policies

future_scope.value: Multi-template road library, 3D terrain visualisation, integration with corporate project management system for drawing version control
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap items post MVP adoption

constraints.value: Must run on Windows 10/11; AutoCAD 2022+ COM interface; no mandatory internet connection; deployable via Microsoft Intune
constraints.rationale: Corporate workstation environment and AutoCAD version constraints

observability_baseline.value: Sentry local crash reporting with opt-in telemetry; structured log file at %APPDATA%/RoadDesignTool/logs
observability_baseline.rationale: Crash diagnostics without network dependency; logs assist drafter IT support

execution_preference.value: Iterative delivery with drafter feedback sessions after each sprint; pilot with one road project team before wide rollout
execution_preference.rationale: Legacy replacement requires drafter buy-in through iterative validation

project.goal: Replace VBA macro CAD toolkit with Electron/Python desktop app for road cross-section and drainage generation
project.target_users: Civil drafters and road design engineers
project.timeline: MVP in 10 weeks; full feature set in 20 weeks

frontend.state: Zustand for local UI state; React Query for Python subprocess results

backend.api: IPC via Electron contextBridge and stdin/stdout JSON protocol

data.files: CSV survey input, DWG template files, generated DXF output

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for Windows build and MSIX packaging

open_questions.com_stability: Confirm AutoCAD COM API stability across AutoCAD 2022, 2023, 2024 versions

reasoner_reviews.product: The scope correctly targets the three core drafter workflows: survey import, cross-section generation, and AutoCAD export. Cut-and-fill calculation is the highest-value feature for road design engineers and must be accurate to within 0.01 cubic metre per chainage interval to match the precision of the legacy VBA macros.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Electron with an embedded Python subprocess is a well-proven pattern for this class of desktop tool. The stdin/stdout JSON IPC protocol is simpler than gRPC for local communication and avoids Electron main-process blocking. Shapely for cross-section polygon computation is the right choice over implementing geometry from scratch.
reasoner_reviews.security: Local-only deployment significantly reduces the attack surface. The primary risk is DLL injection via a malicious AutoCAD plugin in the COM automation layer. Recommend validating the AutoCAD process identity before initiating COM connection and restricting the DXF output path to a user-controlled directory.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: AutoCAD COM API is consistent across 2022-2024 for the ActiveDocument and ModelSpace insertion operations needed. MSIX packaging must declare the com.server capability in the app manifest for COM automation access. No internet connectivity requirement is confirmed; Sentry must operate in offline crash-dump mode.

frontend.app_structure: Electron main process manages Python subprocess lifecycle, AutoCAD COM connection via node-ffi-napi, and SQLite access via better-sqlite3. Renderer process hosts React 18 SPA communicating via contextBridge IPC.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Project Manager screen (open/create/save drafter_projects), Survey Import wizard (CSV column mapping, chainage validation), Cross-Section Viewer (interactive profile canvas with cut-fill shading), Drainage Layout editor (element placement on plan view), Export dialog (AutoCAD template selection and COM export progress)
frontend.state_management: Zustand stores: activeProject, surveyPoints, crossSectionProfiles, drainageElements. React Query manages async Python computation results with loading and error states.
frontend.frontend_risks: AutoCAD COM automation may freeze Electron main process if AutoCAD is unresponsive; mitigated by spawning COM calls in a worker thread with 30-second timeout and user-facing cancel button

backend.service_design: Python 3.12 subprocess started on app launch; communicates via newline-delimited JSON over stdin/stdout. Modules: survey_parser.py (CSV ingestion and chainage interpolation), cross_section.py (cut-fill polygon computation using Shapely), drainage.py (grade calculation and sag detection), export_dxf.py (DXF entity generation)
backend.business_modules: cross_section.py: reads survey_points and road_alignment; computes intersection of design template with ground profile; calculates cut area and fill area per chainage using Shapely Polygon.intersection; returns cut_fill_tables entry. drainage.py: iterates drainage_elements; computes invert levels and grades; flags sag points where grade < 0.5%.
backend.failure_handling: Python subprocess exits non-zero on computation error; Electron main process captures stderr and surfaces structured error to React UI. Shapely geometry exceptions caught and returned as typed error JSON with chainage context.
backend.backend_risks: Shapely polygon intersection can produce unexpected geometry for near-tangent cross-sections; mitigated by tolerance-based cleanup using buffer(0) before area computation

security.auth_design: Windows AD session used implicitly; no login screen. drafter_projects SQLite records creator Windows username via os.getlogin() for audit trail.
security.authorization_model: Single-user local application; all local files accessible to logged-in Windows user. No multi-tenancy or role separation required.
security.abuse_prevention: COM automation validates AutoCAD process identity before inserting geometry; restricts output DXF path to user Documents folder via path validation.
security.security_risks: Malicious DWG template file could exploit AutoCAD renderer vulnerabilities; mitigated by instructing users to only open templates from corporate network share

data.entities: drafter_projects, survey_files, cross_section_profiles, cut_fill_tables, drainage_elements, drawing_templates, road_alignments
data.storage_design: SQLite database at %APPDATA%/RoadDesignTool/projects.db; survey CSV files and DWG templates referenced by absolute path; generated DXF output written to user-specified directory
data.schema_notes: survey_files.chainage_column stores the CSV column name for chainage interpolation; cut_fill_tables stores JSON array of {chainage, cut_area, fill_area, net_volume} per profile
data.migration_notes: Legacy VBA macro project files are .xls format; migration script reads Excel sheets into SQLite on first launch

devops.deployment_topology: Single MSIX installer bundle containing Electron binary, Python 3.12 embedded runtime, NumPy/Shapely wheels, and Node.js better-sqlite3. Deployed via Microsoft Intune App deployment to Windows workstations.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: PR triggers ESLint, Jest unit tests for React components, pytest for Python computation modules. Release workflow builds Electron app with electron-builder targeting win x64, packages MSIX, signs with corporate code-signing certificate, uploads to Intune package repository.
devops.backup_and_recovery: SQLite database file backed up by corporate endpoint backup policy; no application-managed backup. DXF output files saved to user-specified path within corporate network share.
devops.devops_risks: MSIX code-signing certificate renewal must be coordinated with IT security; expired certificate blocks Intune deployment. Python embedded runtime adds ~80MB to installer size.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a local-only Windows desktop app replacing 1990s VBA macros. Key decisions: Electron main process owns COM automation and SQLite; Python 3.12 subprocess handles all geometric computation via JSON IPC to avoid blocking the UI thread; MSIX packaging with embedded Python runtime eliminates Python installation dependency on drafter workstations. Cut-and-fill accuracy and AutoCAD COM stability are the primary engineering risks.
target_output.title: Road Cross-Section and Drainage Design Desktop Application
target_output.executive_summary: A Windows desktop application built with Electron 31 and React 18 that replaces a 1990s VBA CAD macro toolkit for civil drafters. Survey CSV files are ingested and processed by an embedded Python 3.12 subprocess using NumPy and Shapely to compute road cross-section profiles, cut-and-fill volumes per chainage interval, and drainage element grades. Generated geometry is inserted directly into AutoCAD drawing templates via COM automation. Project state is persisted in a local SQLite database with no network dependency.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-layer local architecture: Electron main process as the application host managing Python subprocess lifecycle, AutoCAD COM connection via node-ffi-napi, SQLite project database access, and native Windows file dialogs. Electron renderer process hosts a React 18 SPA communicating with the main process via contextBridge IPC. Python 3.12 subprocess receives computation requests as newline-delimited JSON on stdin and returns results on stdout, keeping all Shapely and NumPy heavy lifting out of the Node.js event loop. AutoCAD COM automation is dispatched from a worker thread to prevent blocking the Electron main process during DXF insertion. All data stays on the drafter workstation; no outbound network calls are made.
target_output.technology_stack: UI: Electron 31, React 18, TypeScript 5.4, Tailwind CSS. State: Zustand for project state, React Query for async Python results. Computation: Python 3.12 embedded runtime, NumPy 1.26, Shapely 2.0, pandas 2.2. Desktop integration: node-ffi-napi for AutoCAD COM, better-sqlite3 for SQLite. Packaging: electron-builder with MSIX target, corporate code-signing certificate. CI: GitHub Actions with Windows runner. Crash reporting: Sentry desktop SDK in offline mode.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Survey Import Module: drafter selects CSV file, maps column headers to chainage, easting, northing, elevation fields; app validates chainage monotonicity and reports out-of-tolerance gaps. Cross-Section Engine: Python subprocess computes Shapely polygon intersection of design template profile against ground profile per chainage station; returns cut area, fill area, and net volume table. Drainage Layout Tool: drafter places drainage_elements (culverts, kerb inlets, open drains) on plan view; Python subprocess computes invert levels, grades, and flags sag points below 0.5% grade. AutoCAD Export: drafter selects drawing_template, app COM-connects to running AutoCAD instance, inserts generated geometry into ModelSpace on designated layers, and reports inserted entity count. Project Manager: create, open, save, and rename drafter_projects with survey file associations and version notes.
target_output.system_components: Electron Main Process: Python subprocess manager (spawn, stdin/stdout JSON IPC, restart on crash), AutoCAD COM worker thread (node-ffi-napi ActiveX dispatch), SQLite manager (better-sqlite3 synchronous API), IPC handler (contextBridge event routing). Electron Renderer Process: React SPA with Survey Import wizard, Cross-Section Viewer canvas (HTML5 Canvas for profile rendering with cut-fill shading), Drainage Layout editor, Export dialog with progress indicator. Python Computation Subprocess: survey_parser.py, cross_section.py (Shapely), drainage.py, export_dxf.py. SQLite Database: projects.db in %APPDATA%/RoadDesignTool/. MSIX Installer bundle: Electron binary, Python 3.12 embedded, NumPy/Shapely/pandas wheels, better-sqlite3 native module.
target_output.workflows: Survey Import and Cross-Section Generation: drafter opens app, creates new drafter_project, clicks Import Survey, selects CSV file, app reads header row and prompts column mapping, Python subprocess parses CSV into survey_points rows with chainage interpolation, drafter selects road_alignment template, Python subprocess computes cross-section profiles for each chainage station using Shapely intersection of design template polygon against ground polygon, cut_fill_tables rows written to SQLite, Cross-Section Viewer renders profile canvas with blue fill polygon and red cut polygon, drafter reviews cut-and-fill table summary. AutoCAD Geometry Export: drafter selects drawing_template DWG file from file dialog, app connects to running AutoCAD 2022+ instance via COM using node-ffi-napi dispatch to Application.ActiveDocument, Python export_dxf.py generates DXF entities for each cross-section profile, COM worker thread calls ModelSpace.InsertBlock for each generated entity on the designated CROSS_SECTION layer, export dialog shows progress per chainage station, completion reports total entity count and any COM errors.
target_output.data_model: drafter_projects (id INTEGER PK, name TEXT, created_by TEXT windows_username, created_at DATETIME, last_opened_at DATETIME, notes TEXT). survey_files (id INTEGER PK, project_id FK, file_path TEXT, chainage_column TEXT, easting_column TEXT, northing_column TEXT, elevation_column TEXT, import_status TEXT, imported_at DATETIME). cross_section_profiles (id INTEGER PK, project_id FK, chainage REAL, ground_elevation REAL, design_elevation REAL, template_id FK). cut_fill_tables (id INTEGER PK, project_id FK, chainage REAL, cut_area REAL m2, fill_area REAL m2, net_volume REAL m3, computed_at DATETIME). drainage_elements (id INTEGER PK, project_id FK, element_type TEXT culvert/kerb_inlet/open_drain, chainage REAL, invert_level REAL, grade REAL percent, sag_flag BOOLEAN). drawing_templates (id INTEGER PK, name TEXT, dwg_path TEXT, autocad_layer TEXT, last_used_at DATETIME). road_alignments (id INTEGER PK, project_id FK, template_name TEXT, design_profile_json TEXT, created_at DATETIME).
target_output.api_design: Internal IPC protocol between Electron main process and Python subprocess via newline-delimited JSON. Requests: {cmd: 'parse_survey', file_path, column_mapping} returns {status, chainage_count, error?}; {cmd: 'compute_cross_sections', project_id, alignment_json} returns {status, profiles_count, cut_fill_summary, error?}; {cmd: 'compute_drainage', project_id, elements_json} returns {status, sag_violations: [{chainage, grade}], error?}; {cmd: 'generate_dxf', project_id, template_path, output_path} returns {status, entity_count, error?}. Electron contextBridge exposes ipcRenderer.invoke('python:request', payload) to renderer process. COM worker thread methods: connectToAutoCAD() returns bool, insertDxfToModelspace(dxf_path, layer) returns entity_count.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Local-only deployment with no network surface. Windows Defender and corporate endpoint protection handle host-level security. AutoCAD COM automation validates that the target process is a known AutoCAD executable path before dispatching COM calls to prevent COM hijacking. DXF output path restricted to user Documents folder or user-selected network share via Electron showSaveDialog path validation. SQLite database stored in %APPDATA% directory, accessible only to the logged-in Windows user account. Python subprocess spawned with the same Windows user identity as the Electron process; no privilege escalation. Sentry crash reporting operates in offline mode, writing crash dumps to %APPDATA%/RoadDesignTool/crashes for manual upload by IT support.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: MSIX installer bundled with electron-builder: Electron binary, Python 3.12 embedded runtime (python-embed package), NumPy 1.26, Shapely 2.0, pandas 2.2, and better-sqlite3 native module pre-compiled for win32-x64. GitHub Actions release workflow: ESLint and TypeScript tsc --noEmit, Jest unit tests for React components, pytest for cross_section.py and drainage.py computation modules, electron-builder MSIX build on Windows runner, code-sign with corporate Authenticode certificate via signtool.exe, upload to Intune App repository via Graph API. Environments: dev (local electron . with hot reload), staging (MSIX installed on test workstation with AutoCAD 2022 fixture), prod (Intune deployment to drafter workstation fleet). No rollback mechanism beyond Intune version assignment; previous MSIX retained in Intune repository for downgrade.
target_output.observability: Sentry desktop SDK configured for offline crash dump collection at %APPDATA%/RoadDesignTool/crashes; dumps uploaded manually by IT support when online. Structured log file at %APPDATA%/RoadDesignTool/logs/app.log with rotating 10MB limit; log entries include: Python subprocess spawn/exit events, COM connection attempts and failures, SQLite query errors, survey import chainage gap warnings. Python subprocess writes computation timing to stderr captured by Electron main process and logged as performance metrics: cross_section_compute_ms per chainage station, drainage_grade_compute_ms per element.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No hosting cost; application runs entirely on drafter workstations. Development cost: approximately 10 weeks of engineering time for MVP. MSIX installer size approximately 150MB including embedded Python runtime and scientific libraries. SQLite database expected to remain under 50MB per project for typical 500-station road alignments. AutoCAD COM automation throughput approximately 200 entity insertions per minute; a full 500-station cross-section export completes in under 3 minutes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Electron/React project scaffold with contextBridge IPC, Python subprocess with survey_parser.py and cross_section.py (Shapely cut-fill computation), SQLite schema for drafter_projects and cut_fill_tables, Cross-Section Viewer canvas with cut-fill shading, AutoCAD COM export for single template, MSIX build pipeline, pilot with one road project team. Phase 2 Drainage (5 weeks): drainage.py module with grade computation and sag detection, Drainage Layout editor in React with plan view canvas, drainage_elements SQLite table, drainage export to AutoCAD on dedicated DRAINAGE layer. Phase 3 Polish (3 weeks): multi-template road library, project history and version notes, legacy VBA .xls project file migration script, Sentry crash reporting integration.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode in Electron renderer and main process. Python modules must have pytest unit tests with numeric tolerance assertions (assert abs(result - expected) < 0.01 for area calculations). ESLint and prettier enforced in CI. Electron contextBridge must not expose Node.js APIs directly to renderer; all native operations proxied through typed IPC handlers. AutoCAD COM calls isolated in worker_thread to prevent main process blocking. GitHub PR requires one review from a civil engineering domain expert to validate computation output correctness.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: AutoCAD COM API differences between versions 2022, 2023, and 2024 may cause entity insertion failures. Mitigation: abstract COM calls behind an AutoCADAdapter class; test against all three AutoCAD versions in CI staging environment. Risk: Shapely polygon intersection returns empty or invalid geometry for near-tangent cross-sections at steep cut faces. Mitigation: pre-process cross-section polygons with buffer(0) to clean degenerate vertices; flag chainage stations with zero-area results for drafter review. Risk: embedded Python runtime increases installer size to approximately 150MB. Mitigation: use python-embed minimal distribution and pre-compile only required wheels. Tradeoff: Python subprocess over in-process Node.js: adds IPC overhead of approximately 5ms per request but isolates computation crashes from Electron main process.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: AutoCAD COM API confirmed consistent across 2022-2024 for ActiveDocument.ModelSpace.InsertBlock. MSIX com.server capability added to app manifest for COM automation access. Python embedded runtime confirmed compatible with NumPy 1.26 and Shapely 2.0 on Windows x64. Legacy VBA project file migration confirmed feasible via openpyxl.

metadata.notes: Civil drafter CAD desktop app replacing 1990s VBA macros with Electron/Python; AutoCAD COM integration is the key technical risk.

project_goal.value: Python library providing a unified interface for reading and writing structural analysis file formats (SAF, IFC-Structural, ETABS .e2k, STAAD.Pro .std) to enable BIM tool developers to exchange analytical models across platforms without bespoke format converters
project_goal.rationale: Market gap: no open-source Python library provides normalised read/write for all four structural analysis formats under one API

target_users.value: BIM software developers and structural engineering tool vendors integrating analytical model exchange into their platforms
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers are developers embedding the library, not end-user engineers

project_class.rationale: Distributed as a PyPI package; no hosted runtime, API server, or database

capabilities.rationale: Pure Python computation and file format parsing; no frontend or auth surface

complexity_level.rationale: Four distinct file format parsers with a normalised intermediate model; IFC4 structural schema mapping adds significant cardinality complexity

risk_level.rationale: Library processes local files with no network calls; no authentication, credentials, or sensitive data handling

data_sensitivity.rationale: Structural model files are technical engineering data, not personal or regulated data

external_exposure.rationale: Published on PyPI; pip-installable by any developer worldwide

access_model.value: No authentication; open-source MIT-licensed library; PyPI package with no access controls
access_model.rationale: Open library with no user-level access model required

feature_scope.value: SAF format read/write, IFC-Structural IFC4 read/write, ETABS .e2k read, STAAD.Pro .std read, normalised in-memory StructuralModel, typed exceptions with line-level context, IFC4 cardinality validation
feature_scope.rationale: Full format coverage required for vendor adoption

mvp_scope.value: ETABS .e2k read and SAF write, normalised StructuralModel with nodes, members, sections, and load cases, typed exceptions with file line context
mvp_scope.rationale: ETABS-to-SAF conversion is the highest-demand workflow among target BIM tool vendors

security_baseline.value: No network calls; input file path validation to prevent directory traversal; XML parser configured to disable XXE and external entity resolution for IFC files
security_baseline.rationale: File parsing library must guard against malicious input files

frontend_stack.value: none - library SDK with no UI surface
frontend_stack.rationale: Library distributed as Python package; no frontend component

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with dataclasses and typing; lark-parser for .e2k and .std grammar-based parsing; ifcopenshell for IFC4 model manipulation
backend_stack.rationale: lark provides maintainable grammar definitions for proprietary text formats; ifcopenshell is the standard Python IFC library

data_platform.value: In-memory StructuralModel dataclass; no persistent database; file I/O only
data_platform.rationale: Library operates on in-memory model objects; persistence is the host application concern

hosting_target.value: PyPI package distribution; GitHub repository with CI-published releases
hosting_target.rationale: Standard Python package distribution channel for developer libraries

privacy_retention_policy.value: No data retained; library processes caller-supplied file paths and returns in-memory objects; no logging or telemetry
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Library has no persistence layer; privacy is not applicable

future_scope.value: SAP2000 .sdb format read support, unit system conversion helpers (SI to imperial), streaming parser for large .e2k files exceeding 500MB
future_scope.rationale: SAP2000 is the third most requested format after ETABS and STAAD; large file streaming addresses high-rise building models

constraints.value: Python 3.10+ compatibility; no mandatory C extension dependencies beyond ifcopenshell (pre-compiled wheel available); library must be importable in zero-dependency test environment
constraints.rationale: BIM tool vendors require predictable dependency trees; C extensions with build tools are acceptable only for ifcopenshell

observability_baseline.value: Python standard logging at DEBUG level for parse events; no telemetry or crash reporting; callers configure log handlers
observability_baseline.rationale: Library must not impose logging configuration on host applications

execution_preference.value: Test-driven development with pytest; 100% unit test coverage for format parsers; semantic versioning with changelog
execution_preference.rationale: Format parsers are correctness-critical; TDD ensures regression safety as additional formats are added

project.goal: Python library for unified structural analysis file format read/write
project.target_users: BIM software developers and structural engineering tool vendors
project.timeline: MVP (ETABS-to-SAF) in 8 weeks; full four-format support in 20 weeks

backend.api: Public module API: read(path) -> StructuralModel, write(model, path, format)

security.input_validation: Path traversal prevention, XML XXE disabled for IFC parsing
security.dependencies: Minimal dependency surface; no network calls

data.files: SAF JSON, IFC4 STEP, ETABS .e2k text, STAAD.Pro .std text

devops.ci: GitHub Actions: pytest, mypy, ruff, build + publish to PyPI on tag

constraints.dependencies: ifcopenshell pre-compiled wheel only C extension

open_questions.ifc_version: Confirm IFC4 vs IFC4x3 target for structural schema mapping

reasoner_reviews.product: The four-format coverage is the right strategic scope. ETABS .e2k is the dominant input format for mid-to-high-rise structural analysis in APAC markets; SAF is the emerging interchange standard backed by the structural software consortium. Prioritising the ETABS-to-SAF path for MVP directly addresses the highest-demand BIM interoperability workflow.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: lark-parser with explicit grammar files for .e2k and .std is architecturally superior to regex-based parsing. The grammar files become the canonical format specification and can be unit-tested independently. The StructuralModel intermediate representation must be format-agnostic; format-specific fields should be stored in a metadata dict to avoid polluting the normalised model.
reasoner_reviews.security: IFC4 files are STEP-encoded and parsed by ifcopenshell, which has a native C parser. The main library-level security concern is the SAF format which is JSON-based: validate against a JSON schema before deserialisation to prevent unexpected field injection. Path traversal must be checked before any file open call.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Python 3.10 compatibility requires using TypeAlias and match/case only with version guards. ifcopenshell provides pre-compiled wheels for CPython 3.10-3.12 on Linux, macOS, and Windows; no build tools needed in CI. lark-parser is pure Python with no C extensions.

backend.service_design: Public API surface: structuralformat.read(path: str, format: FormatEnum | None) -> StructuralModel; structuralformat.write(model: StructuralModel, path: str, format: FormatEnum) -> None. Format auto-detection from file extension when format=None.
backend.business_modules: Parsers package: etabs_e2k.py (lark grammar for .e2k keyword sections: $NODE, $FRAME, $SECTION, $LOADCASE), staad_std.py (lark grammar for .std JOINT COORDINATES and MEMBER INCIDENCES blocks), saf_reader.py and saf_writer.py (JSON schema-validated SAF v1.1), ifc_reader.py and ifc_writer.py (ifcopenshell IFC4 IfcStructuralAnalysisDomain mapping). Models package: structural_model.py (StructuralModel, NodeDefinition, MemberElement, LoadCase, MaterialProperty, SectionProfile, SupportCondition dataclasses).
backend.failure_handling: Typed exception hierarchy: StructuralFormatError base; ParseError(line_number, token, format) for syntax errors; ValidationError(entity_id, constraint) for cardinality violations; UnsupportedFeatureError(feature, format) for format-specific unsupported constructs. All exceptions include source file path and line number.
backend.backend_risks: ifcopenshell IFC4 schema loading adds approximately 200ms cold-start overhead; mitigated by lazy schema loading on first IFC operation and optional pre-warm call for applications that need predictable first-call latency

data.entities: StructuralModel (nodes, members, load_cases, materials, sections, supports, metadata), NodeDefinition (id, x, y, z, coordinate_system), MemberElement (id, start_node_id, end_node_id, section_id, material_id, release_start, release_end), LoadCase (id, name, load_type ENUM dead/live/wind/seismic, load_patterns), MaterialProperty (id, name, elastic_modulus, poisson_ratio, density, yield_strength), SectionProfile (id, name, profile_type ENUM rectangular/circular/I-section/custom, dimensions_json), SupportCondition (node_id, restraint_dof BITMASK ux/uy/uz/rx/ry/rz)
data.storage_design: In-memory only; StructuralModel is a pure Python dataclass tree with no serialisation dependency. Callers can pickle or JSON-serialise via the provided to_dict() method.
data.schema_notes: ETABS .e2k grammar covers keyword sections: $NODE, $FRAME SECTION, $MATERIAL, $LOAD CASE, $JOINT LOAD, $FRAME LOAD. SAF v1.1 JSON schema enforced via jsonschema.validate before deserialisation. IFC4 mapping: IfcStructuralCurveMember maps to MemberElement; IfcStructuralPointConnection maps to NodeDefinition with IfcBoundaryNodeCondition to SupportCondition.

devops.deployment_topology: PyPI package; no server infrastructure. GitHub repository with branch protection on main.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions matrix: Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12 on ubuntu-latest, windows-latest, macos-latest. Steps: ruff lint, mypy --strict type check, pytest with coverage (100% target for parsers), build wheel via python -m build, twine check. Release: tag push triggers publish to PyPI via trusted publisher OIDC (no API key storage).
devops.devops_risks: ifcopenshell wheel availability must be verified for each new Python version before bumping lower bound in pyproject.toml. PyPI trusted publisher OIDC setup must be configured per repository.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a Python library SDK with no hosted runtime. Key decisions: lark-parser with explicit grammar files for ETABS .e2k and STAAD.Pro .std provides maintainable, testable parsers far superior to regex; ifcopenshell handles IFC4 STEP encoding; a format-agnostic StructuralModel intermediate representation is the core design value. The library API surface must be minimal and stable to avoid breaking BIM vendor integrations. Security focus is on preventing file-based attacks: path traversal, XML XXE, and malicious SAF JSON injection.
target_output.title: StructuralFormat Python Library for BIM File Format Interoperability
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.10+ library distributed via PyPI that provides read and write operations for SAF, IFC4-Structural, ETABS .e2k, and STAAD.Pro .std analytical model formats through a single normalised StructuralModel in-memory representation. BIM tool developers call structuralformat.read(path) to obtain a typed StructuralModel and structuralformat.write(model, path, format) to serialise to any supported target format. Typed exceptions with line-level context are raised for parse errors, cardinality violations, and unsupported format features. No hosted infrastructure; pure local file processing.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-package Python library with a public facade module routing read/write calls to format-specific parser and writer modules. ETABS .e2k and STAAD.Pro .std files are parsed using lark-parser grammar definitions that serve as the canonical format specifications. SAF v1.1 files are JSON-schema-validated before deserialisation. IFC4 files are processed via ifcopenshell, mapping IfcStructuralAnalysisDomain entities to StructuralModel dataclasses. A format-agnostic StructuralModel dataclass tree is the sole intermediate representation; format-specific artefacts are captured in a metadata dict. No network calls, no database, no authentication. The library is fully stateless; each read() call constructs a new StructuralModel and returns it to the caller.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12 (compatible with 3.10+). Parser engine: lark-parser 1.2 (pure Python, Earley and LALR grammars) for .e2k and .std. IFC processing: ifcopenshell 0.7 (pre-compiled CPython wheel). SAF serialisation: Python json module with jsonschema 4.22 for SAF v1.1 schema validation. Type checking: mypy 1.9 in strict mode. Linting: ruff 0.4. Testing: pytest 8 with pytest-cov. Build: python-build with pyproject.toml. Distribution: PyPI via GitHub Actions trusted publisher OIDC. No C extension dependencies beyond ifcopenshell.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Format Readers: etabs_e2k reader parses $NODE, $FRAME SECTION, $MATERIAL, $LOAD CASE, $JOINT LOAD, $FRAME LOAD keyword blocks via lark grammar into normalised StructuralModel. staad_std reader parses JOINT COORDINATES, MEMBER INCIDENCES, MEMBER PROPERTIES, SUPPORTS, and LOAD blocks. saf_reader validates SAF v1.1 JSON schema then maps nodes, bars, cross-sections, load cases to StructuralModel. ifc_reader maps IfcStructuralCurveMember to MemberElement and IfcStructuralPointConnection with IfcBoundaryNodeCondition to NodeDefinition with SupportCondition. Format Writers: saf_writer serialises StructuralModel to SAF v1.1 JSON with schema validation pass. ifc_writer generates IFC4 STEP file via ifcopenshell with required IfcProject and IfcStructuralAnalysisModel hierarchy. Validation: IFC4 cardinality constraints checked before write; SAF JSON schema validated before deserialisation. Exception reporting with source file path and line number for all parse and validation errors.
target_output.system_components: Public facade: structuralformat/__init__.py exports read(path, format=None) -> StructuralModel and write(model, path, format) -> None. Format auto-detection: detects .e2k, .std, .json (SAF), .ifc extensions. Parsers package: etabs_e2k.py with lark grammar file e2k.lark; staad_std.py with lark grammar file std.lark; saf_reader.py with jsonschema validation; ifc_reader.py via ifcopenshell API. Writers package: saf_writer.py; ifc_writer.py. Models package: structural_model.py defining StructuralModel, NodeDefinition, MemberElement, LoadCase, MaterialProperty, SectionProfile, SupportCondition dataclasses with to_dict() and from_dict() helpers. Exceptions package: errors.py defining StructuralFormatError, ParseError, ValidationError, UnsupportedFeatureError. Grammars directory: e2k.lark and std.lark checked into source for auditability.
target_output.workflows: ETABS to SAF Conversion: developer calls structuralformat.read('building.e2k') which auto-detects .e2k extension and delegates to EtabsE2kReader; lark parses $NODE section extracting node id, X, Y, Z coordinates into NodeDefinition objects; $FRAME SECTION maps section names to SectionProfile; $MATERIAL maps material constants to MaterialProperty; $LOAD CASE creates LoadCase objects; parser returns populated StructuralModel. Developer calls structuralformat.write(model, 'building_saf.json', Format.SAF); SafWriter maps StructuralModel entities to SAF v1.1 JSON structure; jsonschema.validate run on output dict before serialising to file; file written atomically via tempfile. IFC4 Export with Validation: developer calls structuralformat.write(model, 'building.ifc', Format.IFC4); ifc_writer checks cardinality constraints (every MemberElement must reference valid start and end NodeDefinition ids, every section_id must resolve to a SectionProfile); ValidationError raised with entity_id and constraint description for any violation; on success, ifcopenshell creates IfcProject, IfcStructuralAnalysisModel, maps each MemberElement to IfcStructuralCurveMember with IfcMaterialProfileSet; STEP file written to output path.
target_output.data_model: StructuralModel: nodes: list[NodeDefinition], members: list[MemberElement], load_cases: list[LoadCase], materials: list[MaterialProperty], sections: list[SectionProfile], supports: list[SupportCondition], metadata: dict. NodeDefinition: id str, x float, y float, z float, coordinate_system str default 'global'. MemberElement: id str, start_node_id str, end_node_id str, section_id str, material_id str, release_start: DofRelease, release_end: DofRelease. LoadCase: id str, name str, load_type Enum dead/live/wind/seismic/snow, load_patterns: list[LoadPattern]. MaterialProperty: id str, name str, elastic_modulus_mpa float, poisson_ratio float, density_kg_m3 float, yield_strength_mpa float | None. SectionProfile: id str, name str, profile_type Enum rectangular/circular/I_section/channel/custom, dimensions: dict[str, float]. SupportCondition: node_id str, ux bool, uy bool, uz bool, rx bool, ry bool, rz bool.
target_output.api_design: Public API: structuralformat.read(path: str | Path, format: Format | None = None) -> StructuralModel. Raises ParseError on syntax error with line_number and token. Raises UnsupportedFeatureError for format constructs outside the normalised model. structuralformat.write(model: StructuralModel, path: str | Path, format: Format) -> None. Raises ValidationError on IFC4 cardinality violation with entity_id and constraint. structuralformat.formats() -> list[Format] returns supported formats. Format enum: Format.ETABS_E2K, Format.STAAD_STD, Format.SAF, Format.IFC4. Exception hierarchy: StructuralFormatError(message, source_path) base; ParseError(line_number, token, format) for syntax failures; ValidationError(entity_id, constraint) for model integrity; UnsupportedFeatureError(feature, format) for unsupported constructs.
target_output.security_and_compliance: No authentication or network exposure. Path traversal prevention: input paths resolved via pathlib.Path.resolve() and checked to be within an allowed base directory if caller provides one; absolute paths outside the working directory accepted only with explicit allow_absolute=True flag. XML XXE prevention: IFC STEP files are parsed by ifcopenshell C parser which does not use Python xml libraries; no XXE risk. SAF JSON injection prevention: jsonschema.validate with strict SAF v1.1 schema rejects unexpected fields before any model construction. lark parser grammars are compiled to LALR tables at import time; malicious .e2k or .std files can cause ParseError but cannot execute code. Dependency supply chain: pyproject.toml pins ifcopenshell, lark-parser, and jsonschema to minor version ranges; Dependabot configured for automated security updates.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Python package with pyproject.toml build system. GitHub Actions CI matrix: Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12 on ubuntu-latest, windows-latest, macos-latest. CI steps: ruff check src tests; mypy --strict src; pytest tests/ --cov=structuralformat --cov-fail-under=100 for parser modules; python -m build; twine check dist/*. Release workflow: on tag push matching v*, build wheel and sdist, publish to PyPI via trusted publisher OIDC (no stored API key). Changelog: keep-a-changelog format updated in every PR touching public API. No server infrastructure, no containers, no deployment topology.
target_output.observability: Python standard logging: logger = logging.getLogger('structuralformat'); DEBUG level logs: file path opened, section keywords encountered in .e2k parser, entity counts per format section; WARNING for unsupported optional fields silently skipped. Callers configure log handlers and levels; library never calls logging.basicConfig. No telemetry or crash reporting; all error information surfaced via typed exceptions. pytest-cov enforces 100% coverage for parsers/ and writers/ subpackages; mutation testing via mutmut run on LoadCase and MemberElement mapping logic.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No hosting cost; pure Python package with no server infrastructure. ifcopenshell adds approximately 30MB to installed package size. lark grammar compilation adds approximately 50ms cold-start for .e2k parser (LALR table construction); cached after first import. Memory footprint scales linearly with model size: a typical 50-storey building .e2k file with 10,000 nodes and 40,000 members occupies approximately 80MB in-memory StructuralModel. Streaming parser for large files deferred to future scope.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): lark grammar for .e2k covering $NODE, $FRAME SECTION, $MATERIAL, $LOAD CASE; EtabsE2kReader mapping to StructuralModel; SafWriter with SAF v1.1 schema validation; typed exception hierarchy; mypy strict and 100% pytest coverage; PyPI package publish pipeline. Phase 2 STAAD and IFC (8 weeks): staad_std lark grammar for JOINT COORDINATES and MEMBER INCIDENCES; StaadStdReader; IFCReader via ifcopenshell IFC4 structural domain; IFCWriter with cardinality validation; SafReader with JSON schema validation. Phase 3 Completeness (4 weeks): remaining .e2k sections ($JOINT RESTRAINT, $FRAME LOAD); to_dict/from_dict for StructuralModel; streaming parser prototype for large files; SAP2000 format research spike.
target_output.development_guidelines: Test-driven development: every parser function must have a pytest test with a representative fixture file snippet before implementation. Fixture files checked into tests/fixtures/ directory. Grammar files e2k.lark and std.lark are the canonical format specifications and must be reviewed by a structural engineer before merge. mypy strict mode enforced in CI; no type: ignore comments without linked issue. ruff replaces flake8 and isort. Semantic versioning: public API changes require BREAKING label in changelog and major version bump. ifcopenshell dependency version bumps require CI matrix validation across all Python versions.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ETABS .e2k format has undocumented keyword variations between ETABS versions 9.7 and 21; grammar may not cover edge cases in all versions. Mitigation: collect .e2k sample files from target BIM vendors during Phase 1; grammar designed for easy extension with new keyword rules. Risk: ifcopenshell IFC4 schema changes between minor versions may alter IfcStructuralAnalysisDomain entity behaviour. Mitigation: pin to minor version range and test with IFC4 reference model fixtures. Tradeoff: lark LALR parsing is faster than Earley but requires unambiguous grammars; .e2k grammar requires careful conflict resolution for optional keyword arguments. Tradeoff: in-memory-only model means callers must serialise for persistence; accepted because library scope is format conversion, not model storage.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: IFC4 (not IFC4x3) selected as target because ifcopenshell 0.7 structural domain support is stable for IFC4 only. SAF v1.1 selected over v2.0 because v2.0 schema is still in draft. lark LALR grammar chosen over Earley for .e2k performance; grammar ambiguity to be resolved during Phase 1 development. PyPI trusted publisher OIDC configured on GitHub repository to avoid stored API key credential risk.

metadata.notes: Python library SDK for structural analysis file format interoperability; lark grammar-based parsing and ifcopenshell IFC4 mapping are the core technical elements.

project_goal.value: TypeScript/JavaScript SDK for querying and transforming geospatial construction site data from total stations, GNSS rovers, and drone photogrammetry outputs; normalises coordinates to a project datum and exposes typed methods for volume calculation, stake-out point generation, and progress-vs-design surface comparison
project_goal.rationale: Field data management platforms need a common SDK to avoid reimplementing coordinate normalisation and volume computation for each supported instrument type

target_users.value: Field data management platform developers integrating multi-instrument survey data processing into their applications
target_users.rationale: Library consumers are platform developers, not site engineers directly

project_class.rationale: npm package distributed for embedding in Node.js and browser applications; no hosted runtime

capabilities.rationale: Geospatial computation and data transformation; no UI or auth surface

complexity_level.rationale: TIN triangulation, coordinate reprojection, and cut-fill grid computation require careful numerical precision management

risk_level.rationale: Processes caller-supplied in-memory data with no network calls; no authentication or sensitive data

data_sensitivity.rationale: Survey point coordinates are technical site data with no regulated privacy implications

external_exposure.rationale: Published on npm; installable by any developer worldwide

access_model.value: No authentication; MIT-licensed npm package; no access controls
access_model.rationale: Open library with no user-level access model

mvp_scope.value: Coordinate reprojection to project datum, TIN surface triangulation from drone point cloud, compareSurfaces returning signed volume difference and cut-fill depth grid
mvp_scope.rationale: Drone photogrammetry progress comparison is the highest-demand capability for earthworks monitoring platforms

security_baseline.value: No network calls; input validation for NaN and Infinity coordinate values; no eval or dynamic code execution

frontend_stack.rationale: Library distributed as npm package; no frontend component

backend_stack.value: TypeScript 5.4 targeting Node.js 20 LTS and ES2022 browser; proj4 for coordinate reprojection; delaunator for Delaunay triangulation; no other runtime dependencies
backend_stack.rationale: Minimal dependency footprint critical for npm library adoption; delaunator is a fast pure-JS Delaunay implementation

data_platform.rationale: Library operates on caller-supplied in-memory arrays; persistence is the host application concern

privacy_retention_policy.value: No data retained; library processes caller-supplied arrays and returns new objects; no logging or telemetry
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Library has no persistence layer

constraints.value: Node.js 20 LTS and modern browser (ES2022) compatibility; no mandatory native dependencies; bundle size under 200KB minified for browser builds; tree-shakeable ESM exports
constraints.rationale: Field data platforms often run in offline browser contexts; bundle size and tree-shaking are adoption requirements
