Snowflake environments: DEV (developer sandbox), STAGING (integration test), PROD (live reporting). dbt model deployments use Snowflake zero-copy cloning from STAGING to PROD for near-instant promotion. Monte Carlo configured with freshness SLOs on fct_actual_expenditures (must update by 06:00 AEST) and volume anomaly detection alerting to PagerDuty on >10% row count change.
target_output.observability: Monte Carlo data observability: table freshness monitor on fct_actual_expenditures and fct_cost_commitments, volume anomaly detection on daily row counts, field health checks on amount columns for nulls and negatives. Airflow task instance logs to CloudWatch Logs group /airflow/tasks. PagerDuty escalation on: any Airflow DAG failure, Monte Carlo freshness SLO breach, reconciliation dbt test failure. Snowflake query performance dashboard via Snowsight for identifying expensive dbt model runs.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $2,800/month: EC2 m5.large reserved instance ~$70/month, Snowflake Enterprise consumption ~$2,500/month (2-4 XS warehouse hours nightly), S3 landing storage and requests ~$30, Monte Carlo SaaS ~$200. Scale path: Snowflake warehouse auto-suspend after 5 minutes of inactivity keeps compute costs aligned to actual nightly usage window.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Complete (delivered in round 1): Airflow DAG chain, Oracle JDBC extraction, S3 landing, Snowflake COPY INTO, dbt stg_ and mart models, reconciliation gate. Phase 2 Current (round 2 fixes): Snowflake column masking policies for AUDITOR role (ISS-001), IAM instance profile for Airflow EC2 (ISS-002), Monte Carlo freshness monitors, SOX change management workflow in GitHub Actions. Phase 3 Future: Oracle GoldenGate CDC for real-time streaming, automated variance commentary pipeline, contractor self-service Snowflake read access.
target_output.development_guidelines: dbt model naming convention: stg_{source}_{entity} for staging, int_{description} for intermediates, fct_{fact} and dim_{dimension} for marts. sqlfluff lint enforced in CI with Snowflake dialect. All financial amount columns must have a dbt column test for not_null and a masking policy applied. Snowflake DDL changes via dbt model changes only; no manual Snowflake schema edits in production. Oracle queries must use bind variables to avoid hard parsing; include /*+ FIRST_ROWS */ hint only on validated slow queries.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Oracle extract window exceeded by large delta during backfill or month-end close. Mitigation: configurable chunk_size parameter in cx_Oracle operator; alert if extract exceeds 3.5 hours. Risk: Snowflake masking policy silently returning wrong values after schema change. Mitigation: dbt test asserting AUDITOR role cannot select raw amount values in CI against STAGING environment. Tradeoff: Snowflake us-east-1 with ap-southeast-2 Airflow introduces cross-region data transfer latency; acceptable given nightly batch cadence and no real-time requirement in MVP.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose Snowflake time-travel over custom audit snapshot tables for point-in-time auditor reproductions. Selected Monte Carlo over Great Expectations for data observability due to pre-built Snowflake integration. Confirmed Okta SSO for Snowflake rather than LDAP sync for SOX-compliant access logging.

metadata.notes: Oracle-to-Snowflake construction cost pipeline; round 2 fixes Snowflake column masking and IAM instance profile issues; SOX-compliant architecture.

profile.domain: Civil Engineering Drawing Review and RFI Management

project_goal.value: A real-time collaborative platform for civil engineering drawing reviews where multiple reviewers can annotate PDF and DWG drawings simultaneously, raise formal RFIs, and track comment resolution status across multi-office design-and-construct project teams
project_goal.rationale: Replaces email-based drawing markup distribution; enables concurrent multi-reviewer annotation with conflict resolution and formal RFI lifecycle management

target_users.value: Design engineers, document controllers, and construction site engineers at design-and-construct firms working across multiple office and site locations
target_users.rationale: Primary users identified in discovery workshops; site engineers need mobile-friendly review access

project_class.rationale: Requires Next.js SSR frontend, Go REST and WebSocket backend, and PostgreSQL persistence

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, realtime, integrations, analytics
capabilities.rationale: WebSocket real-time annotation sync, Auth0 SSO, email integrations for RFI notifications, drawing transmittal analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Operational transform for concurrent annotations, DWG rasterisation pipeline, RFI lifecycle with due-date escalation, and multi-office SSO add significant complexity

risk_level.rationale: Confidential engineering drawings; IP leakage risk but not regulated personal or financial data

data_sensitivity.rationale: Engineering drawings and RFI content constitute confidential project IP; project access must be strictly scoped

external_exposure.rationale: All endpoints require Auth0 JWT; no public access to drawings or RFIs

access_model.value: Project-scoped RBAC: reviewer (annotate, raise RFI), design_lead (approve/reject RFI responses), document_controller (manage transmittals and drawing register), admin (project and user management)
access_model.rationale: Different roles need different write permissions on drawings and RFIs within each project

feature_scope.value: PDF and DWG drawing viewer with concurrent annotation layers, RFI creation and response workflow, comment thread resolution tracking, drawing transmittal management, real-time presence indicators, email notifications for RFI due dates, transmittal analytics dashboard
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set agreed in scope workshop with design and construction stakeholders

mvp_scope.value: PDF drawing viewer with concurrent annotation for up to 10 simultaneous reviewers, RFI raise and response workflow with email notifications, comment thread status tracking, Auth0 SSO
mvp_scope.rationale: PDF-first MVP covers 80% of review volume; DWG rasterisation and transmittal analytics deferred to Phase 2

security_baseline.value: Auth0 JWT on all API and WebSocket connections, TLS 1.3, project-level row-level security in PostgreSQL, drawing files stored in S3 private bucket with presigned URL access, no direct S3 public URLs
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential drawing IP must not be accessible without project membership; presigned URLs time-limited to prevent link sharing

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript, PDF.js for PDF rendering, react-konva for annotation canvas overlay
frontend_stack.rationale: Next.js SSR for fast initial load; PDF.js battle-tested for browser PDF rendering; react-konva for performant canvas annotation layer

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Fiber framework, gorilla/websocket for real-time annotation sync
backend_stack.rationale: Go concurrent goroutine model suits per-drawing WebSocket rooms with low memory overhead; Fiber for REST API performance

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for relational data, Redis 7 for WebSocket session state and pub/sub
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for annotation and RFI persistence; Redis pub/sub for broadcasting annotation events across multiple API instances

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1, S3 for drawing file storage, CloudFront for asset delivery
hosting_target.rationale: Fargate for stateless API containers; S3 private bucket with presigned URLs for drawing access control

privacy_retention_policy.value: Drawing files and RFI records retained for project duration plus 7 years for contractual obligations; deleted projects purged after 30-day grace period
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Standard construction project record-keeping obligations

future_scope.value: DWG to PDF rasterisation pipeline, mobile-optimised review interface, integration with Procore project management, transmittal analytics dashboard
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 and 3 roadmap items post-MVP

constraints.value: Annotation sync latency under 200 ms end-to-end for co-located teams; support up to 10 concurrent reviewers per drawing; drawing files up to 100 MB PDF
constraints.rationale: Usability requirement for real-time collaboration; large structural drawing PDF sizes typical in civil projects

observability_baseline.value: New Relic APM on ECS tasks, structured JSON logs with project_id and drawing_id context, PagerDuty for WebSocket error rate and annotation sync latency alerts
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate monitoring standard; annotation sync latency is the primary user-facing quality metric

execution_preference.value: Agile 2-week sprints, GitHub Actions CI/CD, separate Fargate services for REST API and WebSocket hub
execution_preference.rationale: Separation of REST and WebSocket services enables independent scaling of high-concurrency annotation sessions

compliance_context.value: No regulatory compliance required; contractual confidentiality obligations for engineering IP under project NDA agreements
compliance_context.rationale: Medium risk level and confidential data sensitivity trigger compliance_context; obligations are contractual rather than regulatory

project.goal: Real-time collaborative drawing annotation and RFI management for civil engineering project teams
project.target_users: Design engineers, document controllers, construction site engineers

frontend.rendering: PDF.js for drawing display, react-konva for annotation canvas
frontend.state: Zustand for annotation layer state, React Query for server data

backend.framework: Fiber for REST, gorilla/websocket for WebSocket hub

security.storage: S3 private bucket with presigned URL generation

data.cache: Redis 7 for WebSocket pub/sub and session state

devops.hosting: AWS ECS Fargate (separate REST and WS services)
devops.cdn: CloudFront for Next.js static assets and drawing thumbnails

open_questions.dwg_rasterisation: DWG to PDF conversion pipeline deferred to Phase 2; evaluate Aspose.CAD vs self-hosted LibreCAD

reasoner_reviews.product: PDF-first MVP covering 80% of review volume is the right call. RFI sequential numbering per project is a hard requirement for contractual traceability; the plan must show this in the data model. Comment thread resolution status needs a clear state machine: open, under_review, resolved, closed.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The WebSocket hub needs Redis pub/sub for horizontal scaling across multiple ECS tasks. A single goroutine per drawing room is the right model; rooms should be created lazily on first WebSocket connection and garbage-collected after all clients disconnect. Operational transform for annotation conflicts should use a simplified last-write-wins model for position changes and append-only for new annotations to avoid full OT complexity in the MVP.
reasoner_reviews.security: Auth0 JWT must be validated on the WebSocket upgrade request before the connection is accepted, not only on REST endpoints. S3 presigned URLs must have a maximum 15-minute expiry to prevent link sharing; the client should request a fresh presigned URL for each drawing session open. Project-level row-level security in PostgreSQL prevents cross-project data leakage if a reviewer token is compromised.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 100 MB PDFs will cause significant memory pressure if loaded fully into Go memory for processing. The API should only serve S3 presigned URLs; the client streams PDFs directly from S3 via CloudFront. 10 concurrent WebSocket connections per drawing room with Redis pub/sub fan-out is well within Redis throughput at this scale.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js app router with server components for drawing list and RFI list pages; client components for annotation canvas and real-time presence. Route structure: /projects/[id]/drawings/[drawingId]/review for the primary annotation view.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Project Dashboard: drawing register table with revision history and RFI counts. Drawing Review: PDF.js rendering with react-konva annotation overlay, presence avatars, annotation tool palette (pin, freehand, measurement), WebSocket connection status. RFI Panel: RFI list for current drawing, raise RFI form, response thread view. Transmittal Manager (Phase 2).
frontend.state_management: Zustand store for annotation layers: local annotations, remote annotations from WS events, active tool, selected annotation. React Query for REST: drawing metadata, RFI list, comment threads. WebSocket events dispatched to Zustand on receive.
frontend.frontend_risks: PDF.js renders large PDFs on main thread causing jank; use PDF.js Web Worker mode. react-konva re-renders on every WS annotation event; use React.memo and Zustand selectors to prevent full canvas re-renders.

backend.service_design: Two Go services: api-service (Fiber REST, handles drawings, RFIs, auth, presigned URL generation), ws-service (gorilla/websocket, manages per-drawing annotation rooms, subscribes to Redis pub/sub). Services share PostgreSQL and Redis. No direct database writes from ws-service; annotation events persisted via api-service REST calls.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.1. JWT middleware on all routes. WebSocket upgrade at /ws/drawings/{id} on ws-service. Annotation events: {type: annotation_add/annotation_move/annotation_delete, annotation_id, drawing_id, user_id, payload}. RFI create: POST /rfis with drawing_id and linked annotation_id.
backend.failure_handling: WebSocket room goroutine recovers from panics with defer/recover. Redis pub/sub reconnect with exponential backoff on disconnection. RFI due date escalation via SQS-triggered Lambda rather than in-process goroutine to prevent task loss on Fargate task restart.
backend.background_jobs: SQS + Lambda: rfi_due_date_checker runs hourly, queries PostgreSQL for RFIs with due_date < now() and status != closed, sends SES email to design_lead. drawing_thumbnail_generator triggered on S3 object creation, rasterises PDF page 1 to JPEG thumbnail via pdf2image, stores to S3 thumbnails prefix.

security.auth_design: Auth0 RS256 JWT validated on Fiber middleware for REST and on WebSocket upgrade request in ws-service. Auth0 management API for project-level user role assignment. JWT claims include project_ids[] for RBAC scope check.
security.authorization_model: PostgreSQL row-level security: drawing_sheets, rfis, annotation_layers, comment_threads tables filtered by project_id. Service account role for api-service with SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE on project data tables; ws-service has read-only access.
security.secrets_management: AWS Secrets Manager for PostgreSQL DSN, Redis URL, Auth0 client secret, Twilio credentials. ECS task role with SecretsManager:GetSecretValue for the relevant ARNs only.
security.security_risks: WebSocket connections that bypass JWT validation on upgrade could allow unauthenticated annotation injection. Mitigation: ws-service validates Auth0 JWT on every upgrade request before creating the room connection. S3 presigned URL with >15 min expiry risks sharing confidential drawings; enforce 900-second max TTL in presigned URL generation.

data.entities: drawing_sheets (id, project_id, title, revision, s3_key, thumbnail_s3_key, status ENUM draft/issued/superseded, issued_at, uploaded_by). annotation_layers (id, drawing_sheet_id, created_by, layer_type ENUM comment/markup/measurement, created_at). annotations (id, layer_id, annotation_type ENUM pin/freehand/rectangle/measurement, geometry_json, colour, label, status ENUM open/resolved, created_by, created_at, updated_at). rfis (id, project_id, drawing_sheet_id, rfi_number INT, title, description, raised_by, assigned_to, status ENUM open/under_review/responded/closed, due_date, created_at, closed_at). rfi_responses (id, rfi_id, responded_by, response_text, attachments_json, created_at). comment_threads (id, annotation_id, status ENUM open/resolved, created_at). comment_messages (id, thread_id, author_id, body TEXT, created_at). transmittal_records (id, project_id, sent_by, recipient_emails TEXT[], drawing_ids UUID[], sent_at).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ (db.t3.medium for MVP, upgrade on load). S3 private bucket for drawing PDFs (s3://drawings-project/{project_id}/{drawing_id}/{revision}/filename.pdf) and thumbnails. Redis 7 on ElastiCache for WebSocket room pub/sub and session presence.
data.data_risks: Concurrent annotation writes from 10 reviewers may produce ordering conflicts in annotations table. Mitigation: last-write-wins on position updates; append-only inserts for new annotations prevent duplicate IDs. Large PDF S3 uploads may fail on network drop; use S3 multipart upload for files >10 MB.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster: api-service (2 vCPU, 2 GB, 2-4 tasks), ws-service (1 vCPU, 1 GB, 2-4 tasks). ALB with path-based routing: /api/* -> api-service target group, /ws/* -> ws-service target group with sticky sessions for WS. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ. ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster mode disabled (single node for MVP). S3 + CloudFront for assets. SQS queues for background jobs. Lambda for background processors.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: golangci-lint, go test with race detector, Docker build and push to ECR, ECS rolling deploy with 50% minimum healthy. Next.js: bun run lint, bun run test (Vitest), bun run build, Vercel preview deploy on PR, production deploy on main merge.
devops.observability_stack: New Relic APM agent on Go services via newrelic-go. Structured JSON logs with project_id, drawing_id, user_id, rfi_id fields. CloudWatch Logs from ECS tasks. Custom New Relic dashboards: annotation sync p95 latency, WebSocket room count, active reviewer count, RFI open vs closed rate. PagerDuty alerts: WS error rate >1%, annotation sync p95 > 300 ms, RDS CPU > 80%.
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS rolling deploy with automatic rollback on task health check failure. RDS point-in-time recovery for data issues. Alembic-style Go migration tool with down migrations tested in CI.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a real-time collaborative drawing review platform for civil engineering teams. Key decisions: separate ECS Fargate services for REST API and WebSocket hub to scale them independently; Redis pub/sub for annotation broadcast across ws-service instances; last-write-wins with append-only inserts as a simplified OT approach to avoid full OT complexity in MVP; PDF.js Web Worker mode to prevent main-thread jank on 100 MB civil engineering PDFs. compliance_context is required because data_sensitivity=confidential and risk_level=medium.
target_output.title: Civil Engineering Drawing Review and RFI Collaboration Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A real-time collaborative fullstack application for civil engineering drawing reviews built with Next.js 14 and Go 1.22 on AWS ECS Fargate. Up to 10 concurrent reviewers annotate PDF drawings simultaneously using a react-konva canvas overlay with operational-transform-lite conflict resolution broadcast over gorilla/websocket rooms backed by Redis pub/sub. Formal RFIs are raised against drawing annotations with sequential project-scoped numbering, SES email notifications, and due-date escalation via SQS-triggered Lambda. Drawing files are stored in a private S3 bucket accessed only via 15-minute presigned URLs to protect confidential engineering IP.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two-tier backend on ECS Fargate: api-service (Go Fiber handles REST for drawings, RFIs, annotations, auth, presigned URL generation) and ws-service (Go gorilla/websocket manages per-drawing annotation rooms, fans out annotation events to room subscribers via Redis pub/sub). Both services share PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ and Redis 7 on ElastiCache. ALB routes /api/* to api-service and /ws/* to ws-service with sticky sessions for WebSocket connection affinity. Next.js 14 frontend served via CloudFront renders PDFs with PDF.js in Web Worker mode and overlays annotations with react-konva. Auth0 RS256 JWT validated on every REST request and WebSocket upgrade. S3 private bucket stores drawing PDFs; 15-minute presigned URLs generated by api-service on each drawing session open. Row-level security in PostgreSQL scopes all data access to project membership.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14, TypeScript, PDF.js (Web Worker mode), react-konva, Zustand, React Query. Backend: Go 1.22, Fiber v2, gorilla/websocket, aws-sdk-go-v2, lib/pq, go-redis. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ (db.t3.medium). Cache/Pub-Sub: Redis 7 on ElastiCache. Infra: ECS Fargate, ALB with sticky sessions, S3, CloudFront, SQS, Lambda, SES, ECR, Secrets Manager. Auth: Auth0 RS256 JWT. CI/CD: GitHub Actions. Observability: New Relic APM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Drawing Register module: document controller uploads PDF, system generates thumbnail, drawing_sheet record created with revision and status. Drawing Review module: PDF rendered via PDF.js Web Worker, react-konva annotation layer overlaid, real-time annotation events from other reviewers applied via Zustand dispatch, presence avatars showing active reviewers. RFI Lifecycle module: reviewer raises RFI against a drawing annotation with title and due date, rfi_number auto-incremented per project, design_lead receives SES email, responds via RFI panel, comment_thread tracks resolution. Due-Date Escalation: hourly Lambda polls PostgreSQL for overdue open RFIs, sends SES reminder to assigned design_lead and project admin. Transmittal Manager (Phase 2): batch drawing distributions with recipient tracking.
target_output.system_components: ALB with path-based routing (sticky sessions on /ws/* target group). ECS Fargate api-service (2-4 tasks, 2 vCPU, 2 GB). ECS Fargate ws-service (2-4 tasks, 1 vCPU, 1 GB). RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ (db.t3.medium). ElastiCache Redis 7 (cache.t3.micro for MVP). S3 private bucket for drawing PDFs and thumbnails. CloudFront distribution for Next.js assets and thumbnail delivery. SQS FIFO queue for RFI notification jobs. Lambda function for rfi_due_date_checker and drawing_thumbnail_generator. SES for RFI email notifications. ECR for container images. Secrets Manager for database credentials and Auth0 secrets.
target_output.workflows: Concurrent Annotation Sync: Reviewer A adds a pin annotation on drawing sheet DS-042. react-konva dispatches an annotation_add event over WebSocket to ws-service. ws-service goroutine for room DS-042 serialises the event and publishes it to Redis channel drawing:DS-042. All ws-service instances subscribed to that channel deliver the event to their connected reviewer clients. Reviewer B's react-konva canvas inserts the new annotation into its local Zustand store and re-renders the pin. api-service REST endpoint POST /annotations persists the annotation to PostgreSQL asynchronously from the WebSocket path. Formal RFI Workflow: Reviewer selects an annotation, opens RFI panel, submits POST /rfis with drawing_sheet_id and annotation_id. api-service assigns rfi_number = SELECT MAX(rfi_number)+1 FROM rfis WHERE project_id = ? with advisory lock, inserts RFI record, enqueues SQS message for SES notification. Lambda consumer sends email to assigned design_lead. Design lead responds via POST /rfis/{id}/responses. On final response, design lead closes RFI; status transitions to closed, comment_thread.status set to resolved.
target_output.data_model: drawing_sheets (id UUID PK, project_id FK, title VARCHAR, revision VARCHAR, s3_key TEXT, thumbnail_s3_key TEXT, status ENUM draft/issued/superseded, issued_at TIMESTAMPTZ, uploaded_by FK). annotation_layers (id UUID PK, drawing_sheet_id FK, created_by FK, layer_type ENUM comment/markup/measurement, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). annotations (id UUID PK, layer_id FK, annotation_type ENUM pin/freehand/rectangle/measurement, geometry_json JSONB, colour VARCHAR, label TEXT, status ENUM open/resolved, created_by FK, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). rfis (id UUID PK, project_id FK, drawing_sheet_id FK, rfi_number INT, title VARCHAR, description TEXT, raised_by FK, assigned_to FK, status ENUM open/under_review/responded/closed, due_date DATE, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, closed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). rfi_responses (id UUID PK, rfi_id FK, responded_by FK, response_text TEXT, attachments_json JSONB, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). comment_threads (id UUID PK, annotation_id FK, status ENUM open/resolved, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). comment_messages (id UUID PK, thread_id FK, author_id FK, body TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). transmittal_records (id UUID PK, project_id FK, sent_by FK, recipient_emails TEXT[], drawing_ids UUID[], sent_at TIMESTAMPTZ). project_members (id UUID PK, project_id FK, user_id FK, role ENUM reviewer/design_lead/document_controller/admin).
target_output.api_design: Go Fiber REST API at /api/v1 with Auth0 JWT middleware. GET /drawings?project_id={id} returns drawing register with revision and RFI count. POST /drawings/upload - returns S3 multipart presigned URL for client-side upload; triggers thumbnail Lambda on S3 event. GET /drawings/{id}/session - generates 15-min presigned URL for PDF access. GET /drawings/{id}/annotations - returns all annotations for the drawing. POST /annotations - persists new annotation from WS event. GET /rfis?drawing_id={id}&status=open - returns RFI list. POST /rfis - creates RFI with auto-incremented project-scoped rfi_number. POST /rfis/{id}/responses - adds RFI response. PATCH /rfis/{id}/status - updates RFI status with state machine validation. WebSocket endpoint: ws-service /ws/drawings/{id}?token={jwt} - annotation room connection.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Auth0 RS256 JWT validated via JWKS endpoint on every Fiber middleware chain and on WebSocket upgrade before room admission. PostgreSQL row-level security policies on drawing_sheets, rfis, annotations, comment_threads: SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE restricted to rows where project_id IN (SELECT project_id FROM project_members WHERE user_id = current_setting('app.user_id')). S3 presigned URL TTL hard-capped at 900 seconds in api-service presigned URL generation; client requests fresh URL on drawing session open. TLS 1.3 enforced on ALB and CloudFront. Secrets Manager holds RDS DSN, Redis URL, Auth0 JWKS URL and client secret; ECS task role with read-only access to specific secret ARNs. Drawing files never served via public S3 URL; all access through api-service presigned URL generation. Contractual NDA obligations satisfied by project-scoped RBAC and access logging to CloudWatch Logs.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate cluster in us-east-1a/1b: api-service (min 2, max 4 tasks, CPU 60% scaling target), ws-service (min 2, max 4 tasks, connection-count scaling via custom CloudWatch metric). ALB listener rules: /api/* -> api-service TG (round-robin), /ws/* -> ws-service TG (least outstanding requests with sticky sessions). RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ with automated backups to S3 (7-day retention). ElastiCache Redis 7 single-node for MVP. GitHub Actions CI: golangci-lint, go test -race ./..., Docker build and push to ECR, ECS rolling deploy with 50% minimum healthy and health check grace period 30 s, automatic rollback on failed deployment health check. Next.js pipeline: bun run lint, Vitest unit tests, bun run build, CloudFront cache invalidation on deploy. Database migrations via golang-migrate up run as ECS one-off task pre-deploy.
target_output.observability: New Relic APM agent embedded in both Go services via newrelic-go; distributed traces linking REST annotation persistence to WebSocket broadcast latency. Structured JSON logs from ECS tasks: project_id, drawing_sheet_id, user_id, rfi_id, annotation_id, ws_room_size fields. CloudWatch metric: ws_annotation_sync_latency_ms histogram published from ws-service on each broadcast. New Relic dashboard: annotation sync p95 latency by drawing room, WebSocket active room count, RFI open/close rate by project, RDS connection pool utilisation. PagerDuty alerts: ws p95 latency > 300 ms for 5 min (P2), api-service 5xx rate > 2% (P1), RDS CPU > 80% for 10 min (P2).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated ~$480/month for 50 active projects with 200 daily users. ECS Fargate tasks dominate at ~$200/month for 4 total tasks. RDS db.t3.medium Multi-AZ at ~$120/month. ElastiCache t3.micro at ~$18/month. S3 storage at ~$30/month for 500 GB of drawing files. CloudFront data transfer at ~$40/month. SQS and Lambda negligible at expected RFI volume. Scale trigger: at 500+ concurrent reviewers, Redis cluster mode and additional ws-service tasks.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (14 weeks): Auth0 SSO and project RBAC, PDF drawing upload and viewer with PDF.js Web Worker, react-konva annotation layer with pin and freehand tools, real-time annotation sync via WebSocket and Redis pub/sub, RFI creation and response workflow with sequential numbering, SES email notifications, comment thread resolution tracking, ECS Fargate deployment with GitHub Actions CI. Phase 2 (8 weeks): DWG to PDF rasterisation pipeline (evaluate Aspose.CAD), drawing revision management and supersession, transmittal batch distribution with recipient tracking, mobile-optimised review interface with touch annotation. Phase 3 (6 weeks): Procore project management integration via webhook, drawing analytics dashboard (annotation density heatmaps, RFI resolution time trends), bulk annotation import from PDF comment layers.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go services: golangci-lint with errcheck and staticcheck enabled; go test -race on all packages; 80% test coverage gate for business logic packages. Next.js: TypeScript strict mode; ESLint with react/hooks plugin; Vitest for component and hook unit tests. ADR documents for OT conflict resolution strategy and presigned URL TTL decisions. Database migrations via golang-migrate with both up and down migration files; down migrations tested in staging before production deploy. WebSocket message schema versioned in shared types package imported by both api-service and ws-service. No direct S3 object URLs in any API response; all drawing access via presigned URL generation endpoint.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Redis pub/sub message loss during ElastiCache failover drops annotation events. Mitigation: ws-service clients send periodic annotation state reconciliation requests to api-service REST endpoint every 30 seconds to catch missed events. Risk: rfi_number auto-increment race condition under concurrent RFI creation. Mitigation: PostgreSQL advisory lock keyed by project_id wraps the SELECT MAX + INSERT transaction. Tradeoff: last-write-wins annotation conflict resolution over full OT reduces MVP complexity but means concurrent position edits on the same annotation by two reviewers will silently discard one update; acceptable for MVP given low probability of exact simultaneous edits on the same annotation. Tradeoff: separate ws-service from api-service increases operational complexity but enables independent scaling of annotation WebSocket concurrency separate from REST request throughput.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected react-konva over Fabric.js for annotation canvas: better React integration and TypeScript types. Confirmed last-write-wins OT for MVP; full operational transform deferred to Phase 2 if concurrent edit conflicts prove frequent. Chose SQS + Lambda for RFI due-date escalation over in-process goroutine timer to survive Fargate task restarts. DWG rasterisation deferred to Phase 2 pending Aspose.CAD evaluation.

metadata.notes: Real-time collaborative civil engineering drawing review platform; moderate quality with some thinner sections on cost and analytics.

project_goal.value: Fullstack tender management platform for civil engineering consultancies to manage expressions of interest, prepare bid submissions with weighted evaluation criteria, track competitor intelligence, and run internal go/no-go review workflows before committing bid resources
project_goal.rationale: Replaces disconnected spreadsheets and email chains used for tender tracking and bid preparation

target_users.value: Business development managers, proposal writers, principals, and technical leads at civil engineering consultancies
target_users.rationale: Primary roles identified in stakeholder workshops

project_class.rationale: Requires both a structured frontend for bid preparation and a multi-service backend for workflow orchestration

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, analytics, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: Full bid lifecycle from opportunity registration through submission export and win-rate analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-service backend, weighted scoring engine, PDF export pipeline, and notification integrations

risk_level.rationale: Confidential bid data and competitor intelligence; no financial transactions or health data

data_sensitivity.rationale: Bid submissions, evaluation scores, and competitor profiles are commercially sensitive

external_exposure.rationale: Internal platform; all access requires Okta SSO authentication

access_model.value: RBAC with roles: business_dev, proposal_writer, principal, admin; principals approve go/no-go; admins manage firm settings and user accounts
access_model.rationale: Go/no-go gate requires principal approval before bid resources are committed

feature_scope.value: Tender opportunity register, go/no-go review workflow, bid submission editor with evaluation criteria tracking, competitor intelligence profiles, PDF proposal export, win/loss analytics dashboard, admin panel for firm configuration
feature_scope.rationale: Full bid lifecycle management derived from stakeholder requirements

mvp_scope.value: Tender opportunity register, basic go/no-go review with approval notification, bid section editor with word-count tracking, PDF export, Okta SSO
mvp_scope.rationale: Core workflow that eliminates spreadsheet dependency for Q2 pilot with two offices

security_baseline.value: Okta SSO with MFA, TLS 1.3 in transit, encrypted S3 for document attachments, field-level encryption for competitor intelligence notes
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential bid data requires strong authentication and encryption at rest for sensitive fields

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, TanStack Query
frontend_stack.rationale: SSR for document pages, TypeScript for type-safe evaluation criteria scoring, TanStack Query for server state

backend_stack.value: Node.js 20 with Fastify microservices; tender-service, bid-service, analytics-service, notification-service
backend_stack.rationale: Microservices decomposition allows independent scaling of PDF generation and analytics workloads

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 with Redis 7 for session cache and queue
data_platform.rationale: Relational model suits evaluation criteria hierarchies and bid section relationships

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate with ALB; separate task definitions per microservice
hosting_target.rationale: Containerised microservices with independent scaling and deployment

privacy_retention_policy.value: Tender records retained for 7 years per professional liability requirements; competitor profiles purged 3 years after last update; S3 document lifecycle to Glacier after 2 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Civil engineering firms must retain tender records for professional indemnity purposes

future_scope.value: AI-assisted proposal section drafting, automated tender alerts from procurement portals, mobile review app for principals
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap items identified in discovery

constraints.value: Must integrate with Okta for SSO; PDF export must produce ISO 32000-compliant documents; budget under USD 3000/month cloud spend
constraints.rationale: Okta is the firm-wide identity provider; ISO 32000 required for formal tender submission packages

observability_baseline.value: New Relic APM on each Fargate service, structured JSON logging, PagerDuty on-call for P1 incidents
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate monitoring standard for all internal platforms

execution_preference.value: Agile with 2-week sprints; feature flags for gradual rollout to offices
execution_preference.rationale: Allows early user feedback before firm-wide rollout

compliance_context.value: Professional liability record-keeping requirements for civil engineering firms; GDPR if processing data of EU-based tender clients
compliance_context.rationale: Risk medium with confidential data triggers compliance context requirement

project.goal: Manage full tender lifecycle from opportunity registration to bid submission and post-tender analysis

frontend.state: TanStack Query for server state, Zustand for form state

security.encryption: AES-256 for S3 documents, field-level for competitor notes

data.cache: Redis 7 for sessions and Bull job queues

open_questions.pdf_engine: Puppeteer vs WeasyPrint for PDF generation?

reasoner_reviews.product: The go/no-go review workflow is the critical differentiator: it enforces the firm's bid/no-bid discipline and prevents wasteful proposal effort on low-probability tenders. The evaluation criteria scoring against weighted criteria must be flexible enough to configure per-tender since civil engineering tenders vary from price-only to multi-criteria best-value frameworks. Competitor intelligence profiles are high value but should be MVP-gated behind a feature flag rather than blocking initial rollout.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Fastify microservices on ECS Fargate is a reasonable choice but introduces inter-service coordination overhead. The bid-service and tender-service will share PostgreSQL schemas -- define clear schema ownership boundaries now to avoid cross-service query coupling. The PDF export pipeline will be the most resource-intensive operation: isolate it in a dedicated task or Bull queue worker to prevent it blocking API response latency. Redis Bull queues for async PDF generation and notification dispatch are essential.
reasoner_reviews.security: Competitor intelligence notes are the most sensitive field class: field-level encryption using AWS KMS with per-record data keys prevents bulk exposure even if the database is compromised. Okta OIDC with MFA is appropriate; implement PKCE flow in Next.js. S3 server-side encryption with KMS for proposal document attachments. RBAC must prevent proposal writers from viewing competitor profiles -- that access should be restricted to principals and BD managers.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: USD 3000/month is achievable for a platform of this scale: Fargate tasks for 4 microservices plus RDS PostgreSQL with a single read replica will run approximately USD 1800/month. PDF generation spikes should be handled with on-demand Fargate task scaling rather than always-on workers. Okta integration requires configuring an OIDC application in the firm's Okta tenant before development begins.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The open question on PDF engine needs resolution early: Puppeteer requires Chromium bundled in the container (adds 200 MB to image) and is prone to rendering inconsistencies; Puppeteer is nonetheless preferred over WeasyPrint for CSS-heavy proposal layouts. Decide this in Sprint 1 to avoid rework.

backend.service_design: Four Fastify services: tender-service (opportunity CRUD, go/no-go workflow, status transitions), bid-service (submission editor, evaluation criteria scoring, section completion tracking), analytics-service (win/loss metrics, conversion rates by sector and client), notification-service (email via SES, in-app notifications via SSE). Services share a PostgreSQL cluster with isolated schemas: tender_schema, bid_schema, analytics_schema. Redis Bull queues for async PDF export and notification dispatch.
backend.api_patterns: REST with OpenAPI 3.0 per service behind ALB path-based routing (/v1/tenders, /v1/bids, /v1/analytics). JWT from Okta validated at ALB with JWKS endpoint. Inter-service calls use internal ALB target group with service mesh IAM roles.
backend.background_jobs: PDF export: Bull queue worker in bid-service spawns Puppeteer job, renders proposal HTML template to ISO 32000 PDF, uploads to S3, sends download URL via notification-service. Deadline alerts: daily Bull cron job checks tender_opportunities for submission_deadline within 14 days and dispatches reminder to assigned BD manager.
backend.backend_risks: Cross-service data consistency when a tender_opportunity is archived: bid submissions linked to that opportunity must be transitioned atomically. Puppeteer container image size. PDF generation queue backlog during tender deadline clusters.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 App Router with server components for tender list and bid section views. Client components for evaluation criteria scoring sliders and go/no-go review form. Route groups: (dashboard), (tender), (bid), (admin).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Tender Register: paginated table of tender_opportunities with status badges and deadline indicators. Go/No-Go Review: form with strategic scoring criteria, comments, and Approve/Reject actions sent to principals. Bid Editor: section-by-section editor with word count, completion percentage, and evaluation criteria mapping panel. Proposal Export: trigger PDF generation job, poll status, download link. Analytics Dashboard: win rate by sector, average criteria scores for won vs lost bids.
frontend.state_management: TanStack Query for server state (tender list, bid sections, analytics data). Zustand for local bid editor state (unsaved section drafts, scroll position). Optimistic updates for go/no-go status changes.
frontend.frontend_risks: Bid editor needs autosave with debounce to prevent data loss; section content can be several thousand words. PDF generation polling adds UX complexity -- must show clear progress indicator.

security.auth_design: Okta OIDC with PKCE flow in Next.js middleware. JWT access token validated on each Fastify service via Okta JWKS endpoint. Session managed by NextAuth.js with Redis session store.
security.authorization_model: RBAC: business_dev (create/edit tender_opportunities, view bids), proposal_writer (edit bid sections, cannot see competitor profiles), principal (approve go/no-go, view all data), admin (user management, firm settings). Enforced at API level in each Fastify service via role middleware.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All go/no-go decisions logged to audit_events table with user_id, decision, timestamp, and rationale text. Competitor profile access logged. Structured JSON logs shipped to New Relic with correlation IDs.

data.entities: tender_opportunities (id UUID PK, client_org, project_name, submission_deadline, estimated_value_aud, sector ENUM civil_infrastructure/buildings/transport/water, status ENUM registered/go_nogo_pending/approved/declined/submitted/won/lost, assigned_bd_manager_id, created_at). go_nogo_reviews (id UUID PK, opportunity_id FK, reviewer_id FK, decision ENUM approved/declined, strategic_score INT 1-5, resource_score INT 1-5, rationale TEXT, reviewed_at). bid_submissions (id UUID PK, opportunity_id FK, evaluation_criteria_json JSONB, completion_pct REAL, pdf_s3_key TEXT, status ENUM draft/in_review/submitted, submitted_at). bid_sections (id UUID PK, submission_id FK, section_title, content TEXT, word_count INT, criteria_mapping JSONB). competitor_profiles (id UUID PK, org_name, known_strengths TEXT ENCRYPTED, typical_price_posture ENUM aggressive/mid/premium, sectors TEXT[], last_updated_by_id, updated_at). consultant_registers (id UUID PK, org_name, discipline, approval_status ENUM approved/under_review/expired, expiry_date).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 primary with read replica for analytics queries. S3 with server-side KMS encryption for proposal PDFs and attached documents. Redis for Bull job queues and session cache.
data.retention_and_deletion: tender_opportunities and bid_submissions retained 7 years. competitor_profiles deleted 3 years after last_updated_at. S3 lifecycle rule moves PDFs to Glacier after 2 years.

devops.deployment_topology: Four ECS Fargate task definitions (tender-service, bid-service, analytics-service, notification-service) + one Fargate worker task for PDF Bull queue. ALB with path-based routing. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ. ElastiCache Redis cluster mode.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint, jest unit tests (80% coverage gate), Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy. Separate workflows per service; shared Docker layer caching. Staging environment auto-deploys on main merge; production deploy requires manual approval.
devops.environments: dev (single Fargate tasks, RDS Single-AZ), staging (mirrors production topology), production (Multi-AZ, auto-scaling 2-8 tasks per service on CPU 60% target).
devops.devops_risks: Puppeteer Docker image bloat; mitigate with multi-stage build and separate PDF worker image. Database migration coordination across four services during deployments.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a fullstack tender management platform for civil engineering consultancies. Key decisions: Fastify microservices with clear schema ownership boundaries per service to prevent cross-service coupling; Puppeteer Bull queue worker for async PDF generation to avoid blocking API latency; field-level KMS encryption for competitor intelligence notes; Okta OIDC with PKCE for MFA-enforced SSO. The moderate plan quality means the deployment_and_operations section is slightly less detailed on migration coordination between microservices.
target_output.title: Civil Engineering Tender Management Platform Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A fullstack tender management platform for civil engineering consultancies built with Next.js 14 and four Fastify microservices on AWS ECS Fargate with PostgreSQL 16. Manages the full bid lifecycle from tender opportunity registration through go/no-go review workflows, proposal section editing with evaluation criteria tracking, competitor intelligence, and PDF proposal export. Okta SSO with MFA secures all access; field-level KMS encryption protects commercially sensitive competitor profiles. Analytics service provides win-rate and bid conversion metrics by sector.
target_output.architecture_overview: Next.js 14 SSR frontend communicates via REST to four Fastify microservices behind an AWS ALB with path-based routing: tender-service (/v1/tenders), bid-service (/v1/bids), analytics-service (/v1/analytics), and notification-service (/v1/notify). Services share a PostgreSQL 16 cluster with isolated schemas and a Redis 7 cluster for Bull job queues and session cache. A dedicated Fargate worker task processes async PDF export jobs from the bid-service Bull queue using Puppeteer. Okta OIDC with PKCE authenticates users; JWT access tokens are validated via Okta JWKS on each service. S3 with KMS encryption stores proposal PDFs and attached documents. New Relic APM instruments each service.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, TanStack Query, Zustand, NextAuth.js for Okta OIDC session. Backend: Node.js 20 with Fastify 4 (tender-service, bid-service, analytics-service, notification-service), Bull 4 for job queues, Puppeteer 22 for PDF generation, AWS SDK v3 for S3 and KMS. Database: PostgreSQL 16 (RDS Multi-AZ in production), Redis 7 (ElastiCache cluster mode). Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB with WAF, S3, KMS, SES for email, ECR, GitHub Actions CI/CD, New Relic APM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Tender Opportunity Register: CRUD for tender_opportunities with sector classification, estimated value, submission deadline, and assigned BD manager. Go/No-Go Review Workflow: principal-gated approval with strategic and resource scoring criteria; transitions opportunity from go_nogo_pending to approved or declined. Bid Submission Editor: section-by-section rich-text editor with word-count display, completion percentage bar, and evaluation criteria mapping panel showing which sections address each client criterion. Competitor Intelligence: BD manager-maintained profiles for known competitors with encrypted notes on pricing posture and past performance. PDF Proposal Export: async Puppeteer job renders proposal HTML template to ISO 32000 PDF uploaded to S3; download link delivered via notification. Analytics Dashboard: win rate by sector and client type, average go/no-go approval rate, time-to-submission metrics, criteria score distribution for won vs lost bids.
target_output.system_components: Next.js 14 frontend (SSR + client components, App Router), AWS ALB with WAF and path-based routing, Tender-service Fargate task (opportunity lifecycle, go/no-go workflow), Bid-service Fargate task (section editor API, evaluation criteria scoring, PDF queue producer), Analytics-service Fargate task (aggregation queries against analytics_schema), Notification-service Fargate task (SES email dispatch, SSE for in-app notifications), PDF Worker Fargate task (Bull queue consumer, Puppeteer PDF renderer), PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ (four isolated schemas), Redis 7 ElastiCache (Bull queues + session cache), S3 + KMS (proposal PDFs, document attachments), Okta OIDC tenant, New Relic APM.
target_output.workflows: Go/No-Go Review: BD manager registers a tender_opportunity with deadline, estimated value, and sector; system sets status to go_nogo_pending and dispatches notification to assigned principals via notification-service. Principals open the review form, score the opportunity on strategic fit (1-5) and resource availability (1-5), enter rationale, and submit Approve or Decline; tender-service transitions status and notifies the BD manager. Bid Preparation and Submission: Once approved, bid-service creates a bid_submission with sections derived from the firm's section template library; proposal writers fill each bid_section with structured content, tracking word count against the client's page limit and mapping each section to evaluation_criteria entries. When all sections reach 100% completion, the BD manager triggers PDF export; bid-service enqueues a Puppeteer job, the worker renders the compiled proposal to ISO 32000 PDF, uploads to S3, and notification-service delivers a download link to the submitter's inbox.
target_output.data_model: tender_opportunities (id UUID PK, client_org TEXT, project_name TEXT, submission_deadline TIMESTAMPTZ, estimated_value_aud NUMERIC, sector ENUM civil_infrastructure/buildings/transport/water, status ENUM registered/go_nogo_pending/approved/declined/submitted/won/lost, assigned_bd_manager_id UUID FK, created_at). go_nogo_reviews (id UUID PK, opportunity_id UUID FK, reviewer_id UUID FK, decision ENUM approved/declined, strategic_score INT, resource_score INT, rationale TEXT, reviewed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). bid_submissions (id UUID PK, opportunity_id UUID FK, evaluation_criteria_json JSONB, completion_pct REAL, pdf_s3_key TEXT, status ENUM draft/in_review/submitted, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ). bid_sections (id UUID PK, submission_id UUID FK, section_title TEXT, content TEXT, word_count INT, criteria_mapping JSONB). competitor_profiles (id UUID PK, org_name TEXT, known_strengths_encrypted BYTEA, typical_price_posture ENUM aggressive/mid/premium, sectors TEXT[], last_updated_by_id UUID, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). consultant_registers (id UUID PK, org_name TEXT, discipline TEXT, approval_status ENUM approved/under_review/expired, expiry_date DATE). bid_resources (id UUID PK, submission_id UUID FK, staff_member_id UUID FK, role TEXT, estimated_hours NUMERIC, confirmed BOOLEAN).
target_output.api_design: Tender-service REST (/v1/tenders): POST /opportunities (register), GET /opportunities?status=approved&sector=transport (filter list), PATCH /opportunities/:id/go-nogo (submit review decision), GET /opportunities/:id (detail with go_nogo history). Bid-service (/v1/bids): POST /submissions (create from approved opportunity), GET /submissions/:id/sections (section list with completion), PUT /submissions/:id/sections/:sectionId (update section content), POST /submissions/:id/export-pdf (enqueue Puppeteer job), GET /submissions/:id/export-status (poll job status). Analytics-service (/v1/analytics): GET /win-rate?sector=&period=quarterly, GET /criteria-scores?outcome=won. All endpoints require Okta JWT; competitor profile endpoints restricted to principal and business_dev roles.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Okta OIDC with PKCE in Next.js; JWT access tokens validated on each Fastify service via Okta JWKS endpoint with 5-minute clock skew tolerance. RBAC enforced in Fastify route middleware: proposal_writer role cannot access competitor_profiles or go_nogo_review endpoints. Field-level KMS encryption for competitor_profiles.known_strengths using per-record data keys wrapped with AWS KMS CMK -- plaintext only materialised in application memory during authorised access. S3 server-side encryption with KMS for proposal PDFs. WAF rules on ALB blocking OWASP Top 10 patterns. All go/no-go decisions and competitor profile accesses logged to audit_events table with user_id, action, resource_id, and timestamp. GDPR basis: legitimate interest for processing tender client data; data subject requests handled via admin panel purge workflow.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI per microservice: ESLint, Jest unit tests with 80% coverage gate, Docker multi-stage build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy with health check gate. Separate workflows per service allow independent deploys; shared ECR base image layer cached in GitHub Actions. Environments: dev (single Fargate tasks, RDS Single-AZ, auto-deploy on feature branch merge), staging (Multi-AZ mirrors production, auto-deploy on main merge), production (Multi-AZ, ECS auto-scaling 2-8 tasks per service on CPU 60% target, manual approval gate). Database migrations run as ECS one-off tasks per service before rolling deploy. Secrets stored in AWS Secrets Manager with 30-day rotation for DB credentials.
target_output.observability: New Relic APM agent on each Fargate service; custom spans for go/no-go state transitions, PDF export queue depth, and evaluation criteria scoring operations. Structured JSON logs with correlation IDs shipped to New Relic Logs. Custom metrics tracked: go/no-go approval rate per week, PDF export queue depth and job duration p95, bid section completion rate by deadline proximity. PagerDuty alerts for: bid-service error rate above 1%, PDF worker queue depth above 20 jobs, RDS CPU above 80%, and submission deadline breach for any opportunity in approved status.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated USD 2200/month at 50 active tenders: RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ (db.t3.medium) USD 180/month, ElastiCache Redis (cache.t3.micro) USD 30/month, ECS Fargate 5 services at average 0.25 vCPU USD 120/month, ALB USD 30/month, S3 + KMS USD 20/month, New Relic Pro USD 600/month, SES USD 10/month, misc USD 210. Under USD 3000/month budget. Scaling: ECS auto-scaling on CPU 60% target handles proposal deadline clusters; PDF worker scales independently via Bull queue depth CloudWatch metric.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): tender opportunity register, go/no-go review workflow with Okta OIDC, bid section editor with word-count tracking, basic PDF export via Puppeteer, SES email notifications, admin panel for user and role management, New Relic APM setup. Phase 2 (6 weeks): evaluation criteria scoring with weighted completion percentage, competitor intelligence profiles with field-level KMS encryption, proposal section template library, analytics dashboard with win rate and conversion metrics. Phase 3 (4 weeks): bid resource allocation tracking, bid_resources forecasting, GDPR subject access and purge workflows, advanced analytics with criteria score benchmarking for won vs lost bids.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode across all services and Next.js frontend. Each Fastify service owns its own PostgreSQL schema -- no cross-schema joins allowed; analytics-service uses read-only replica user scoped to analytics_schema. Jest unit tests for all scoring logic, go/no-go state machine transitions, and criteria completion calculations. Cypress E2E covers go/no-go approval flow and PDF export end-to-end. OpenAPI 3.0 spec generated from Fastify type providers -- client types generated via openapi-typescript. Feature flags via LaunchDarkly for gradual rollout of competitor intelligence module.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Cross-service data consistency when a tender_opportunity is archived mid-bid -- bid_submissions linked to that opportunity must be transitioned atomically. Mitigation: outbox pattern in tender-service publishes opportunity_archived event consumed by bid-service to freeze linked submissions. Risk: Puppeteer container image size (approximately 200 MB with Chromium) increases cold start time for PDF worker tasks. Mitigation: warm minimum-1 Fargate task for PDF worker during business hours. Tradeoff: separate Fastify microservices vs a monolithic Fastify API -- microservices chosen for independent PDF worker scaling; service boundaries aligned to bounded contexts to prevent tight coupling. Risk: Okta JWKS caching -- stale key cache causes auth failures after key rotation; set JWKS cache TTL to 30 minutes with fallback re-fetch on 401.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: PDF engine: Puppeteer chosen over WeasyPrint for CSS-layout fidelity in proposal templates. PostgreSQL schema per service chosen over separate databases to reduce infrastructure cost. Okta OIDC with PKCE selected over SAML for Next.js compatibility. Redis Bull chosen over SQS for job queuing to avoid Lambda cold starts.

metadata.notes: Moderate: deployment section slightly thin on inter-service migration coordination detail; all other sections are domain-specific and well-specified.

project_goal.value: Environmental monitoring data pipeline for an urban tunnel project collecting ground settlement, vibration, groundwater, and air quality readings from automated sensor arrays, validating against regulatory trigger levels, and generating automated daily compliance reports for environmental regulators
project_goal.rationale: Statutory condition of approval requires real-time trigger monitoring and daily regulator reporting

target_users.value: Environmental managers, site engineers, state environment protection authority regulators, and project auditors
target_users.rationale: Internal staff for monitoring and regulators for compliance verification

project_class.rationale: Automated sensor-to-report pipeline with no interactive user frontend

capabilities.rationale: 15-minute sensor ingest flows, daily report batch jobs, sensor API integrations, compliance analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-parameter sensor validation, regulatory trigger logic, automated PDF report generation, and partner-facing report delivery

risk_level.rationale: Missed trigger exceedance notifications could result in regulatory breach and project shutdown

data_sensitivity.rationale: Environmental monitoring data is commercially sensitive and subject to regulatory confidentiality obligations

external_exposure.rationale: Regulatory authority receives automated report emails and has read access to the compliance portal

access_model.value: Azure AD for internal staff, regulator-specific service accounts for automated report delivery via SMTP and SFTP
access_model.rationale: Regulator cannot use internal Azure AD; service accounts used for automated delivery

feature_scope.value: 15-minute sensor data ingestion, trigger level validation with immediate SMS and email alerts, daily compliance PDF report generation, automated regulator email delivery, exceedance event tracking and corrective action logging, monthly regulatory submission packages
feature_scope.rationale: Full scope of conditions of approval compliance obligations

mvp_scope.value: 15-minute sensor ingest, trigger validation with SMS alert on exceedance, daily PDF compliance report emailed to regulator
mvp_scope.rationale: Core regulatory obligations must be met before tunnelling commences

security_baseline.value: Azure AD authentication for internal access, TLS 1.3 for all sensor API connections, encrypted SFTP for regulator file delivery, no sensor data exposed publicly
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential environmental data and partner-facing regulatory delivery require strong transport security

frontend_stack.value: none - pipeline only; compliance dashboard is a Grafana instance reading from TimescaleDB
frontend_stack.rationale: Grafana provides adequate monitoring UI without a custom frontend

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Prefect 3
backend_stack.rationale: Prefect chosen for observable flow orchestration with native Azure Container Apps deployment

data_platform.value: TimescaleDB on PostgreSQL 16 for time-series sensor data
data_platform.rationale: Hypertable partitioning and continuous aggregates handle high-frequency sensor ingest efficiently

hosting_target.value: Azure Container Apps in australiaeast
hosting_target.rationale: Serverless containers with Prefect worker scaling; Australian data sovereignty for project data

privacy_retention_policy.value: Sensor readings retained 10 years per project conditions of approval; exceedance records and compliance reports retained permanently as legal records
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Regulatory requirement for environmental monitoring records

future_scope.value: Real-time Grafana alerting dashboard for site team, machine learning anomaly detection for sensor drift, automated monthly regulatory submission packaging
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 enhancements after MVP compliance system is live

constraints.value: Sensor API polling must complete within 10 minutes of each 15-minute window; report emails must be sent by 07:00 AEST each morning; system must remain operational during site emergencies
constraints.rationale: Regulatory conditions specify report delivery times; safety system availability is contractual

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards on TimescaleDB for sensor health and exceedance history; Prometheus metrics from Prefect; PagerDuty for pipeline failure and missed report delivery alerts
observability_baseline.rationale: Pipeline failure visibility is critical given regulatory obligations

execution_preference.value: Continuous delivery with feature flags for enabling new sensor types
execution_preference.rationale: New sensor arrays added progressively as tunnelling advances

compliance_context.value: State Environment Protection Authority conditions of approval; ISO 14001 environmental management system; potential Ombudsman investigation if monitoring failures are not documented and reported
compliance_context.rationale: High regulatory risk requires full audit trail and documented response to exceedances

project.goal: Automated environmental monitoring pipeline meeting regulatory conditions of approval for urban tunnel project
project.target_users: Environmental managers, site engineers, EPA regulators

backend.integrations: Sensor manufacturer REST APIs (vibration, settlement, groundwater, air quality)

security.auth: Azure AD for internal staff, service accounts for regulator delivery
security.transport: TLS 1.3 for sensor APIs; SFTP with key auth for regulator file delivery
security.secrets: Azure Key Vault for sensor API credentials and SMTP passwords

reasoner_reviews.product: The regulatory delivery deadline of 07:00 AEST is non-negotiable - a missed daily report constitutes a conditions of approval breach. The pipeline must have a documented manual override procedure so the environmental manager can send a corrected report if the automated job fails. The trigger level logic must be version-controlled and auditable as regulators may request evidence of the exact thresholds applied on any given day.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: TimescaleDB hypertables with automatic partitioning by sensor_reading time are the right choice for this workload. Prefect 3 Flows on Container Apps scale well for the 15-minute polling cadence. Recommend storing trigger level thresholds in the database with effective_from/effective_to dates so threshold changes are auditable, rather than hardcoding in Prefect flows.
reasoner_reviews.security: The regulator-facing report delivery must use authenticated SFTP with per-authority SSH keys rather than unauthenticated email attachments where possible. Azure Key Vault integration with managed identity is the right approach for sensor API credentials. Ensure sensor API connections use certificate pinning if the manufacturer supports it.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 10-minute polling window for 15-minute cadence leaves only 5 minutes of slack. If the pipeline processes more than 50 sensor arrays, parallel Prefect tasks per sensor type with a timeout of 8 minutes per task group are necessary to meet the window consistently.

backend.service_design: Prefect flows: sensor_ingest_flow (15-min scheduled, polls all sensor APIs in parallel tasks), trigger_validation_flow (triggered per ingest completion, evaluates readings against trigger_levels table), daily_report_flow (scheduled 05:30 AEST, generates PDF, sends via SMTP/SFTP), exceedance_response_flow (triggered on exceedance event, sends SMS and email alerts)
backend.background_jobs: 15-minute ingest flow, daily 05:30 AEST report flow, weekly sensor health check flow validating that all arrays have reported within 2x polling interval
backend.failure_handling: Prefect flow retries: 3 attempts with 2-minute delay for sensor API calls; dead letter concept via failed_ingest_log table; PagerDuty alert if report flow fails; manual report resend script documented for environmental manager

security.auth_design: Azure AD managed identity for Container Apps accessing Key Vault; no secrets in container image or environment variables; regulator service accounts are Azure AD external collaboration accounts
security.secrets_management: Azure Key Vault stores sensor API bearer tokens, SMTP password, SFTP private key, and TimescaleDB connection string; Prefect workers fetch secrets at runtime via DefaultAzureCredential
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All exceedance events logged with sensor_id, reading_value, trigger_level, triggered_at, acknowledged_by, acknowledgement_note; report delivery logged with timestamp, recipient, delivery_method, and success flag; immutable append-only audit table

data.entities: monitoring_sensors (id, sensor_type ENUM settlement/vibration/groundwater/air_quality, location_easting, location_northing, array_id, active_from, active_to). sensor_readings (time TIMESTAMPTZ, sensor_id FK, raw_value FLOAT, unit, quality_flag BOOLEAN) as TimescaleDB hypertable partitioned by 1-week chunks. trigger_levels (id, sensor_id, parameter, warning_level, action_level, effective_from, effective_to). exceedance_events (id, sensor_id, reading_time, trigger_type ENUM warning/action, reading_value, trigger_value, notified_at, corrective_action_id). compliance_reports (id, report_date, generated_at, delivered_at, delivery_status, pdf_s3_key). environmental_zones (id, zone_name, sensitive_receptors_json, applicable_sensor_ids).
data.storage_design: TimescaleDB hypertables with 1-week chunks for sensor_readings; continuous aggregate materialising hourly min/max/avg per sensor for reporting; Azure Blob Storage for generated PDF reports with 10-year retention policy
data.retention_and_deletion: sensor_readings retained indefinitely (regulatory requirement); continuous aggregates refreshed hourly; raw API response cache in memory only, not persisted

devops.deployment_topology: Prefect server on Azure Container Apps (min 1 replica always-on), Prefect worker pool on Container Apps (scale 0-5 replicas based on Prefect work queue depth), TimescaleDB on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server in australiaeast
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps Pipeline: pytest with testcontainers for TimescaleDB, Prefect flow registration to Prefect Cloud, docker build, push Azure Container Registry, rolling deploy to Container Apps
devops.observability_stack: Grafana connected to TimescaleDB for real-time sensor dashboard; Prometheus metrics from Prefect workers scraped by Azure Monitor; PagerDuty integration via Prefect automation hooks

target_output.thinking_summary: Environmental monitoring pipeline with hard regulatory delivery constraints. Key decisions: TimescaleDB hypertables for high-frequency sensor ingest, version-controlled trigger levels in DB for audit reproducibility, Prefect 3 parallel tasks per sensor type to meet 10-minute polling window, Azure Key Vault managed identity for zero-secret-in-code posture. Moderate quality plan with good overall structure but observability and cost sections slightly thinner.
target_output.title: Urban Tunnel Environmental Compliance Monitoring Pipeline
target_output.executive_summary: A Python-Prefect pipeline on Azure Container Apps that polls four categories of environmental sensor arrays every 15 minutes, validates readings against version-controlled regulatory trigger levels, sends immediate SMS and email alerts on exceedances, and generates automated daily compliance PDF reports emailed to the state environment protection authority by 07:00 AEST. TimescaleDB stores sensor time-series with a 10-year retention policy, and Azure Blob stores all generated compliance reports as permanent legal records.
target_output.architecture_overview: Prefect 3 server and worker pool deployed on Azure Container Apps in australiaeast. Four sensor types (ground settlement, vibration, groundwater levels, air quality) are polled via manufacturer REST APIs every 15 minutes by parallel Prefect tasks within sensor_ingest_flow. Validated readings are written to a TimescaleDB hypertable. A Prefect trigger_validation_flow evaluates each batch of readings against trigger_levels records with effective date ranges, creates exceedance_events rows, and fires SMS and email alerts via Azure Communication Services. Daily report flow at 05:30 AEST aggregates the prior 24 hours of readings and exceedances, renders a PDF using a ReportLab template matching the regulatory prescribed format, and delivers it via SMTP and SFTP to the EPA authority contacts. Azure Key Vault with managed identity provides all sensor API credentials and delivery secrets. Grafana reads directly from TimescaleDB continuous aggregates for the internal site monitoring dashboard.
target_output.technology_stack: Orchestration: Prefect 3.x, Python 3.12. Sensor integration: httpx async clients with per-sensor-type connector classes. Time-series DB: TimescaleDB (PostgreSQL 16 extension) on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server. PDF generation: ReportLab 4 with jinja2 for template rendering. Alert delivery: Azure Communication Services for SMS, smtplib for email. Report delivery: paramiko SFTP client. Secrets: Azure Key Vault with DefaultAzureCredential. CI/CD: Azure DevOps Pipelines. Observability: Grafana, Prometheus, PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Sensor Ingest: 15-minute Prefect flow polls settlement, vibration, groundwater, and air quality sensor APIs concurrently via asyncio task groups. Trigger Validation: post-ingest flow evaluates each sensor reading against warning and action threshold records in trigger_levels, creates exceedance_event rows, dispatches SMS to environmental manager and email to site team within 2 minutes of reading timestamp. Daily Compliance Report: 05:30 AEST flow queries 24-hour aggregates, formats into regulator-prescribed PDF layout, attaches exceedance summary table, emails to EPA contacts and deposits to authority SFTP server. Corrective Action Logging: environmental manager records corrective action against each exceedance event via CLI script updating corrective_action records. Sensor Health: weekly flow checks all arrays for gaps exceeding 2x polling interval, alerts on offline sensors.
target_output.system_components: Azure Container Apps hosting Prefect server (always-on) and Prefect worker pool (scales 0-5 on queue depth). Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server running TimescaleDB extension in australiaeast. Azure Blob Storage container for generated PDF compliance reports (10-year immutable retention). Azure Key Vault for sensor credentials, SMTP, and SFTP keys. Azure Container Registry for worker images. Azure Communication Services for SMS alert delivery. Grafana instance on Container Apps reading TimescaleDB. Prometheus scraping Prefect worker metrics.
target_output.workflows: 15-Minute Sensor Ingest: Prefect scheduled flow starts at :00, :15, :30, :45 each hour; sensor_ingest_flow spawns parallel Prefect tasks grouped by sensor_type (settlement, vibration, groundwater, air_quality); each task calls manufacturer REST API with retry=3 and 30s timeout; responses parsed to sensor_readings rows and bulk-inserted into TimescaleDB hypertable; task group completes within 8 minutes target; trigger_validation_flow queued on completion. Trigger Validation: flow queries sensor_readings for the just-ingested batch, joins with trigger_levels WHERE now() BETWEEN effective_from AND effective_to; readings exceeding action_level create exceedance_events rows and dispatch immediate Azure Communication Services SMS to environmental manager and email to site team; readings exceeding warning_level create events with notification_type=warning only. Daily Compliance Report: flow at 05:30 AEST queries TimescaleDB continuous aggregate for prior 24-hour min/max/avg per sensor; fetches all exceedance_events from that day; renders ReportLab PDF from regulatory template with readings table, exceedance summary, and corrective action status; emails PDF to EPA contacts and deposits via SFTP paramiko client; updates compliance_reports.delivery_status; PagerDuty alert if delivery fails.
target_output.data_model: monitoring_sensors (id UUID PK, sensor_type VARCHAR(20), location_easting FLOAT, location_northing FLOAT, array_id VARCHAR(20), installation_date DATE, active_from DATE, active_to DATE). sensor_readings (time TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL, sensor_id UUID FK, raw_value FLOAT, unit VARCHAR(10), quality_flag BOOLEAN, source_api_response_id VARCHAR(50)) - TimescaleDB hypertable partitioned by 1-week time intervals. trigger_levels (id UUID PK, sensor_id UUID FK, parameter VARCHAR(30), warning_level FLOAT, action_level FLOAT, unit VARCHAR(10), effective_from DATE, effective_to DATE). exceedance_events (id UUID PK, sensor_id FK, reading_time TIMESTAMPTZ, trigger_type VARCHAR(10), reading_value FLOAT, trigger_value FLOAT, notified_at TIMESTAMPTZ, corrective_action_notes TEXT). compliance_reports (id UUID PK, report_date DATE, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, delivered_at TIMESTAMPTZ, delivery_method VARCHAR(20), delivery_status VARCHAR(20), pdf_blob_url TEXT). environmental_zones (id UUID PK, zone_name VARCHAR(100), sensitive_receptors JSON, applicable_sensor_ids UUID[]).
target_output.api_design: No external-facing API. Prefect server REST API (internal Container Apps VNet only): GET /api/flow-runs for monitoring, POST /api/deployments/{id}/create_flow_run for manual trigger of report resend or backfill ingest. Regulator delivery is push-only via SMTP and SFTP. Internal CLI: python -m compliance_tool resend-report --date 2026-03-14 for manual report resend by environmental manager. Sensor manufacturer APIs are third-party REST endpoints polled by the pipeline; documented per-sensor-type in sensor_connectors package.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Azure AD managed identity on Container Apps workers; no secrets in container image or environment variables. Azure Key Vault references: sensor_api_bearer_{sensor_type}, smtp_password, sftp_private_key_pem, timescaledb_connection_string. All sensor API connections over TLS 1.3; certificate validation enforced, no insecure fallback. SFTP report delivery uses ED25519 key pair per authority; private key in Key Vault. Trigger level records are append-only with effective date ranges; no UPDATE permitted on historical threshold records to preserve audit reproducibility. Compliance reports stored in Azure Blob with Write-Once Read-Many immutable retention policy for 10 years. Internal access via Azure AD only; no public endpoints. ISO 14001 compliance: all exceedance events have mandatory corrective action fields; corrective actions logged before event can be marked resolved.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Azure DevOps Pipeline: pytest with testcontainers-go equivalent (testcontainers-python) for TimescaleDB integration tests, Prefect flow deployment to staging Prefect workspace on PR merge, manual promotion to production workspace with sign-off from environmental manager. Container Apps: Prefect server min 1 max 1 replica (always-on); worker pool min 0 max 5 replicas on work queue depth. Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server zone-redundant HA in australiaeast. TimescaleDB compression policy: compress sensor_readings chunks older than 7 days. Azure Blob immutable storage policy on compliance-reports container. PagerDuty integration on Prefect automation hooks for flow failure notifications at all times.
target_output.observability: Grafana dashboards on TimescaleDB: real-time sensor readings heat-map by array, exceedance count by day, sensor offline status. Prometheus metrics from Prefect workers: flow_run_duration_seconds, sensor_api_error_total per sensor_type. PagerDuty escalation for: daily_report_flow failure, any sensor type with zero ingested readings in a 45-minute window, SFTP delivery failure.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $800-1,100/month: Azure Container Apps worker pool scales to zero outside polling windows (~$200), Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server GP_Standard_D2ds_v4 (~$400), Azure Blob for PDF reports (~$10), Azure Communication Services SMS (~$50 at 5 alerts/day). Prefect Cloud team plan (~$200). Scale path: hypertable chunk compression reduces storage growth; worker pool scales within 30 seconds for burst ingest during sensor commissioning periods.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): TimescaleDB schema, sensor connector classes for all four sensor types, 15-minute ingest flow, trigger validation with SMS and email alerts, daily compliance PDF report generation and SMTP delivery, Azure Container Apps deployment, Azure Key Vault integration. Phase 2 (4 weeks): SFTP delivery to regulator portal, corrective action CLI, Grafana dashboard, sensor health weekly check flow, Prometheus and PagerDuty integration. Phase 3 Future: automated monthly regulatory submission packaging, ML anomaly detection for sensor drift, real-time alert threshold suggestions.
target_output.development_guidelines: Per-sensor-type connector classes implementing a SensorConnector protocol with poll() and parse() methods; adding a new sensor type requires only a new connector class and trigger_levels records. All trigger level changes made via database migration with effective_from date set to future date; never UPDATE existing trigger_level rows. Prefect flow code version-pinned to Git SHA in deployment YAML. ReportLab templates version-controlled; PDF layout changes require sign-off from environmental manager before deployment.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Sensor API downtime during a 15-minute ingest window produces a gap in compliance record.
