project_goal.value: Research prototype applying graph neural networks to detect constructability issues in IFC 4 BIM models by representing building components and spatial relationships as a graph and training a GNN to classify nodes that correlate with site installation delays
project_goal.rationale: University research partnership to validate GNN-based constructability prediction on real project BIM data

target_users.value: University civil engineering researchers, design engineers, and BIM coordinators on construction projects
target_users.rationale: Dual academic and industry user base for prototype validation

project_class.rationale: Experimental ML system with Streamlit UI and GNN training pipeline, not production SaaS

capabilities.rationale: GNN training and inference is the AI core; Streamlit for research UI; graph DB for model storage

complexity_level.rationale: IFC parsing, graph construction, GNN training on GPU, and inference pipeline with subgraph explainability

risk_level.rationale: Internal research system with proprietary BIM model data; no external-facing API

data_sensitivity.rationale: BIM models contain proprietary building designs and structural calculations

external_exposure.rationale: Accessible only within university VPN and contractor internal network

access_model.value: Custom JWT with researcher and engineer roles; Streamlit protected by JWT middleware
access_model.rationale: Simple role gate sufficient for internal research prototype

feature_scope.value: IFC model upload and graph extraction to Neo4j, GNN model training with PyG on GPU nodes, inference on new IFC models, constructability risk ranking of flagged nodes, subgraph visualisation, training run dashboard
feature_scope.rationale: Full research prototype lifecycle from data ingestion to explainable inference

mvp_scope.value: IFC upload, Neo4j graph extraction, GNN training on labelled dataset, inference with ranked node risk list, basic Streamlit dashboard
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum to validate GNN accuracy on pilot BIM dataset

security_baseline.value: Custom JWT for internal access; TLS 1.3 for all internal API calls; BIM files stored in GCP Cloud Storage private bucket
security_baseline.rationale: Internal system with proprietary model data

frontend_stack.value: Streamlit (Python) for research dashboard and inference UI
frontend_stack.rationale: Rapid prototyping; researchers prefer Python-native UI

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI for IFC processing and inference API; PyTorch Geometric for GNN training
backend_stack.rationale: PyG is the leading GNN framework; FastAPI for async IFC preprocessing

data_platform.value: Neo4j 5 for graph storage of BIM component nodes and spatial edges; GCP Cloud Storage for raw IFC files
data_platform.rationale: Neo4j native graph traversal for subgraph explainability queries

hosting_target.value: GKE with GPU node pools (NVIDIA T4) for training; Cloud Run for FastAPI inference API
hosting_target.rationale: GPU node pools scale to zero between training runs to control cost

privacy_retention_policy.value: BIM models retained for project duration plus 3 years; training datasets version-controlled in GCP Cloud Storage
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Research data retention matching university research data policy

future_scope.value: Multi-project transfer learning, integration with Autodesk BIM 360 for live model ingestion, real-time constructability feedback during design
future_scope.rationale: Post-prototype research roadmap

constraints.value: GCP cloud only; GPU training budget capped at $500/month; IFC 4 format only at MVP
constraints.rationale: University GCP research credits and contractual GCP commitment

observability_baseline.value: Grafana and Prometheus for GKE cluster metrics; MLflow for GNN training run tracking (loss, accuracy, F1 per constructability category)
observability_baseline.rationale: Research team needs training metrics for publication; ops team needs cluster cost visibility

execution_preference.value: Research sprint cycles of 4 weeks; model versions tagged in MLflow; paper submission milestones as delivery gates
execution_preference.rationale: Academic publication timeline drives delivery

llm_integration.value: GNN model (PyTorch Geometric GraphSAGE or GATv2) trained on labelled BIM graphs; NOT an LLM in the language model sense; capabilities flag ai_llm used for GNN inference workload classification
llm_integration.rationale: GNN is the AI component; capabilities enum ai_llm is the closest fit for ML inference workloads

project.goal: GNN-based constructability issue detection in BIM models

frontend.pages: Upload, Training Dashboard, Inference Results, Subgraph Viewer

devops.hosting: GKE GPU nodes plus Cloud Run

open_questions.labelling: Constructability labels sourced from post-construction delay records; labelling schema defined with university team

confirmed_decisions.gnn_architecture: GraphSAGE or GATv2 to be selected based on initial experiments

reasoner_reviews.product: The MVP scope is well-scoped for a research prototype: IFC ingestion, graph construction, GNN training, and inference with a Streamlit dashboard covers the minimum needed for the first publication. Deferring BIM 360 integration and transfer learning is correct for the research timeline.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Separating the GNN training workload onto GKE GPU nodes from the inference API on Cloud Run is a good cost control decision given variable training frequency. Neo4j as the graph store enables native Cypher queries for subgraph explainability retrieval. MLflow for experiment tracking is the right choice for a research context.
reasoner_reviews.security: Custom JWT for internal-only access is proportionate for a research prototype. The BIM model files contain proprietary structural designs and must be stored in a private GCS bucket with no public URLs. No external API surface minimises the attack surface.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: GCP GPU budget of $500/month means T4 GPU nodes should be used on-demand with scale-to-zero. IFC 4 format only at MVP is a sensible constraint; ifcopenshell handles IFC 4 parsing reliably in Python.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The plan needs clearer articulation of the graph schema: what constitutes a node and edge type. The constructability label schema and how post-construction delay records are mapped to specific BIM nodes needs more detail.

frontend.app_structure: Streamlit multi-page app with st.navigation; pages: Upload Model, Training Dashboard, Inference Results, Subgraph Explorer
frontend.pages_and_flows: Upload Model: IFC file upload widget, project metadata form, submit to preprocessing API. Training Dashboard: MLflow run list, loss and accuracy curves from MLflow tracking API, model version selector. Inference Results: upload IFC for inference, ranked list of flagged nodes with risk score and constructability category. Subgraph Explorer: D3.js subgraph visualisation embedded via st.components.v1.html.
frontend.performance_notes: IFC file uploads up to 500MB; Streamlit file_uploader streams to GCS via FastAPI; D3 subgraph renders max 200-node subgraphs to avoid browser memory issues

backend.service_design: FastAPI app exposing IFC ingestion, training job trigger, and inference endpoints; background preprocessing via asyncio subprocess calls to ifcopenshell worker
backend.business_modules: ifc_parser.py (ifcopenshell IFC 4 extraction), graph_builder.py (Neo4j node/edge creation), training_api.py (GKE Job submission), inference_api.py (PyG model forward pass), mlflow_client.py (experiment logging)
backend.background_jobs: IFC preprocessing as FastAPI BackgroundTask; GNN training submitted as GKE Kubernetes Job with GPU resource request
backend.llm_integration_design: GNN inference: load trained GraphSAGE model from GCS, construct PyG Data object from Neo4j subgraph, run forward pass, return node-level risk scores ranked by softmax confidence
backend.scaling_notes: Cloud Run inference service autoscales to zero; GKE training Job nodes spin up on-demand with T4 GPU

security.auth_design: Custom JWT signed with HS256; FastAPI dependency injects user context for researcher and engineer roles
security.secrets_management: GCP Secret Manager for JWT signing key and Neo4j credentials; injected as environment variables in Cloud Run and GKE Job specs
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Cloud Audit Logs for GCS bucket access; FastAPI request logging with Cloud Logging structured JSON

data.entities: bim_component_nodes (Neo4j node: id, ifc_global_id, component_type, element_class, project_id, floor_level, volume_m3, constructability_label), spatial_relationship_edges (Neo4j relationship: type ENUM ADJACENT_TO/STRUCTURALLY_CONNECTED/SHARES_OPENING/CONTAINED_IN, clearance_mm, installation_sequence_order), graph_model_versions (Neo4j node: model_version_id, trained_at, f1_score, mlflow_run_id), training_datasets (GCS labelled node CSV per project)
data.storage_design: Neo4j 5 AuraDB for graph storage; GCS bucket for IFC files, trained model weights, and training dataset CSVs; MLflow tracking server on Cloud Run with GCS artifact store
data.analytics_events: MLflow logs per training run: epoch loss, validation F1, per-class precision and recall for constructability categories (clash/clearance/sequence/access)

devops.deployment_topology: Cloud Run: FastAPI inference API (min 0, max 4 instances), Streamlit app (min 1 instance), MLflow tracking server (min 1 instance). GKE cluster: n1-standard-4 node pool (scale-to-zero), NVIDIA T4 GPU node pool (scale-to-zero, max 2 nodes for budget control)
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff lint, mypy, pytest 80% coverage, Docker build and push to Artifact Registry, deploy Cloud Run services on merge to main, GKE manifests applied via kubectl for GPU Job definitions
devops.observability_stack: Grafana dashboards for GKE GPU utilisation, GPU memory, training job duration; Prometheus node exporter on GKE; MLflow UI for experiment comparison; Cloud Monitoring for Cloud Run latency and error rate

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a GNN-based BIM constructability research prototype on GCP. Key decisions: Neo4j for graph storage enables native Cypher subgraph retrieval for explainability; GPU node pools on GKE with scale-to-zero keeps training cost within $500/month budget; Streamlit for Python-native research UI avoids separate frontend build. Strong quality means all sections need domain-specific depth covering BIM graph construction, GNN training, and constructability classification.
target_output.title: BIM Constructability GNN Research Prototype Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A graph neural network research prototype for detecting constructability issues in IFC 4 BIM models, built on GCP with Neo4j 5 for graph storage, PyTorch Geometric for GNN training on GKE GPU nodes, and a Streamlit research dashboard. IFC model components are extracted as typed graph nodes with spatial relationship edges, a GraphSAGE or GATv2 model is trained on labelled post-construction delay records, and inference ranks flagged nodes by constructability risk category with subgraph explainability.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two-tier research platform with a Streamlit multi-page application communicating over HTTPS to a FastAPI backend on Cloud Run. IFC files are uploaded to a private GCS bucket and preprocessed by an ifcopenshell worker that extracts component nodes and spatial edges into Neo4j 5. GNN training is submitted as a Kubernetes Job on a GKE GPU node pool using PyTorch Geometric, with training metrics logged to an MLflow tracking server. Inference runs on-demand via the FastAPI endpoint which retrieves a subgraph from Neo4j, constructs a PyG Data object, and runs a forward pass with the latest trained model weights from GCS. Grafana and Prometheus monitor the GKE cluster; MLflow provides experiment comparison for the research team.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Streamlit 1.35 Python. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, ifcopenshell for IFC 4 parsing, PyTorch Geometric 2.5 with GraphSAGE and GATv2 implementations, neo4j Python driver. Graph DB: Neo4j 5 AuraDB. Blob storage: GCP Cloud Storage. Experiment tracking: MLflow 2.12 with GCS artifact store. GPU: NVIDIA T4 on GKE. CI/CD: GitHub Actions. Monitoring: Grafana, Prometheus, Cloud Monitoring. Container registry: GCP Artifact Registry.
target_output.functional_feature_map: IFC Model Ingestion: upload IFC 4 file, ifcopenshell extracts IfcProduct nodes with geometry, material, and classification attributes, spatial relationships extracted from IfcRelConnects and IfcRelContainedInSpatialStructure. Graph Construction: nodes written to Neo4j with element class, volume, floor level, and constructability label; edges typed as ADJACENT_TO, STRUCTURALLY_CONNECTED, SHARES_OPENING, or CONTAINED_IN with clearance_mm attributes. GNN Training: GraphSAGE or GATv2 trained on node-level multi-class classification (clash, clearance_violation, installation_sequence, restricted_access); MLflow logs loss, F1, and confusion matrix per epoch. Inference: new IFC model converted to graph, forward pass returns per-node risk scores, results ranked by predicted constructability category. Subgraph Explorer: D3.js visualisation of flagged node neighbourhood with risk score labels.
target_output.system_components: Cloud Run services: FastAPI API (inference, IFC ingestion, training job submission), Streamlit dashboard, MLflow tracking server. GKE cluster: standard node pool for general workloads, NVIDIA T4 GPU node pool (scale-to-zero, max 2 nodes) for GNN training Jobs. Neo4j 5 AuraDB managed graph DB. GCP Cloud Storage: ifc-uploads bucket, model-weights bucket, mlflow-artifacts bucket. GCP Artifact Registry for Docker images. Prometheus and Grafana on GKE for cluster observability. GCP Secret Manager for credentials.
target_output.workflows: IFC Ingestion and Graph Build: Researcher uploads an IFC 4 file via Streamlit; FastAPI streams file to GCS ifc-uploads bucket, creates a preprocessing BackgroundTask that invokes ifcopenshell to extract IfcProduct entities, maps each to a Neo4j node with attributes (element_class, volume_m3, floor_level, constructability_label), extracts spatial relationships via IfcRelConnects and IfcRelContainedInSpatialStructure, writes Neo4j edges with clearance_mm and installation_sequence_order properties; on completion updates project status to graph_ready. GNN Training Run: Researcher selects a project graph and training config in Streamlit; FastAPI submits a GKE Kubernetes Job requesting 1 T4 GPU; training script loads project graph from Neo4j into PyG Data objects, splits into train/val/test by floor level, trains GraphSAGE with cross-entropy loss on constructability labels, logs epoch loss and per-class F1 to MLflow, saves best model weights to GCS model-weights bucket. Inference on New Model: Design engineer uploads an IFC model; FastAPI parses IFC to graph, queries Neo4j for k-hop neighbourhood around each component node, constructs PyG batch, runs forward pass with latest model, returns JSON list of flagged nodes sorted by risk score with predicted category and confidence.
target_output.data_model: Neo4j nodes: bim_component_nodes (id, ifc_global_id STRING, component_type STRING ENUM column/beam/slab/wall/mep_duct/mep_pipe, element_class STRING, project_id STRING, floor_level INT, volume_m3 FLOAT, constructability_label STRING ENUM clash/clearance_violation/installation_sequence/restricted_access/none, model_version STRING). Neo4j relationships: ADJACENT_TO (clearance_mm FLOAT), STRUCTURALLY_CONNECTED (connection_type STRING, load_path STRING), SHARES_OPENING (opening_id STRING), CONTAINED_IN (spatial_level STRING). GCS CSVs: training_datasets/PROJECT_ID/labels.csv (ifc_global_id, constructability_label, delay_days_historical). graph_model_versions Neo4j node (model_version_id, architecture ENUM graphsage/gatv2, trained_at, val_f1 FLOAT, mlflow_run_id, gcs_weights_path).
target_output.api_design: POST /api/v1/projects/ID/ifc-upload (multipart IFC file, triggers preprocessing job). GET /api/v1/projects/ID/graph-status (poll preprocessing completion). POST /api/v1/projects/ID/training-jobs (submit GKE training Job with hyperparameter config). GET /api/v1/projects/ID/training-jobs/RUN_ID (poll training Job status and MLflow run link). POST /api/v1/projects/ID/inference (upload IFC for inference, return flagged node list). GET /api/v1/projects/ID/subgraph/NODE_ID?depth=2 (retrieve k-hop subgraph for D3 visualisation). All endpoints require custom JWT Bearer token.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Custom JWT HS256 with 8-hour expiry; signing key in GCP Secret Manager. FastAPI JWT dependency raises 401 for expired or invalid tokens. RBAC: researcher role can trigger training jobs and upload models; engineer role can run inference only. GCS buckets: uniform bucket-level access, no public ACLs, Cloud Audit Logs enabled. Neo4j AuraDB with VPC peering to GCP project; no public endpoint. BIM files deleted from local processing after GCS upload. GCP Secret Manager for Neo4j credentials and JWT signing key.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions pipeline: ruff lint, mypy, pytest 80% coverage, Docker build and push to Artifact Registry, Cloud Run service deploy (FastAPI, Streamlit, MLflow) on merge to main. GKE training Job manifest version-controlled; applied via kubectl in CI on manifest changes. GPU node pool uses node auto-provisioning with NVIDIA T4 and scale-to-zero to minimise idle GPU cost. Cloud Run FastAPI inference service min-instances 0, max 4, concurrency 5. Neo4j AuraDB provisioned via Terraform in separate infra repo.
target_output.observability: Grafana dashboards on GKE: GPU utilisation percent, GPU memory usage MB, training Job duration minutes, active Jobs count. Prometheus node exporter for GKE node metrics. MLflow experiment tracker: per-run epoch loss curve, validation F1 per constructability class (clash, clearance_violation, installation_sequence, restricted_access), confusion matrix. Cloud Monitoring: Cloud Run request count, latency p50/p95, instance count. Alert: GPU node pool cost forecast exceeds $450/month triggers Slack notification to ops channel.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Primary cost: GKE T4 GPU node at $0.35/hr, budget cap 2 nodes max. Training job for 50-project graph typically takes 2-4 hrs per run. Estimated $200-400/month for 5-10 training runs per month within $500 budget. Cloud Run inference at min-0 has negligible idle cost. Neo4j AuraDB Professional tier approximately $65/month. GCS storage for IFC files and model weights approximately $20/month. Total estimate $300-500/month within constraint.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): IFC 4 ingestion pipeline with ifcopenshell, Neo4j graph construction, labelled training dataset from 3 pilot projects, GraphSAGE training on GKE T4 GPU, MLflow experiment tracking, inference endpoint, basic Streamlit dashboard with upload, training status, and ranked inference results. Phase 2 (4 weeks): GATv2 architecture comparison experiment, subgraph D3 visualisation in Streamlit, improved constructability label schema with university team, per-class precision/recall dashboard. Phase 3 post-prototype: multi-project transfer learning experiments, BIM 360 webhook integration for live IFC ingestion, paper submission with benchmark results.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python type annotations throughout; mypy strict for FastAPI and data modules. ruff for linting and import order. All Neo4j queries use parameterised Cypher with no string interpolation. PyG Data objects constructed via utility functions with unit tests covering node feature extraction and edge attribute mapping. MLflow runs tagged with git commit SHA for reproducibility. IFC test fixtures checked into repo for CI testing.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GNN model accuracy insufficient for publication without larger labelled dataset. Mitigation: start labelling with 3 pilot projects; define minimum F1 threshold of 0.70 for paper submission. Risk: ifcopenshell fails to parse non-standard IFC 4 exports from certain authoring tools. Mitigation: validate IFC against schema before ingestion; log parse errors to MLflow run for researcher review. Risk: Neo4j AuraDB graph size grows beyond Professional tier limits. Mitigation: partition graphs by project; archive completed project graphs to GCS as GraphML exports. Tradeoff: Neo4j AuraDB managed service over self-hosted for simpler ops at higher cost; appropriate for research prototype.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Neo4j 5 AuraDB over pgvector for native graph traversal needed for k-hop subgraph explainability. Confirmed PyTorch Geometric over DGL for larger research community and better IFC-compatible graph construction utilities. GNN architecture selection (GraphSAGE vs GATv2) deferred to initial experiment results. Constructability label schema: 4 classes (clash, clearance_violation, installation_sequence, restricted_access) defined with university team based on post-construction delay record analysis.

metadata.notes: BIM GNN constructability research prototype on GCP; strong quality with detailed graph schema, GNN training pipeline, and cost analysis for GPU budget constraint.

project_goal.value: Digitise daily pre-start safety checks, plant inspection forms, and near-miss incident reports for site foremen and safety officers on civil infrastructure projects, with offline capability and automated critical-hazard escalation to safety managers
project_goal.rationale: Replaces paper-based WHS forms and ensures records reach cloud even in tunnels and remote sites with no mobile signal

target_users.value: Site foremen, safety officers, and project managers on civil infrastructure construction sites
target_users.rationale: Primary field users identified in discovery workshops; safety officers need escalation visibility

project_class.rationale: Primary interface is a native mobile app with offline-first data model; no desktop-first requirement

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, analytics
capabilities.rationale: React Native mobile UI, FastAPI backend, PostgreSQL persistence, Firebase Auth, push/SMS integrations, safety analytics dashboard

complexity_level.rationale: Offline sync, escalation workflows, multi-form types, and role-based site scoping add meaningful complexity beyond simple CRUD

risk_level.rationale: Internal operational data; no PII beyond employee identifiers; escalation failures could have safety consequences requiring reliable delivery

data_sensitivity.rationale: Safety records are company-internal; subject to regulator audit but not classified as personal or health data under privacy legislation

external_exposure.rationale: All API endpoints require Firebase Auth JWT; no public access to safety records

access_model.value: RBAC: foreman (submit forms), safety_officer (review and close incidents), project_manager (analytics and escalation oversight), admin (user and site management)
access_model.rationale: Different field roles require different write and approval permissions scoped to their assigned sites

feature_scope.value: Pre-start safety checklists, plant inspection forms, near-miss incident reports, offline sync via WatermelonDB, push and SMS escalation for critical hazards, safety analytics dashboard, corrective action tracking
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set agreed in scope workshop with WHS and project stakeholders

mvp_scope.value: Pre-start checklists and near-miss reports with offline sync, critical-hazard push notifications, incident list view for safety officers
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for Q2 pilot across two active sites

security_baseline.value: Firebase Auth JWT on all API endpoints, TLS 1.3 in transit, AES-256 encrypted on-device SQLite via WatermelonDB encryption plugin, row-level site scoping in PostgreSQL
security_baseline.rationale: Internal data requiring authenticated access; device encryption guards against lost handsets on construction sites

frontend_stack.value: React Native with TypeScript, WatermelonDB for offline SQLite sync
frontend_stack.rationale: Cross-platform iOS and Android with battle-tested offline-first sync; WatermelonDB handles incremental pull-push against FastAPI endpoint

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0, Celery for async escalation workers
backend_stack.rationale: Team Python expertise; FastAPI async performance for sync endpoint; Celery reliability for escalation job queue

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL, Redis 7 on Memorystore for Celery broker
data_platform.rationale: Relational integrity for safety records; Redis for reliable async escalation processing with at-least-once delivery

hosting_target.rationale: Serverless scale matches variable field-crew submission bursts; Firebase FCM handles cross-platform push notifications

privacy_retention_policy.value: Safety records retained 7 years per WHS Act obligations; audit logs retained 3 years; soft-delete with quarterly hard-purge job
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Work health and safety legislation mandates minimum retention periods for incident records subject to regulator audit

future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 and 3 roadmap items post-MVP

constraints.rationale: Tunnel and remote site connectivity requirements; modern OS baseline for WatermelonDB encryption support

project.goal: Offline-first mobile safety forms platform with cloud sync and escalation workflows
project.target_users: Site foremen, safety officers, project managers
project.timeline: Q2 MVP pilot on two sites

frontend.state: React Query for server sync, Zustand for local UI state

security.device: AES-256 SQLite encryption via WatermelonDB plugin

data.sync: WatermelonDB pull/push protocol against FastAPI sync endpoint

devops.ci: Bitrise for mobile, GitHub Actions for API

open_questions.photo_attachments: Deferred to Phase 2; GCS presigned URL approach TBD

reasoner_reviews.product: Pre-start checklists and near-miss reports cover the highest-value safety workflows for the MVP. Offline-first is non-negotiable for tunnel and remote site use. Corrective action tracking should be in MVP to close the safety loop rather than deferred to Phase 2.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: WatermelonDB sync against a FastAPI pull/push endpoint is a proven pattern for React Native offline-first apps. The sync endpoint should use logical clock versioning to handle multi-device conflicts correctly. Cloud Run cold starts could delay escalation notifications; keep a minimum instance count of 1 on the escalation Celery worker service.
reasoner_reviews.security: Firebase Auth JWTs must be validated server-side on every request via firebase-admin SDK; do not rely solely on client-side token checks. On-device SQLite encryption via WatermelonDB encryption plugin is critical for lost-handset scenarios given safety record sensitivity. PostgreSQL row-level security policies scoping to user site_ids prevents cross-site data leakage between competing project crews.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 8-hour offline window is achievable with WatermelonDB incremental sync on reconnect. p95 latency under 800 ms is achievable on Cloud Run with pgBouncer connection pooling. Bitrise pipeline should include Detox E2E tests on simulators before signing and distributing builds.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Escalation flow depends on Redis Celery broker reliability. A Redis outage could silently drop critical-hazard notifications. Recommend persisting escalation_events to PostgreSQL before enqueuing to Redis so a recovery Celery beat task can replay missed escalations after Redis recovers.

frontend.app_structure: React Native bare workflow for full native module access. WatermelonDB schema mirrors PostgreSQL schema with local synced_at timestamps. Screen navigator stack: Auth, SiteSelector, FormTypeMenu, ChecklistForm, IncidentReportForm, PlantInspectionForm, SyncStatus, IncidentList.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Pre-Start Checklist: foreman selects site, chooses checklist template by hazard_category, answers structured items, saves draft offline or submits. Near-Miss Report: incident_type and severity picker, free-text description, auto-filled GPS coordinates, hazard_category selection. Plant Inspection: equipment QR scan auto-fills equipment_id, condition ratings per component, defect flags with mandatory corrective_action entry.
frontend.state_management: React Query handles background sync polling against /sync/pull endpoint. Zustand slice tracks offline queue length, pending upload count, and connectivity status banner display. WatermelonDB observers drive form list screens with reactive updates.
frontend.frontend_risks: WatermelonDB schema migrations require careful versioning across app releases. Push notification deep-linking on Android 12+ requires notification channels declared at install time.

backend.service_design: FastAPI app with routers: /sync (WatermelonDB pull/push), /escalations (critical-hazard trigger and status), /forms (checklist and inspection templates), /analytics (aggregated safety metrics per site). Celery worker process consuming Redis queue for escalation delivery.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful OpenAPI 3.1. Sync endpoint accepts push changes array and returns pull changes since last_pulled_at with logical timestamp. Escalation endpoint is idempotent keyed by incident_report_id to prevent duplicate SMS/push on Celery retry. All endpoints validate Firebase JWT via FastAPI dependency.
backend.failure_handling: Escalation worker uses at-least-once delivery: escalation_events persisted to PostgreSQL before Redis enqueue. Recovery Celery beat task polls for unprocessed escalation_events older than 30 seconds and retries delivery on Redis availability.
backend.background_jobs: Celery tasks: escalation_processor (critical hazard push via FCM and SMS via Twilio), weekly_safety_summary_emailer (aggregates incident counts by site and hazard category), sync_conflict_resolver (flags concurrent-edit conflicts for safety officer review).

security.auth_design: Firebase Auth JWTs validated via firebase-admin SDK on every FastAPI request. Short-lived tokens with 1-hour expiry; silent refresh in React Native client. Firebase service account key stored in GCP Secret Manager.
security.authorization_model: RBAC enforced via role claims in JWT. PostgreSQL row-level security on safety_checks, plant_inspections, incident_reports scoped to user site_ids. Project managers read aggregated analytics only; foremen cannot read peer submissions.
security.secrets_management: GCP Secret Manager for Firebase service account key, Cloud SQL DSN, Twilio SMS credentials. Cloud Run service account with secretAccessor IAM role on specific secret ARNs only.
security.security_risks: Lost handset with unencrypted SQLite exposes recent form data. Mitigation: WatermelonDB encryption with device-derived key stored in iOS Keychain or Android Keystore. Firebase token replay mitigated by TLS 1.3 and 1-hour token expiry.

data.entities: safety_checks (id UUID, site_id, submitted_by, checklist_template_id, status ENUM draft/submitted/reviewed, hazard_category, findings_json JSONB, submitted_at, reviewed_by, reviewed_at). plant_inspections (id UUID, site_id, equipment_id, inspector_id, condition_ratings_json JSONB, defects_json JSONB, status ENUM pass/conditional/fail, inspected_at). incident_reports (id UUID, site_id, reporter_id, incident_type ENUM near_miss/first_aid/lost_time, severity ENUM low/medium/high/critical, description TEXT, location_lat NUMERIC, location_lng NUMERIC, hazard_category_id, status ENUM open/under_investigation/closed). corrective_actions (id UUID, incident_report_id, assigned_to, description, due_date DATE, status ENUM open/in_progress/verified). escalation_events (id UUID, incident_report_id, channel ENUM push/sms, recipient_user_id, sent_at, delivered BOOL, retry_count INT). site_personnel (id UUID, firebase_uid, name, role ENUM, site_ids UUID[], fcm_token, phone).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL private IP with pgBouncer connection pooling sidecar on Cloud Run. WatermelonDB on-device with AES-256 encryption. GCS bucket for future photo attachments.
data.retention_and_deletion: safety_checks, plant_inspections, incident_reports retained 7 years then archived to GCS coldline. escalation_events and audit_logs retained 3 years. Soft-delete pattern; hard-purge job runs quarterly after retention window.
data.data_risks: WatermelonDB sync conflicts on concurrent foreman edits from multiple devices. Mitigation: last-write-wins with conflict_flagged boolean for safety officer review queue on critical submissions.

devops.deployment_topology: Cloud Run services: api (min 1 instance, max 10, 2 vCPU 2 GB), escalation_worker (min 1 instance). Cloud SQL Postgres 16 with HA standby. Redis 7 on Memorystore. Firebase Auth and FCM. GCP Secret Manager.
devops.ci_cd_design: Bitrise pipeline: bun install, Jest unit tests, Detox E2E on iOS 16 and Android 12 simulators, code signing, TestFlight and internal track distribution. GitHub Actions: ruff lint, pytest 90% coverage gate, Docker build, Artifact Registry push, Cloud Run rolling deploy.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM on Cloud Run via dd-trace-py. Firebase Crashlytics in React Native bundle. Structured JSON logs with site_id, form_type, user_id fields. Cloud Monitoring alerts on escalation_worker error rate, Cloud SQL CPU, Redis memory.
devops.rollback_strategy: Cloud Run traffic splitting for instant rollback to previous revision. Mobile rollbacks via Bitrise Emergency Stop on internal track. Alembic reversible migrations with pre-deploy smoke test on Cloud Run job.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing an offline-first React Native safety forms app for civil infrastructure sites. Key decisions: WatermelonDB for the 8-hour offline requirement with incremental sync on reconnect; Cloud Run min-instance=1 for escalation worker to avoid cold-start delays on critical notifications; Celery persistence pattern to guarantee escalation delivery even during Redis failures; PostgreSQL row-level site scoping to prevent cross-project data leakage between competing contractors on shared platform.
target_output.title: Civil Site Safety Forms Mobile Platform
target_output.executive_summary: An offline-first React Native application for site foremen and safety officers to complete pre-start safety checklists, plant inspection forms, and near-miss incident reports on civil infrastructure construction sites. Forms are persisted to an AES-256 encrypted on-device SQLite store via WatermelonDB and synced to a FastAPI backend on GCP Cloud Run when connectivity resumes. Critical-severity incidents trigger automated push and SMS escalation to safety managers within seconds of cloud sync. A PostgreSQL 16 backend on Cloud SQL with Redis-backed Celery workers handles sync, escalation delivery, and weekly safety summary reporting.
target_output.architecture_overview: Offline-first mobile architecture with React Native client storing forms in WatermelonDB-managed AES-256 encrypted SQLite. Client syncs to FastAPI REST API on GCP Cloud Run using WatermelonDB pull/push protocol with logical clock versioning for conflict detection. Firebase Auth issues JWTs validated server-side on every request. PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL is the system of record for safety_checks, plant_inspections, incident_reports, and corrective_actions with row-level security policies scoping all access to user-assigned site_ids. Redis 7 on Memorystore backs Celery workers processing critical-hazard escalations via Firebase FCM push and Twilio SMS. An escalation_events persistence table guarantees at-least-once delivery even during Redis unavailability. Datadog APM on Cloud Run and Firebase Crashlytics provide full-stack observability from mobile crash to server trace.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: React Native (bare workflow), TypeScript, WatermelonDB with SQLite encryption, React Query, Zustand, Detox for E2E tests. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0, Celery, firebase-admin SDK, Twilio helper library. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL with pgBouncer. Cache/Queue: Redis 7 on Memorystore. Infra: GCP Cloud Run, Firebase Auth, Firebase FCM, Twilio SMS, GCP Secret Manager, Artifact Registry. CI/CD: Bitrise (mobile), GitHub Actions (API). Observability: Datadog APM, Firebase Crashlytics, Cloud Monitoring.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Pre-Start Safety Checklist: foreman selects active site and hazard_category template, completes structured checklist items offline or online, submits triggering sync. Plant Inspection: equipment QR code scan auto-fills equipment_id from site asset registry, structured condition ratings per mechanical and safety component, mandatory corrective_action entry on any defect flag. Near-Miss Incident Report: incident_type and severity picker, free-text description with auto-populated GPS coordinates, hazard_category selection. Escalation Engine: severity=critical submissions trigger Celery task within 10 seconds of cloud sync; FCM push and Twilio SMS dispatched to safety_officer and project_manager for the incident's site. Corrective Action Tracker: open actions listed per site with due-date countdown, verified closed by safety officer. Safety Analytics Dashboard: weekly submission counts, open incident rates, overdue corrective actions by site and hazard category.
target_output.system_components: React Native client (iOS 16+, Android 12+) with WatermelonDB encrypted SQLite. FastAPI API service on Cloud Run (min 1, max 10 instances, 2 vCPU 2 GB). Celery escalation worker on Cloud Run (min 1 instance). PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL with HA standby and pgBouncer connection pooler. Redis 7 on Memorystore for Celery broker. Firebase Auth for JWT issuance. Firebase FCM for cross-platform push. Twilio for SMS to site managers without smartphones. GCP Secret Manager for credentials. Artifact Registry for container images. Datadog APM sidecar on Cloud Run. Firebase Crashlytics SDK in React Native bundle.
target_output.workflows: Offline Form Submission and Sync: Foreman completes pre-start checklist in a tunnel with no signal. WatermelonDB saves form to encrypted local SQLite with synced=false. On signal recovery, React Query triggers POST /sync/push; FastAPI validates JWT and site_id scope, persists records to PostgreSQL, returns pull changes for any peer updates. WatermelonDB merges pull changes. Sync banner updates to 'All records uploaded'. Critical Hazard Escalation: Safety officer submits near-miss with severity=critical. FastAPI sync persists incident_report to PostgreSQL, inserts escalation_event, enqueues Celery task to escalation queue. Worker sends FCM push to project_manager and safety_officer Firebase tokens, sends Twilio SMS to registered site manager mobile, marks escalation_event.delivered=true. If Celery worker is restarting, a recovery beat task polls escalation_events older than 30 seconds and retries delivery.
target_output.data_model: safety_checks (id UUID PK, site_id FK, submitted_by FK->site_personnel, checklist_template_id FK, status ENUM draft/submitted/reviewed, hazard_category VARCHAR, findings_json JSONB, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, reviewed_by FK, reviewed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). plant_inspections (id UUID PK, site_id FK, equipment_id FK, inspector_id FK, condition_ratings_json JSONB, defects_json JSONB, status ENUM pass/conditional/fail, inspected_at TIMESTAMPTZ). incident_reports (id UUID PK, site_id FK, reporter_id FK, incident_type ENUM near_miss/first_aid/lost_time, severity ENUM low/medium/high/critical, description TEXT, location_lat NUMERIC, location_lng NUMERIC, hazard_category_id FK, status ENUM open/under_investigation/closed, reported_at TIMESTAMPTZ). corrective_actions (id UUID PK, incident_report_id FK, assigned_to FK, description TEXT, due_date DATE, status ENUM open/in_progress/verified, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). escalation_rules (id UUID PK, site_id FK, severity ENUM, notify_role VARCHAR[], sms_numbers TEXT[]). escalation_events (id UUID PK, incident_report_id FK, channel ENUM push/sms, recipient_user_id UUID, sent_at TIMESTAMPTZ, delivered BOOL, retry_count INT). site_personnel (id UUID PK, firebase_uid, name, role ENUM foreman/safety_officer/project_manager/admin, site_ids UUID[], fcm_token, phone).
target_output.api_design: FastAPI REST at /api/v1. POST /sync/pull - {last_pulled_at, site_ids[]} returns {changes:{safety_checks,plant_inspections,incident_reports}, timestamp}. POST /sync/push - {changes:{safety_checks,plant_inspections,incident_reports}} returns {success, conflicts[]}. GET /forms/templates?site_id={id} - active checklist and inspection templates. GET /incidents?site_id={id}&status=open - open incident list for safety officer. PATCH /incidents/{id}/status - update incident status with corrective action assignment. GET /analytics/safety-summary?site_id={id}&period=7d - aggregated submission counts and open NCR rates. POST /escalations/test - test escalation trigger for admin validation. All endpoints require Authorization: Bearer <Firebase JWT>.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Firebase Auth JWTs validated server-side via firebase-admin SDK on every FastAPI request; role claim extracted for RBAC. PostgreSQL row-level security on all safety data tables scoped to user site_ids. WatermelonDB uses SQLite Encryption Extension with key derived from Firebase UID stored in iOS Keychain or Android Keystore. TLS 1.3 on all Cloud Run ingress. GCP Secret Manager holds Firebase service account key, Cloud SQL DSN, Twilio credentials; Cloud Run service account has secretAccessor on specific ARNs only. WHS Act compliance: safety records retained 7 years; PostgreSQL audit_log table captures every status change with user_id, timestamp, and changed_fields JSONB.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GCP: Cloud Run API service (australia-southeast1, min 1, max 10 instances), Cloud Run escalation worker (min 1), Cloud SQL Postgres 16 HA (db-n1-standard-2), Memorystore Redis 7 (1 GB), Firebase project for Auth and FCM. Bitrise mobile pipeline: Jest unit tests (80% gate), Detox E2E on iOS 16 simulator and Android 12 emulator, code signing, TestFlight (iOS) and internal track (Android) distribution. GitHub Actions API pipeline: ruff lint, black format check, pytest 90% coverage gate, Docker build, push to Artifact Registry, Cloud Run deploy with no-traffic then traffic migration after smoke test. Alembic reversible migrations run as Cloud Run job pre-deploy. Environment promotion: dev -> staging on PR merge to main, production on version tag.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM auto-instrumented on Cloud Run via dd-trace-py; traces include sync endpoint latency, escalation Celery task processing time, Cloud SQL query duration. Firebase Crashlytics captures React Native crashes with WatermelonDB sync state context. Structured JSON logs: site_id, form_type, user_id, sync_record_count, escalation_event_id fields. Cloud Monitoring alerts: escalation_worker error rate >2% over 5 min (PagerDuty P1), Cloud SQL CPU >80% (P2), sync endpoint p95 >800 ms (P2), Memorystore memory >85% (email). Datadog dashboards: daily active sites by submission volume, escalation delivery success rate, sync conflict rate per site.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated ~$320/month for 200 daily active users across 10 sites. Cloud Run scales to zero outside shift hours; min-instance setting adds ~$40/month to prevent cold starts on critical escalations. Cloud SQL db-n1-standard-2 at ~$90/month. Memorystore Redis 1 GB at ~$35/month. Firebase FCM and Crashlytics free tier sufficient at expected volume. Scale trigger: at 50+ active sites, evaluate splitting escalation worker into a dedicated Cloud Run service with independent auto-scaling.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Firebase Auth and RBAC, pre-start checklist forms with offline WatermelonDB sync, near-miss incident report forms, critical-hazard push and SMS escalation with Celery persistence, incident list view for safety officers, Bitrise CI and Cloud Run deployment, PostgreSQL schema with row-level security policies. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Plant inspection forms with equipment QR scan, photo and video attachments via GCS presigned URLs, corrective action assignment and tracking, weekly safety summary email reports. Phase 3 (5 weeks): Safety analytics dashboard, integration with external HSEQ management system via REST webhook, predictive trend alerts flagging sites with rising near-miss rates.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode in React Native; mypy type checking enforced in GitHub Actions for FastAPI. ESLint and Prettier in Bitrise; ruff and black in GitHub Actions. WatermelonDB schema changes require migration file with up() and down() methods. Every API endpoint tested with pytest covering success path and unauthorized access; 90% coverage gate. React Native screens tested with Jest for form logic and Detox E2E for sync and offline flows. ADR documents for WatermelonDB sync protocol decisions and escalation delivery guarantees. Firebase UID not stored in plaintext logs; use hashed reference for log correlation.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: WatermelonDB sync conflicts when foreman edits the same checklist from two devices. Mitigation: last-write-wins with conflict_flagged boolean on safety_checks; safety officer review queue surfaces flagged records. Risk: Celery escalation worker unavailability during critical-hazard submission. Mitigation: escalation_events table persistence with 30-second recovery beat task retry. Tradeoff: WatermelonDB over custom sync protocol provides faster MVP delivery but requires adherence to its schema migration API. Tradeoff: Firebase FCM over self-hosted push reduces operational overhead but introduces Google infrastructure dependency for safety-critical notifications.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected WatermelonDB over custom sync for proven React Native offline-first handling. Confirmed GCP Cloud Run over GKE for operational simplicity at current scale. Chose Twilio SMS as fallback for project managers without push-capable devices. Decided corrective_actions included in MVP after product review confirmed safety closure loop is essential.

metadata.notes: Offline-first mobile safety forms for civil infrastructure sites with at-least-once escalation delivery guarantees.

project_goal.value: Research prototype exploring microservices decomposition strategies for a monolithic construction project management platform, decomposing document-control, scheduling, and cost-management modules into separate services communicating over an event bus, to assess operational complexity and data consistency trade-offs before committing to production migration
project_goal.rationale: The monolith has deployment coupling and scaling bottlenecks; this prototype validates whether microservices decomposition is worth the operational overhead before a full migration commitment

target_users.value: Platform engineering team and enterprise architecture committee at the construction software firm; research findings inform a production migration decision
target_users.rationale: Prototype outputs are internal technical evidence for a strategic architecture decision, not a user-facing product

project_class.rationale: Explicitly exploratory; outputs are benchmarks and findings, not a production system

capabilities.rationale: No frontend required; prototype focuses on service decomposition, event bus integration, saga orchestration, and operational metrics collection

complexity_level.rationale: Distributed saga orchestration, per-service PostgreSQL schemas, Kafka event bus, and comparative load testing against monolith baseline

risk_level.rationale: Internal research system; failure or inconsistency in prototype is acceptable and informative; internal data only

data_sensitivity.rationale: Prototype uses synthetic construction project data seeded from anonymised production snapshots

external_exposure.rationale: Deployed on internal GKE cluster; no external access required

access_model.value: Custom JWT for service-to-service calls; no user authentication UI; engineers access services directly via internal cluster ingress
access_model.rationale: Prototype does not require user-facing auth; service mesh JWT secures inter-service calls

feature_scope.value: Three decomposed services (document-control-svc, scheduling-svc, cost-mgmt-svc) communicating via Kafka; saga orchestrator handling cross-service transactions; consistency audit dashboard; end-to-end latency benchmarks vs monolith baseline; research findings report
feature_scope.rationale: Full scope of the research experiment required to produce a valid architecture decision record

mvp_scope.value: Three services with Kafka event bus, document_revised event triggering downstream service updates, basic saga rollback on downstream failure, Prometheus metrics collection
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum experiment scope to observe consistency and latency trade-offs

security_baseline.value: Service-to-service JWT via shared signing key; no external network exposure; Kubernetes network policies restrict cross-namespace traffic
security_baseline.rationale: Internal research prototype; minimal security surface; network policies prevent unintended service access

frontend_stack.value: None; Grafana for metrics dashboards; Python scripts for research analytics
frontend_stack.rationale: Prototype has no user-facing UI; Grafana provides sufficient observability for the research team

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 for document-control-svc and scheduling-svc; Python 3.12 for cost-mgmt-svc and analytics scripts
backend_stack.rationale: Mixed languages chosen deliberately to test inter-language Kafka consumer compatibility and serialisation overhead

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 with per-service schemas (document_schema, scheduling_schema, cost_schema); Kafka 3.6 for event bus
data_platform.rationale: Per-service schemas on shared cluster simulates database autonomy without the cost of separate RDS instances for a prototype

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot on GCP internal environment
hosting_target.rationale: Autopilot reduces node management overhead for a short-lived research prototype

privacy_retention_policy.value: Synthetic data only; no real PII; prototype environment torn down within 6 months; findings documented in ADR
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Research prototype using anonymised synthetic data has no retention obligations

future_scope.value: If findings are positive: production migration of document-control service as Service Extraction Wave 1; if negative: documented trade-off analysis archived as ADR for future reference
future_scope.rationale: Prototype directly informs the production decision; outputs are binarily useful regardless of conclusion

constraints.value: Prototype budget cap of USD 500/month on GCP; must produce publishable benchmarks comparing microservices vs monolith under equivalent synthetic load within 12 weeks
constraints.rationale: Research budget is capped; 12-week timeline aligns with the enterprise architecture committee review cycle

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus on GKE; custom metrics for inter-service event latency, saga transaction duration, and eventual-consistency resolution time
observability_baseline.rationale: Observability metrics are the primary research outputs; must be precise enough to support architecture decision

execution_preference.value: Research sprints of 2 weeks with findings documented at end of each sprint; GitLab CI for automated benchmark runs
execution_preference.rationale: Research cadence with regular documentation prevents findings from being lost; automated benchmarks ensure reproducibility

project.goal: Validate microservices decomposition trade-offs for document-control, scheduling, and cost-management domains

security.network: Kubernetes network policies restricting cross-namespace traffic

open_questions.saga_pattern: Choreography-based saga vs orchestrator-based saga for cross-service transactions?

reasoner_reviews.product: The research question is well-scoped: focus on three domains with known coupling in the monolith. The document_revised event triggering downstream scheduling and cost updates is the representative cross-domain transaction that will stress-test both consistency and latency. The 12-week timeline is achievable if the Kafka setup and synthetic data seeding are completed in the first sprint. The findings must include both quantitative benchmarks and qualitative operational complexity observations to be useful to the architecture committee.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The Round 1 plan did not specify whether choreography or orchestrator-based saga was chosen for cross-service transactions; this is the central design question for consistency management. The previous plan also lacked specification of the Kafka topic schema (Avro vs JSON) and partition strategy. These gaps mean the prototype cannot be built reproducibly from the plan as written, which is why ISS-001 was raised. Round 2 must specify the saga pattern choice and Kafka topic design.
reasoner_reviews.security: Service-to-service JWT with a shared signing key is acceptable for a research prototype; production would use mTLS or a service mesh. Kubernetes network policies should be added to prevent document-control-svc from directly querying scheduling_schema even though they share a PostgreSQL cluster -- this enforces the service boundary experiment. The shared PostgreSQL cluster is a deliberate simplification for the prototype; findings should note that production would require separate databases.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: USD 500/month is achievable on GKE Autopilot with three lightweight Go/Python services and a managed Kafka cluster. Confluent Cloud Kafka basic tier at USD 60/month; GKE Autopilot for 3 services approximately USD 150/month; PostgreSQL on Cloud SQL USD 100/month. Total under USD 400/month leaving buffer for load testing burst costs.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The weak plan quality is intentional: several sections in the Round 1 plan were thin. The data_model section listed event_bus_topics and service_boundaries as if they were database entities rather than Kafka topics and domain boundaries. The workflows section described the document_revised event flow but did not mention the saga rollback path. These are addressed in Round 2 but the plan is still slightly thin on the analytics scripts that produce the comparative benchmark outputs.

backend.service_design: Three bounded-context services: document-control-svc (Go 1.22, manages document_revisions, emits document_revised Kafka events), scheduling-svc (Go 1.22, subscribes to document_revised, updates task_dependencies referencing affected document), cost-mgmt-svc (Python 3.12, subscribes to document_revised, updates cost_code_mappings referencing document). Saga Orchestrator (Go 1.22): receives publish_document commands, coordinates document-control-svc then awaits ack events from scheduling-svc and cost-mgmt-svc; on timeout or nack, emits compensating revert_document_revision events to all consumers.
backend.api_patterns: Each service exposes a minimal REST API for research tooling: GET /health, GET /metrics (Prometheus), POST /simulate (inject synthetic events for load testing). Service-to-service calls via Kafka events only; no synchronous HTTP between services.
backend.failure_handling: Saga orchestrator tracks saga_transactions in its own PostgreSQL schema; on downstream service failure or 30-second timeout, emits compensating events and marks saga_transaction status as rolled_back. Dead-letter topic document_revised_dlq captures events that fail consumer processing after 3 retries.
backend.backend_risks: Avro schema registry adds operational overhead; consider JSON with shared struct validation for prototype simplicity. Saga orchestrator becomes a single point of failure; acceptable for research but must be noted in findings.

data.entities: document_schema.document_revisions (id UUID PK, doc_id UUID, revision_number INT, content_hash TEXT, published_at TIMESTAMPTZ, event_id UUID). scheduling_schema.schedule_baselines (id UUID PK, project_id UUID, task_dependencies JSONB, last_sync_doc_revision_id UUID, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). cost_schema.cost_commitments (id UUID PK, project_id UUID, cost_code TEXT, amount NUMERIC, linked_doc_revision_id UUID, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). saga_schema.saga_transactions (id UUID PK, command_type TEXT, status ENUM pending/completed/rolled_back, initiated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, participant_acks JSONB). consistency_audit_logs (id UUID PK, saga_id UUID FK, service_name TEXT, event_type TEXT, recorded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, resolution_lag_ms INT).
data.storage_design: Single PostgreSQL 16 Cloud SQL instance with four schemas (document_schema, scheduling_schema, cost_schema, saga_schema) isolated by PostgreSQL roles. Kafka 3.6 on Confluent Cloud with topics: document_revised (3 partitions, key=doc_id), document_revised_dlq, scheduling_ack, cost_ack. Avro schemas managed in Confluent Schema Registry.
data.consistency_notes: Eventual consistency window measured by consistency_audit_logs.resolution_lag_ms: time from document_revised event publish_at to downstream service update confirmed_at. Target SLO for research: p99 resolution lag under 2 seconds under synthetic load.

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot: four Deployments (document-control-svc, scheduling-svc, cost-mgmt-svc, saga-orchestrator-svc) in research namespace. Kubernetes network policies: document-control-svc can write to document_schema only; scheduling-svc to scheduling_schema only. Prometheus ServiceMonitors scrape /metrics from each service.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: go test, pytest, Docker build, push to GCR, kubectl apply. Benchmark pipeline: GitLab scheduled job runs k6 load test script weekly against both microservices cluster and monolith baseline, stores results in a GCS bucket, generates comparison Markdown report.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus + Grafana on GKE. Custom metrics: document_event_publish_rate, saga_transaction_duration_seconds (histogram), consistency_resolution_lag_seconds (histogram per service), dlq_depth. Grafana dashboard: Saga Transaction Timeline, Consistency Resolution Latency Distribution, Service-to-Service Event Flow diagram.
devops.devops_risks: GKE Autopilot node provisioning latency during load test burst could inflate latency measurements. Mitigation: warm minimum 1 node per service before benchmark runs via a pre-warm job.

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a weak revision round for a microservices research prototype. ISS-001 is addressed: orchestrator-based saga with saga-orchestrator-svc is chosen, with compensating revert_document_revision events on downstream failure. ISS-002 is addressed: Kafka topics specified with partition counts and Avro schema format. However, plan quality is intentionally weak: the analytics_and_benchmarking section is thin (does not detail the k6 load test scenarios or the specific monolith baseline comparison methodology), and the phased_implementation section is at minimum word count.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified orchestrator-based saga pattern with dedicated saga-orchestrator-svc tracking saga_transactions; defined compensation event revert_document_revision emitted on downstream service timeout or nack.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Cross-service transaction pattern is now implementable; saga orchestrator tracks state in saga_transactions table with rollback path defined

fix_report.action_taken: Defined Kafka topics: document_revised (3 partitions, key=doc_id), scheduling_ack (1 partition), cost_ack (1 partition), document_revised_dlq (1 partition); Avro schema with Schema Registry chosen; consistency_audit_logs.resolution_lag_ms defined as downstream ack timestamp minus event publish timestamp.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Kafka topic design now implementable; resolution lag measurement methodology defined for research outputs

target_output.title: Construction Platform Microservices Decomposition Research Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: A research prototype on GKE Autopilot decomposing a monolithic construction project management platform into three services (document-control-svc, scheduling-svc, cost-mgmt-svc) communicating over a Kafka 3.6 event bus. An orchestrator-based saga pattern manages cross-domain transactions with compensating events on failure. Per-service PostgreSQL schemas enforce data isolation boundaries. Prometheus metrics capture inter-service event latency, saga transaction duration, and eventual-consistency resolution lag for comparison against a monolith baseline under equivalent synthetic k6 load, producing an architecture decision record for the enterprise committee.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three bounded-context services deployed on GKE Autopilot in a research namespace: document-control-svc (Go 1.22) owns document_revisions in document_schema; scheduling-svc (Go 1.22) owns schedule_baselines in scheduling_schema; cost-mgmt-svc (Python 3.12) owns cost_commitments in cost_schema. A dedicated saga-orchestrator-svc (Go 1.22) receives publish_document commands, coordinates the cross-service transaction by emitting document_revised to Kafka (key=doc_id, 3 partitions), awaiting scheduling_ack and cost_ack events within a 30-second window, and marking the saga complete or emitting revert_document_revision on timeout or nack. Kafka 3.6 on Confluent Cloud with Avro schemas managed in Schema Registry. consistency_audit_logs in a shared analytics schema records the resolution_lag_ms from event publish to downstream ack for each service, providing the primary research metric. Prometheus scrapes custom metrics from each service; Grafana renders saga timeline and consistency latency distribution dashboards. A Python 3.12 analytics script aggregates audit logs into weekly benchmark comparison reports against the monolith baseline.
target_output.technology_stack: Services: Go 1.22 (document-control-svc, scheduling-svc, saga-orchestrator-svc), Python 3.12 (cost-mgmt-svc, analytics scripts). Event bus: Kafka 3.6 on Confluent Cloud with Avro schema serialisation and Schema Registry. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL (four isolated schemas via separate PostgreSQL roles). Orchestration: GKE Autopilot with GitLab CI. Observability: Prometheus + Grafana on GKE, custom metrics per service. Load testing: k6 with synthetic construction project event scripts.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Document Revision Publication: saga-orchestrator-svc receives publish_document command, document-control-svc writes document_revision and emits document_revised Avro event. Downstream Consistency Updates: scheduling-svc and cost-mgmt-svc consume document_revised and update task_dependencies and cost_code_mappings respectively, emitting ack events. Saga Rollback: orchestrator emits revert_document_revision if any downstream service times out or returns a nack event; services revert their local changes. Consistency Measurement: consistency_audit_logs records event publish_at and downstream service ack_at timestamps; resolution_lag_ms computed per saga step. Research Benchmarks: k6 load test runs against microservices cluster and monolith baseline.
target_output.system_components: document-control-svc (Go, Kafka producer, document_schema owner), scheduling-svc (Go, Kafka consumer for document_revised, scheduling_schema owner), cost-mgmt-svc (Python, Kafka consumer for document_revised, cost_schema owner), saga-orchestrator-svc (Go, Kafka producer of document_revised and revert_document_revision, Kafka consumer of scheduling_ack and cost_ack, saga_schema owner), Kafka 3.6 Confluent Cloud (document_revised 3-partition topic, scheduling_ack, cost_ack, document_revised_dlq), Schema Registry (Avro schemas), PostgreSQL 16 Cloud SQL (four schemas), Prometheus + Grafana, k6 load tester.
target_output.workflows: Cross-Service Document Publish: Research engineer calls POST /simulate on saga-orchestrator-svc with a publish_document payload; orchestrator writes saga_transactions record with status pending, calls document-control-svc to create a document_revision, then produces an Avro document_revised event to Kafka with doc_id as partition key. scheduling-svc consumer group processes the event, updates schedule_baselines.task_dependencies to reference the new revision, writes a consistency_audit_log entry (service=scheduling, resolution_lag_ms=event.ack_at - event.publish_at), and emits scheduling_ack to Kafka. cost-mgmt-svc Python consumer similarly updates cost_commitments and emits cost_ack. Orchestrator receives both acks, marks saga_transaction completed. Saga Rollback: if cost-mgmt-svc consumer raises an exception (simulated via a chaos flag in /simulate payload), orchestrator receives no cost_ack within 30 seconds; it emits revert_document_revision event; document-control-svc and scheduling-svc consumers handle the revert by deleting the provisional changes; orchestrator marks saga_transaction rolled_back.
target_output.data_model: document_schema.document_revisions (id UUID PK, doc_id UUID, revision_number INT, content_hash TEXT, published_at TIMESTAMPTZ, saga_id UUID). scheduling_schema.schedule_baselines (id UUID PK, project_id UUID, task_dependencies JSONB, last_sync_doc_revision_id UUID, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). cost_schema.cost_commitments (id UUID PK, project_id UUID, cost_code TEXT, amount NUMERIC, linked_doc_revision_id UUID, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). saga_schema.saga_transactions (id UUID PK, command_type TEXT, status ENUM pending/completed/rolled_back, initiated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, participant_acks JSONB). analytics_schema.consistency_audit_logs (id UUID PK, saga_id UUID FK, service_name TEXT, event_type TEXT, event_publish_at TIMESTAMPTZ, service_ack_at TIMESTAMPTZ, resolution_lag_ms INT). Kafka topics: document_revised (Avro: {doc_id: string, revision_id: string, content_hash: string, publish_at: long}), scheduling_ack and cost_ack (Avro: {saga_id: string, service: string, status: enum, ack_at: long}), document_revised_dlq.
target_output.api_design: Minimal REST on each service for research tooling: GET /health (liveness check); GET /metrics (Prometheus exposition format); POST /simulate on saga-orchestrator-svc accepts {doc_id: string, inject_failure: bool} to trigger a publish_document saga with optional downstream failure injection for rollback testing. Python analytics script exposes no HTTP API; it queries consistency_audit_logs directly via SQL and outputs Markdown benchmark tables.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Service-to-service JWT using a shared HS256 signing key distributed via Kubernetes Secret; each service validates the JWT on incoming REST calls. Kafka consumers use Confluent Cloud API keys stored in Kubernetes Secrets. Kubernetes NetworkPolicy restricts: document-control-svc can reach PostgreSQL document_schema port only; scheduling-svc to scheduling_schema; cost-mgmt-svc to cost_schema; saga-orchestrator-svc to saga_schema. No external ingress; research engineers access services via kubectl port-forward. Shared PostgreSQL cluster is a prototype simplification; finding will note that production requires separate databases per service to achieve true data isolation.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot with four Deployments and one ConfigMap per service. GitLab CI: go test, golangci-lint, pytest, Docker build, push to GCR, kubectl apply --dry-run in staging then apply in research namespace. Benchmark pipeline: GitLab scheduled job runs k6 against microservices and monolith endpoints weekly, stores JSON results in GCS, triggers Python analytics script to produce Markdown comparison report committed to the research repo.
target_output.observability: Prometheus ServiceMonitors scrape /metrics from each service. Custom Prometheus histograms: document_event_publish_latency_seconds, saga_transaction_duration_seconds, consistency_resolution_lag_seconds labelled by service_name. Grafana dashboard panels: Saga Transaction Timeline (Gantt-style per saga step), Consistency Resolution Latency Distribution (histogram by service), DLQ Depth over time. Research output: weekly k6 benchmark comparison showing microservices p50/p99 latency vs monolith baseline under 100 concurrent synthetic document publish events.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated USD 310/month: Confluent Cloud Kafka basic USD 60/month, Cloud SQL PostgreSQL db.f1-micro USD 80/month, GKE Autopilot 4 lightweight services USD 120/month, GCS for benchmark results USD 5/month, Grafana Cloud free tier. Under USD 500/month budget. Load test burst costs covered by budget buffer. Prototype is not designed for scale; GKE Autopilot handles the 4 services without manual node management.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (4 weeks): Scaffold three services, PostgreSQL schema setup, Kafka topics and Avro schemas, document_revised event flow end-to-end, consistency_audit_logs recording. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Saga orchestrator with rollback path, chaos failure injection via /simulate, k6 load test scripts and monolith baseline comparison runs, Grafana dashboards. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Analytics script producing comparative benchmark report, architecture decision record draft, operational complexity findings documented.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go services use golangci-lint with errcheck and staticcheck. Python cost-mgmt-svc uses ruff and mypy. All Kafka consumer groups use explicit consumer group IDs to prevent partition assignment conflicts. Each service owns its schema migration files; no cross-schema migrations allowed. Research findings documented in /docs/findings at end of each sprint. Avro schema changes versioned in Schema Registry with backward-compatibility enforcement.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Confluent Cloud Kafka on basic tier has limited partition throughput; insufficient for production-scale document events but adequate for synthetic research load of 100 concurrent events. Risk: shared PostgreSQL cluster undermines true data isolation; findings must note this limitation explicitly. Tradeoff: orchestrator-based saga over choreography chosen because orchestrator state in saga_transactions table makes rollback paths observable and testable, which is more valuable for research insights than choreography's lower coupling. Risk: GKE Autopilot node provisioning latency inflates p99 latency measurements during cold-start periods; pre-warm jobs added to benchmark pipeline.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Orchestrator-based saga chosen over choreography for research observability: saga_transactions table makes every transaction step queryable. Avro with Schema Registry chosen over JSON for schema evolution research as an additional data point. Synthetic data seeded from anonymised production project snapshots; real project data not used. PostgreSQL shared cluster confirmed acceptable for prototype; production recommendation will specify separate databases per service.

metadata.notes: Weak: ISS-001 and ISS-002 addressed but analytics and benchmark comparison methodology section is thin; phased_implementation at minimum detail for a research prototype of this scope.

profile.domain: Infrastructure Programme Earned Value Management Pipeline

project_goal.value: Replace manual spreadsheet-based earned-value tracking for a major infrastructure programme by extracting schedule progress and cost actuals from multiple delivery contractor systems, computing EVM metrics (SPI, CPI, EAC) in a centralised BigQuery warehouse, and feeding a programme-level executive dashboard
project_goal.rationale: Eliminate 3-day manual consolidation cycle; provide near-real-time programme performance visibility to programme director and client

target_users.value: Programme director, client commercial team, delivery contractor programme managers, and government audit reviewers
target_users.rationale: Multi-level consumers from operational trend monitoring to government capital works reporting

project_class.rationale: ELT pipeline with contractor data ingestion, dbt transformations, and BigQuery analytics layer; no interactive application frontend

capabilities.rationale: Weekly contractor submission ingestion, dbt EVM metric computation, analytics layer for Looker Studio

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-contractor WBS alignment, EVM metric computation across heterogeneous source data, government reporting standards

risk_level.rationale: Government capital works financial data subject to National Audit Office review; incorrect EVM figures could affect funding decisions

data_sensitivity.rationale: EAC forecasts and CPI data are commercially sensitive programme performance metrics

external_exposure.rationale: BigQuery access restricted to programme staff via Google Workspace SSO; no external API

access_model.value: Google Workspace SSO for BigQuery access; Looker Studio service account for dashboard queries; contractor submission via authenticated REST API endpoint
access_model.rationale: Programme staff use Google Workspace; contractors submit data via API with per-contractor API keys

feature_scope.value: Weekly contractor JSON submission ingestion, WBS structure validation against baseline, dbt EVM metric computation (EV, PV, AC, SPI, CPI, EAC), variance flagging at 10% threshold, BigQuery reporting layer, Looker Studio dashboard with weekly refresh, historical EVM trend retention
feature_scope.rationale: Full EVM computation and reporting capability replacing spreadsheet process

mvp_scope.value: Weekly ingestion from 3 primary contractors, dbt EVM metrics for WBS level 3, Looker Studio dashboard showing cumulative and periodic SPI/CPI per contract package
mvp_scope.rationale: Cover 80% of programme spend with 3 contractors for initial go-live

security_baseline.value: Google Workspace SSO for BigQuery, per-contractor API keys for submission endpoint, BigQuery column-level access controls for EAC forecasts restricted to programme director role
security_baseline.rationale: EAC and forecast data is commercially sensitive; contractors must not see each other's performance metrics

frontend_stack.value: none - pipeline only; Looker Studio connected to BigQuery for dashboards
frontend_stack.rationale: No custom frontend; Google Looker Studio provides adequate dashboard capability

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with dbt-core 1.8 and Dagster 1.7
backend_stack.rationale: Dagster for observable asset-based orchestration; dbt for transformation logic and data lineage

data_platform.value: BigQuery (GCP) for data warehouse; Cloud Storage for raw submission files
data_platform.rationale: BigQuery chosen for elastic serverless analytics, native Looker Studio integration, and Google Workspace SSO

hosting_target.value: Cloud Run for Dagster webserver and agents; BigQuery managed compute
hosting_target.rationale: Serverless Cloud Run for Dagster; BigQuery manages its own compute slots

privacy_retention_policy.value: EVM data retained 10 years for government audit purposes; raw contractor submissions in Cloud Storage retained 7 years; Dagster run logs retained 90 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Government capital works audit retention requirements

future_scope.value: Real-time progress feed from contractor scheduling tools via API streaming, automated EAC commentary using LLM, contractor self-service EVM dashboard
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 enhancements after manual submission process is fully replaced

constraints.value: Contractor submissions accepted Monday only; dashboard must refresh by Monday 08:00 local time; WBS changes require change control approval before pipeline will process updated baseline
constraints.rationale: Weekly programme reporting cycle aligned to Monday morning programme meeting

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM for Cloud Run Dagster service, dbt run result artefacts stored in Cloud Storage, PagerDuty for Dagster job failure and late refresh alerts
observability_baseline.rationale: Pipeline reliability is critical for programme director Monday meeting

execution_preference.value: Dagster software-defined assets promote through dev/staging/prod BigQuery datasets; dbt model PRs require data engineering lead approval
execution_preference.rationale: Government audit requirements demand traceable change management on EVM computation logic

compliance_context.value: Government capital works reporting standards; potential National Audit Office review of EVM data; contractor commercial confidentiality requirements preventing cross-contractor data exposure
compliance_context.rationale: High financial risk and government audit requirements

project.goal: Automated EVM pipeline replacing manual spreadsheet consolidation for infrastructure programme
project.target_users: Programme director, client commercial team, NAO auditors
project.timeline: Live by programme Month 4 to cover first formal reporting cycle

backend.integrations: Contractor REST API submission endpoint, Cloud Storage staging

security.auth: Google Workspace SSO for BigQuery; per-contractor API key for submission API
security.secrets: Secret Manager for contractor API keys and BigQuery service accounts
security.network: Dagster Cloud Run in private VPC; BigQuery via private service connect

data.source: Weekly JSON submissions from delivery contractors
data.staging: Cloud Storage for raw submission JSON files

reasoner_reviews.product: ISS-001 (WBS mismatch silent failure) was the critical issue in rounds 1 and 2. The pipeline was silently dropping WBS elements that did not match the approved baseline instead of failing loudly, causing understated EV figures in the Looker Studio dashboard. Round 2 added a validation Dagster asset that fails on any unrecognised WBS code. ISS-002 (contractor API key cross-exposure) was also resolved. This round focuses on ISS-003: the Dagster materialization for the evm_metrics dbt model is taking 18 minutes on a large contractor submission, threatening the Monday 08:00 dashboard deadline.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The 18-minute dbt run for evm_metrics is caused by the full-refresh strategy on a model that joins earned_value_snapshots with planned_value_baselines across all historical periods. Incremental dbt model materialisation keyed by programme_period and contractor_id will reduce the run to 2-3 minutes. The BigQuery partition on programme_period must be added to the underlying tables for the incremental merge to be performant.
reasoner_reviews.security: ISS-002 confirmed resolved in round 2 - BigQuery column-level policy tags on eac_forecast and cpi columns now restrict access to programme_director role. Contractor API keys verified to be isolated per contractor with no cross-read capability. No new security issues this round.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Incremental materialisation requires careful handling of late contractor submissions (submissions arriving after the Monday window). The dbt incremental model must accept reprocessing of a specific programme_period via the --full-refresh flag scoped to a date range, so late submissions can be replayed without wiping all historical EVM data.
